Then $(\sqrt{m} \pm \sqrt{2})^2 = 2 + m \pm 2\sqrt{2m}$ is an integer, so $m$ is twice a perfect square, say $m = 2n^2$. But then $\sqrt{m} \pm \sqrt{2} = (n \pm 1)\sqrt{2}$ is only rational if $n = \pm 1$, i.e., if $m = 2$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_009.jpg	Next, suppose that the subset contains two elements; then we can take it to be one of $\{\sqrt{m}\pm\sqrt{2}\}, \{\sqrt{2}\pm\sqrt{m}\}$ or $\{\pm(\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{2})\}$. In all cases, the sum and the product of the elements of the subset must be a rational number. In the first case, this means $2\sqrt{m} \in \mathbb{Q}$, so $m$ is a perfect square. In the second case, we have $2\sqrt{2} \in \mathbb{Q}$, contradiction. In the third case, we have $(\sqrt{m}+\sqrt{2})^2 \in \mathbb{Q}$, or $m+2+2\sqrt{2m} \in \mathbb{Q}$, which means that $m$ is twice a perfect square.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_010.jpg	We conclude that $P_{m}(x)$ factors into two nonconstant polynomials over the integers if and only if $m$ is either a square or twice a square.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_011.jpg	Note: a more sophisticated interpretation of this argument can be given using Galois theory. Namely, if $m$
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_012.jpg	is neither a square nor twice a square, then the number fields $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{m})$ and $\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2})$ are distinct quadratic fields, so their compositum is a number field of degree 4, whose Galois group acts transitively on $\{\pm\sqrt{m}\pm\sqrt{2}\}$. Thus $P_m$ is irreducible.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_013.jpg	A-4 Choose $r, s, t $ so that $E C = rBC, F A = sCA, G B = tCB$ and let $[XYZ]$ denote the area of triangle $XYZ$ Then $[ A B E ] = [ A F E ]$ since the triangles have the same altitude and base. Also $[ A B E ] = ( B E / B C ) [ A B C ] = 1 - r $, and $ [ E C F ] = ( E C / B C ) ( C F / C A ) [ A B C ] = r ( 1 - s )$ (e.g., by the law of sines). Adding this all up yields
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_014.jpg	or $r ( 1 + s ) = 1$ . Similarly $s ( 1 + t ) = t ( 1 + r ) = 1$
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_015.jpg	Let $f : [0, \infty) \to [0, \infty)$ be the function given by $f(x) =$ $1/(1 + x)$; then $f(f(f(r))) = r$. However, $f(x)$ is strictly decreasing in $x$, so $f(f(x))$ is increasing and $f(f(f(x)))$ is decreasing. Thus there is at most one $x$ such that $f(f(f(x))) = x$; in fact, since the equation $f(z) = z$ has a positive root $z = (-1 + \sqrt{5})/2$, we must have $r = s = t = z$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_016.jpg	We now compute $[ABF] = (AF/AC)[ABC] = z$, $[ABR] = (BR/BF)[ABF] = z/2$, analogously $[BCS] = [CAT] = z/2$, and $[RST] = |[ABC] - [ABR] - [BCS] - [CAT]| =|1-3z/2| = \frac{7-3\sqrt{5}}{4}.$
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_017.jpg	Note:the key relation $r ( 1 + s ) = 1$ can also be derived by computing using homogeneous coordinates or vectors.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_018.jpg	A-5 Suppose $a^{n+1} - (a + 1)^n = 2001$. Notice that $a^{n+1} + [(a + 1)^n - 1]$ is a multiple of $a$; thus $a$ divides $2002 = 2 \times 7 \times 11 \times 13$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_019.jpg	Since 2001 is divisible by 3, we must have $a \equiv 1$ (mod 3), otherwise one of $a^{n+1}$ and $(a+1)^n$ is a multiple of 3 and the other is not, so their difference cannot be divisible by 3. Now $a^{n+1} \equiv 1$ (mod 3), so we must have $(a+1)^n \equiv 1$ (mod 3), which forces $n$ to be even, and in particular at least 2.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_020.jpg	If a is even, then $a^{n+1} - (a+1)^n \equiv -(a+1)^n \pmod{4}$. Since n is even, $-(a+1)^n \equiv -1 \pmod{4}$. Since $2001 \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$, this is impossible. Thus a is odd, and so must divide $1001 = 7 \times 11 \times 13$. Moreover, $a^{n+1} - (a+1)^n \equiv a \pmod{4}$, so $a \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2001_Solutions_2001s_page_001_line_021.jpg	Of the divisors of $7 \times 11 \times 13$, those congruent to 1 mod 3 are precisely those not divisible by 11 (since 7 and 13 are both congruent to 1 mod 3). Thus $a$ divides $7 \times 13$. Now $a \equiv 1 \pmod{4}$ is only possible if $a$ divides 13.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_000.jpg	converges absolutely, and $\int_{0}^{B} \cos(x^2 - x) $ can be treated similarly.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_001.jpg	A-5 Let $a, b, c$ be the distances between the points. Then the area of the triangle with the three points as vertices is $abc/4r$. On the other hand, the area of a triangle whose vertices have integer coordinates is at least $1/2$ (for example, by Pick's Theorem). Thus $abc/4r \geq 1/2$, and so
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_002.jpg	A-6 Recall that if $f(x)$ is a polynomial with integer coefficients, then $m-n$ divides $f(m) - f(n)$ for any integers $m$ and $n$. In particular, if we put $b_n = a_{n+1} - a_n$, then $b_n$ divides $b_{n+1}$ for all $n$. On the other hand, we are given that $a_0 = a_m = 0$, which implies that $a_1 = a_{m+1}$ and so $b_0 = b_m$. If $b_0 = 0$, then $a_0 = a_1 = \cdots = a_m$ and we are done. Otherwise, $|b_0| = |b_1| = |b_2| = \cdots$, so $b_n = \pm b_0$ for all $n$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_003.jpg	Now $b_0 + \cdots + b_{m-1} = a_m - a_0 = 0$, so half of the integers $b_0, \ldots, b_{m-1}$ are positive and half are negative. In particular, there exists an integer $0 < k < m$ such that $b_{k-1} = -b_k$, which is to say, $a_{k-1} = a_{k+1}$. From this it follows that $a_n = a_{n+2}$ for all $n \geq k-1$; in particular, for $m=n$, we have
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_004.jpg	B-1 Consider the seven triples $(a,b,c)$ with $a,b,c \in \{0,1\}$ not all zero. Notice that if $r_j, s_j, t_j$ are not all even, then four of the sums $ar_j + bs_j + ct_j$ with $a,b,c \in \{0,1\}$ are even and four are odd. Of course the sum with $a=b=c=0$ is even, so at least four of the seven triples with $a,b,c$ not all zero yield an odd sum. In other words, at least $4N$ of the tuples $(a,b,c,j)$ yield odd sums. By the pigeonhole principle, there is a triple $(a,b,c)$ for which at least $4N/7$ of the sums are odd.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_005.jpg	B-2 Since $\gcd( m , n )$ is an integer linear combination of $m$ and $n$, it follows that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_006.jpg	is an integer linear combination of the integers
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_007.jpg	and hence is itself an integer.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_008.jpg	B-3 Put $f_k(t) = \frac{d^k f}{dt^k}$. Recall Rolle's theorem: if $f(t)$ is differentiable, then between any two zeroes of $f(t)$ there exists a zero of $f'(t)$. This also applies when the zeroes are not all distinct: if $f$ has a zero of multiplicity $m$ at $t = x$, then $f'$ has a zero of multiplicity at least $m-1$ there.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_009.jpg	Therefore, if $0 \leq a_0 \leq a_1 \leq \cdots \leq a_r < 1$ are the roots of $f_k$ in $[0,1)$, then $f_{k+1}$ has a root in each of the intervals $(a_0, a_1), (a_1, a_2), \ldots, (a_{r-1}, a_r)$, so long as we adopt the convention that the empty interval $(t,t)$ actually contains the point $t$ itself. There is also a root in the “wraparound” interval $(a_r, a_0)$. Thus $N_{k+1} \geq N_k$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_010.jpg	Next, note that if we set $z = e^{2\pi it}$; then
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_011.jpg	is equal to $z^{-N}$ times a polynomial of degree $2N$. Hence as a function of $z$, it has at most $2N$ roots; therefore $f_k(t)$ has at most $2N$ roots in $[0, 1]$. That is, $N_k \le 2N$ for all $N$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_012.jpg	To establish that $N_k \to 2N$, we make precise the observation that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_013.jpg	is dominated by the term with $j=N$. At the points $t = (2i+1)/(2N)$ for $i=0, 1, \ldots, N-1$, we have $N^{4k}a_N \sin(2\pi Nt) = \pm N^{4k}a_N$. If $k$ is chosen large enough so that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_014.jpg	then $f_k((2i + 1)/2N)$ has the same sign as $a_N \sin(2\pi Nat)$, which is to say, the sequence $f_k(1/2N), f_k(3/2N), \ldots$ alternates in sign. Thus between these points (again including the “wraparound” interval) we find $2N$ sign changes of $f_k$. Therefore $\lim_{k\to\infty}N_k = 2N$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_015.jpg	B-4 For $t$ real and not a multiple of $\pi$, write $g(t) = \frac{f(\cos t)}{\sin t}$. Then $g(t+\pi) = g(t)$; furthermore, the given equation implies that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_016.jpg	In particular, for any integer $n$ and $k$, we have
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_017.jpg	Since $f$ is continuous, $g$ is continuous where it is defined; but the set $\{1 + n\pi/2^k|n,k \in \mathbb{Z}\}$ is dense in the reals, and so $g$ must be constant on its domain. Since $g(-t) = -g(t)$ for all $t$, we must have $g(t) = 0$ when $t$ is not a multiple of $\pi$. Hence $f(x) = 0$ for $x \in (-1, 1)$. Finally, setting $x=0$ and $x=1$ in the given equation yields $f(-1) = f(1) = 0$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2000_Solutions_2000s_page_002_line_018.jpg	B-5 We claim that all integers $N$ of the form $2^k$, with $k$ a positive integer and $N > \max\{S_0\}$, satisfy the desired conditions.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_000.jpg	Solutions to the 64th William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_001.jpg	Saturday, December 6, 2003
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_002.jpg	Manjul Bhargava, Kiran Kedlaya, and Lenny Ng
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_003.jpg	A1 There are $n$ such sums. More precisely, there is exactly one such sum with $k$ terms for each of $k = 1,\ldots,n$ (and clearly no others). To see this, note that if $n = a_1 + a_2 + \cdots + a_k$ with $a_1 \leq a_2 \leq \cdots \leq a_k \leq a_1 + 1$, then
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_004.jpg	However, there is a unique integer $a_1$ satisfying these inequalities, namely $a_1 = \lfloor n/k \rfloor$. Moreover, once $a_1$ is fixed, there are $k$ different possibilities for the sum $a_1 +$ $a_2 + \cdots + a_k$: if $i$ is the last integer such that $a_i = a_1$, then the sum equals $ka_1 + (i - 1)$. The possible values of $i$ are $1, \ldots, k$, and exactly one of these sums comes out equal to $n$, proving our claim.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_005.jpg	Note: In summary, there is a unique partition of $n$ with $k$ terms that is "as equally spaced as possible" . One can also obtain essentially the same construction inductively: except for the all-ones sum, each partition of $n$ is obtained by "augmenting" a unique partition of $n - 1$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_006.jpg	A2 First solution: Assume without loss of generality that $a_i + b_i > 0$ for each $i$ (otherwise both sides of the desired inequality are zero). Then the AM-GM inequality gives
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_007.jpg	and likewise with the roles of $a$ and $b$ reversed. Adding these two inequalities and clearing denominators yields the desired result.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_008.jpg	Second solution: Write the desired inequality in the form
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_009.jpg	expand both sides, and compare the terms on both sides in which $k$ of the terms are among the $a_i$. On the left, one has the product of each $k$-element subset of $\{1,\dots,n\}$; on the right, one has
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_010.jpg	which is precisely $\binom{n}{k}$ times the geometric mean of the terms on the left. Thus AM-GM shows that the terms under consideration on the left exceed those on the right; adding these inequalities over all $k$ yields the desired result.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_011.jpg	Third solution: Since both sides are continuous in each $a_i$, it is sufficient to prove the claim with $a_1, \ldots, a_n$ all positive (the general case follows by taking limits as some of the $a_i$ tend to zero). Put $r_i = b_i/a_i$; then the given inequality is equivalent to
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_012.jpg	In terms of the function
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_013.jpg	and the quantities $s_{i} = \log r_{i}$, we can rewrite the desired inequality as
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_014.jpg	This will follow from Jensen's inequality if we can verify that $f$ is a convex function; it is enough to check that $f ^ { \prime \prime } ( x ) > 0$ for all $x$ . In fact,
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_015.jpg	is an increasing function of $x$, so $f''(x) > 0$ and Jensen's inequality thus yields the desired result. (As long as the $a_i$ are all positive, equality holds when $s_1 = \cdots = s_n$, i.e., when the vectors $(a_1,\ldots,a_n)$ and $(b_1,\ldots,b_n)$. Of course other equality cases crop up if some of the $a_i$ vanish, i.e., if $a_1 = b_1 = 0$.)
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_016.jpg	Fourth solution: We apply induction on $n$, the case $n = 1$ being evident. First we verify the auxiliary inequality
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_017.jpg	for $a,b,c,d \geq 0$. The left side can be written as
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2003_Solutions_2003s_page_001_line_018.jpg	Applying the weighted AM-GM inequality between matching terms in the two sums yields
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_000.jpg	Solutions to the 63rd William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_001.jpg	Solutions to the 63rd WilliamLowell Putnam Mathematical Competition Saturday,December7,2002
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_002.jpg	Kiran Kedlaya and Lenny $\mathrm { N g }$
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_003.jpg	A-1 By differentiating $P_n(x)/(x^k - 1)^{n+1}$, we find that $P_{n+1}(x) = (x^k - 1)P_n^{\prime}(x) - (n + 1)kx^{k-1}P_n(x)$; substituting $x = 1$ yields $P_{n+1}(1) = -(n + 1)kP_n(1)$. Since $P_0(1) = 1$, an easy induction gives $P_n(1) = (-k)^n n!$ for all $n \geq 0$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_004.jpg	Note:one can also argue by expanding in Taylor series around 1. Namely, we have
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_007.jpg	A-2 Draw a great circle through two of the points. There are two closed hemispheres with this great circle as boundary, and each of the other three points lies in one of them. By the pigeonhole principle, two of those three points lie in the same hemisphere, and that hemisphere thus contains four of the five given points.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_008.jpg	Note: by a similar argument, one can prove that among any $n { \mathrel { + { 3 } } }$ points on an $n$ -dimensional sphere, some $n + 2$ of them lie on a closed hemisphere. (One cannot get by with only $n + 2$ points: put them at the vertices of a regular simplex.) Namely, any $n$ of the points lie on a great sphere, which forms the boundary of two hemispheres; of the remaining three points, some two lie in the same hemisphere.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_009.jpg	A-3 Note that each of the sets $\{1\},\{2\},\dots,\{n\}$ has the desired property. Moreover, for each set $S$ with integer average $m$ that does not contain $m$, $S \cup \{m\}$ also has average $m$, while for each set $T$ of more than one element with integer average $m$ that contains $m$, $T \backslash \{m\}$ also has average $m$. Thus the subsets other than $\{1\},\{2\},\dots,\{n\}$ can be grouped in pairs, so $T_n - n$ is even.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_010.jpg	A-4 (partly due to David Savitt) Player 0 wins with optimal play. In fact, we prove that Player 1 cannot prevent Player 0 from creating a row of all zeroes, a column of all zeroes, or a $2 \times 2$ submatrix of all zeroes. Each of these forces the determinant of the matrix to be zero.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_011.jpg	For $i, j = 1,2,3$, let $A_{ij}$ denote the position in row $i$ and column $j$. Without loss of generality, we may assume that Player 1's first move is at $A_{11}$. Player 0 then plays at $A_{22}$:
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_012.jpg	After Player 1's second move, at least one of $A_{23}$ and $A_{32}$ remains vacant. Without loss of generality, assume $A_{23}$ remains vacant; Player 0 then plays there.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_013.jpg	After Player 1's third move, Player 0 wins by playing at $A_{21}$ if that position is unoccupied. So assume instead that Player 1 has played there. Thus of Player 1's three moves so far, two are at $A_{11}$ and $A_{21}$. Hence for $i$ equal to one of 1 or 3, and for $j$ equal to one of 2 or 3, the following are both true:
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_014.jpg	(a) The $2 \times 2$ submatrix formed by rows 2 and $i$ and by columns 2 and 3 contains two zeroes and two empty positions. (b) Column j contains one zero and two empty positions.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_015.jpg	Player 0 next plays at $A_{ij}$. To prevent a zero column, Player 1 must play in column $j$, upon which Player 0 completes the $2 \times 2$ submatrix in (a) for the win.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_016.jpg	Note: one can also solve this problem directly by making a tree of possible play sequences. This tree can be considerably collapsed using symmetries: the symmetry between rows and columns, the invariance of the outcome under reordering of rows or columns, and the fact that the scenario after a sequence of moves does not depend on the order of the moves (sometimes called “transposition invariance").
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_017.jpg	Note (due to Paul Cheng): one can reduce Determinant Tic-Tac-Toe to a variant of ordinary tic-tac-toe. Namely, considera tic-tac-toe grid labeled as follows:
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_001_line_018.jpg	Then each term in the expansion of the determinant occurs in a row or column of the grid. Suppose Player 1 first plays in the top left. Player 0 wins by playing first in the top row, and second in the left column.Then there are only one row and column left for Player 1 to threaten, and Player 1 cannot already threaten both on the third move,so Player 0 has time to block both.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_000.jpg	The 65th William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_001.jpg	Saturday, December 4, 2004
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_002.jpg	A1 Basketball star Shanille O'Keal's team statistician keeps track of the number, $S ( N )$ ，of successful free throws she has made in her first $N$ attempts of the season. Early in the season, $S ( N )$ was less than $80 \%$ of $N$, but by the end of the season, $S ( N )$ was more than $80 \%$ of $N$ .Was there necessarily a moment in between when $S ( N )$ was exactly $80 \%$ of $N$ ？
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_003.jpg	A2 For $i=1,2$ let $T_i$ be a triangle with side lengths $a_i,b_i,c_i$, and area $A_i$. Suppose that $a_1 \le a_2, b_1 \le b_2, c_1 \le c_2$, and that $T_2$ is an acute triangle. Does it follow that $A_1 \le A_2$?
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_004.jpg	A3 Define a sequence $\{u_n\}_{n=0}^{\infty}$ by $u_0 = u_1 = u_2 = 1$, and thereafter by the condition that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_005.jpg	for all $n \geq 0$. Show that $u_n$ is an integer for all $n$. (By convention, $0! = 1$.)
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_006.jpg	A4 Show that for any positive integer $n$ there is an integer $N$ such that the product $x_1x_2 \cdots x_n$ can be expressed identically in the form
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_007.jpg	where the $c_i$ are rational numbers and each $a_{ij}$ is one of the numbers $-1, 0, 1$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_008.jpg	A5 An $m \times n$ checkerboard is colored randomly: each square is independently assigned red or black with probability $1/2$. We say that two squares, $p$ and $q$, are in the same connected monochromatic region if there is a sequence of squares, all of the same color, starting at $p$ and ending at $q$, in which successive squares in the sequence share a common side. Show that the expected number of connected monochromatic regions is greater than $mn/8$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_009.jpg	A6 Suppose that $f(x,y)$ is a continuous real-valued function on the unit square $0 \leq x \leq 1, 0 \leq y \leq 1$. Show that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_010.jpg	B1 Let $P(x) = c_n x^n + c_{n-1} x^{n-1} + \dots + c_0$ be a polynomial with integer coefficients. Suppose that $r$ is a rational number such that $P(r) = 0$. Show that the $n$ numbers
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_011.jpg	are integers.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_012.jpg	B2 Let $m$ and $n$ be positive integers. Show that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_013.jpg	B3 Determine all real numbers $a > 0$ for which there exists a nonnegative continuous function $f ( x )$ defined on $[ 0 , a ]$ with the property that the region
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_014.jpg	has perimeter $k$ units and area $k$ square units for some real number $k$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_015.jpg	B4 Let $n$ be a positive integer, $n \geq 2$, and put $\theta = 2\pi/n$. Define points $P_k = (k, 0)$ in the $xy$-plane, for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, n$. Let $R_k$ be the map that rotates the plane counterclockwise by the angle $\theta$ about the point $P_k$. Let $R$ denote the map obtained by applying, in order, $R_1$, then $R_2, \ldots,$ then $R_n$. For an arbitrary point $(x, y)$, find, and simplify, the coordinates of $R(x, y)$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_016.jpg	B5 Evaluate
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Problems_2004_page_001_line_017.jpg	B6 Let $\mathscr{A}$ be a non-empty set of positive integers, and let $N(x)$ denote the number of elements of $\mathscr{A}$ not exceeding $x$. Let $\mathscr{B}$ denote the set of positive integers $b$ that can be written in the form $b = a - a'$ with $a \in \mathscr{A}$ and $a' \in \mathscr{A}$. Let $b_1 < b_2 < \cdots$ be the members of $\mathscr{B}$, listed in increasing order. Show that if the sequence $b_{i+1} - b_i$ is unbounded, then
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_000.jpg	A-5 It suffices to prove that for any relatively prime positive integers $r$, $s$, there exists an integer $n$ with $a_n = r$ and $a_{n+1}=s$. We prove this by induction on $r+s$, the case $r+s=2$ following from the fact that $a_0 = a_1 = 1$. Given $r$ and $s$ not both 1 with $\operatorname{gcd}(r,s)=1$, we must have $r \neq$ s. If $r > s$, then by the induction hypothesis we have $a_n = r-s$ and $a_{n+1}=s$ for some $n$; then $a_{2n+2} = r$ and $a_{2n+3}=s$. If $r < s$, then we have $a_n = r$ and $a_{n+1}=s-r$ for some $n$; then $a_{2n+1} = r$ and $a_{2n+2} = s$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_001.jpg	Note: a related problem is as follows. Starting with the sequence
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_002.jpg	repeat the following operation: insert between each pair $\frac{a}{b}$ and $\frac{c}{d}$ the pair $\frac{a+c}{b+d}$. Prove that each positive rational number eventually appears.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_003.jpg	Observe that by induction, if $\frac{a}{b}$ and $\frac{c}{d}$ are consecutive terms in the sequence, then $bc-ad=1$. The same holds for consecutive terms of the $n$-th *Farey sequence*, the sequence of rational numbers in $[0,1]$ with denominator (in lowest terms) at most $n$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_004.jpg	A–6 The sum converges for $b=2$ and diverges for $b \geq 3$. We first consider $b \geq 3$. Suppose the sum converges; then the fact that $f(n)=nf(d)$ whenever $b^{d-1} \leq n \leq b^d - 1$ yields
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_006.jpg	However, by comparing the integral of $1 / x $ with a Riemann sum, we see that
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_007.jpg	where log denotes the natural logarithm.Thus(1) yields
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_008.jpg	a contradiction since $\log b > 1$ for $b \geq 3$ .Therefore the sum diverges.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_009.jpg	For $b=2$, we have a slightly different identity because $f(2) \neq 2f(2)$. Instead, for any positive integer $i$, we have
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_011.jpg	Again comparing an integral to a Riemann sum, we see
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_012.jpg	that for $d \geq 3$,
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_013.jpg	Put $c = \frac{1}{8} + \log 2$ and $L = 1 + \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{6(1-c)}$. Then we can prove that $\sum_{n=1}^{2^i-1} \frac{1}{f(n)} < L$ for all $i \ge 2$ by induction on $i$. The case $i = 2$ is clear. For the induction, note that by (2),
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_014.jpg	as desired. We conclude that $ \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{1}{f(n)} $ converges to a limit less than or equal to L.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_015.jpg	Note: the above argument proves that the sum for $b=2$ is at most $L<2.417$. One can also obtain a lower bound by the same technique, namely $1+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6(1-c')}$ with $c' = \log 2$. This bound exceeds 2.043. (By contrast, summing the first 100000 terms of the series only yields a lower bound of 1.906.) Repeating the same arguments with $d \geq 4$ as the cutoff yields the upper bound 2.185 and the lower bound 2.079.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_016.jpg	B-1 The probability is 1/99.In fact,we show by induction on n that after n shots,the probability of having made any number of shots from 1 to n - 1 is equal to 1 / ( n - 1)．This is evident for n = 2 .Given the result for n we see that the probability of making i shots after n + 1 attempts is
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_017.jpg	as claimed.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2002_Solutions_2002s_page_002_line_018.jpg	B-2 (Note: the problem statement assumes that all polyhedra are connected and that no two edges share more than one face,so we will do likewise.In particular,these are true for all convex polyhedra.）We show that in fact the first player can win on the third move.Suppose the polyhedron has a face $A$ with at least four edges.If the first player plays there first,after the second player's first move there will be three consecutive faces $ { B , C , D }$ adjacent to $A$ which are all unoccupied. The first player wins by playing in $C$ ；after the second player's second
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_000.jpg	or in other words
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_001.jpg	To show this, we check that the function $f(x) = (1 - 1/x)^{x-1}$ is strictly decreasing for $x > 1$; while this can be achieved using the weighted arithmetic-geometric mean inequality, we give a simple calculus proof instead. The derivative of $\log f(x)$ is $\log(1 - 1/x) + 1/x$, so it is enough to check that this is negative for $x > 1$. An equivalent statement is that $\log(1 - x) + x < 0$ for $0 < x < 1$; this in turn holds because the function $g(x) = \log(1 - x) + x$ tends to 0 as $x \to 0^+$ and has derivative $1 - \frac{1}{1-x} < 0$ for $0 < x < 1$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_002.jpg	B-3 The answer is $\{a|a > 2\}$. If $a > 2$, then the function $f(x) = 2a/(a-2)$ has the desired property; both perimeter and area of $R$ in this case are $2a^2/(a-2)$. Now suppose that $a \leq 2$, and let $f(x)$ be a nonnegative continuous function on $[0, a]$. Let $P = (x_0, y_0)$ be a point on the graph of $f(x)$ with maximal $y$-coordinate; then the area of $R$ is at most $ay_0$ since it lies below the line $y = y_0$. On the other hand, the points $(0,0), (a,0)$, and $P$ divide the boundary of $R$ into three sections. The length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $P$ is at least the distance between $(0,0)$ and $P$, which is at least $y_0$; the length of the section between $P$ and $(a,0)$ is similarly at least $y_0$; and the length of the section between $(0,0)$ and $(a,0)$ is $a$. Since $a \leq 2$, we have $2y_0 + a > ay_0$ and hence the perimeter of $R$ is strictly greater than the area of $R$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_003.jpg	B-4 First solution: Identify the $xy$-plane with the complex plane $\mathbb{C}$, so that $P_k$ is the real number $k$. If $z$ is sent to $z'$ by a counterclockwise rotation by $\theta$ about $P_k$, then $z'-k = e^{i\theta}(z-k)$; hence the rotation $R_k$ sends $z$ to $\zeta z +$ $k(1-\zeta)$, where $\zeta = e^{2\pi i/n}$. It follows that $R_1$ followed by $R_2$ sends $z$ to $\zeta(\zeta z + (1-\zeta)) + 2(1-\zeta) = \zeta^2 z +$ $(1-\zeta)(\zeta+2)$, and so forth; an easy induction shows that $R$ sends $z$ to
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_004.jpg	Expanding the product $(1-\zeta)(\zeta^{n-1}+2\zeta^{n-2}+\cdots+(n-1)\zeta+n)$ yields $-\zeta^n - \zeta^{n-1} - \cdots - \zeta + n = n$. Thus $R$ sends $z$ to $z+n$; in cartesian coordinates, $R(x,y)=(x+n,y)$.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_005.jpg	Second solution: (by Andy Lutomirski, via Ravi Vakil) Imagine a regular $n$-gon of side length 1 placed with its top edge on the $x$ -axis and the left endpoint of that edge at the origin. Then the rotations correspond to rolling this $n$ -gon along the $x$ -axis; after the $n$ rotations, it clearly ends up in its original rotation and translated $n$ units to the right. Hence the whole plane must do so as well.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_006.jpg	Third solution: (attribution unknown) Viewing each $R_k$ as a function of a complex number $z$ as in the first solution, the function $R_n \circ R_{n-1} \circ \cdots \circ R_1(z)$ is linear in
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_007.jpg	$z$ with slope $\zeta^n=1$. It thus equals $z+T$ for some $T \in \mathbb{C}$. Since $f_1(1)=1$, we can write $1+T = R_n \circ \cdots \circ R_2(1)$. However, we also have
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_008.jpg	by the symmetry in how the $R_i$ are defined. Hence
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_009.jpg	that is, $R_n(T) = T$. Hence $T=n$, as desired.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_010.jpg	B-5 First solution: By taking logarithms, we see that the desired limit is $\exp(L)$, where $L = \lim_{x\to1^{-}}\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}x^n(\ln(1+x^{n+1}) - \ln(1+x^n)).$ Now
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_011.jpg	since $\lim_{N \to \infty}(x^N \ln(1 + x^{N+1})) = 0$ for $0 < x < 1$, we conclude that $L = -\ln 2 + \lim_{x \to 1^-} f(x)$, where
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_012.jpg	This final double sum converges absolutely when $0 < x < 1$, since
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_013.jpg	which converges. (Note that $-\ln(1-x)$ and $-\ln(1-x^n)$ are positive.) Hence we may interchange the summations in $f(x)$ to obtain
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2004_Solutions_2004s_page_004_line_014.jpg	This last sum converges absolutely uniformly in $x$, so it is legitimate to take limits term by term. Since
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_000.jpg	The 68th William Lowell Putnam Mathematical Competition
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_001.jpg	Saturday, December 1, 2007
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_002.jpg	A-1 Find all values of $\alpha$ for which the curves $y = \alpha x^2 + \alpha x + \frac{1}{24}$ and $x = \alpha y^2 + \alpha y + \frac{1}{24}$ are tangent to each other.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_003.jpg	A-2 Find the least possible area of a convex set in the plane that intersects both branches of the hyperbola $x y = 1$ and both branches of the hyperbola $x y = - 1 $.(A set $S$ in the plane is called convex if for any two points in $S$ the line segment connecting them is contained in $S$.)

test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_007.jpg	A-6 A triangulation $\mathscr{T}$ of a polygon $P$ is a finite collection of triangles whose union is $P$, and such that the intersection of any two triangles is either empty, or a shared vertex, or a shared side. Moreover, each side is a side of exactly one triangle in $\mathscr{T}$. Say that $\mathscr{T}$ is admissible if every internal vertex is shared by 6 or more triangles. For example, [figure omitted.] Prove that there is an integer $M_n$, depending only on $n$, such that any admissible triangulation of a polygon $P$ with $n$ sides has at most $M_n$ triangles.
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_008.jpg	B-1 Let $f$ be a polynomial with positive integer coefficients. Prove that if $n$ is a positive integer, then $f( n )$ divides
test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_009.jpg	$f(f(n)+1)$ if and only if $n=1$. [Editor's note: one must assume $f$ is nonconstant.]

test/images/exam_paper_en-file-putnam-archive_2007_Problems_2007_page_001_line_011.jpg	B-3 Let $x_0 = 1$ and for $n \geq 0$, let $x_{n+1} = 3x_n + \lfloor x_n\sqrt{5} \rfloor$.
