test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_019_line_075.jpg	Chicago Tribune | Section 2 | Wednesday, January 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_000.jpg	Bulls guard Coby White dunks the ball over Spurs center Victor Wembanyamain during the final minute of the fourth period Monday night at the United Center. ARMANDO L. SANCHEZ/CHICAGO TRIBUNE PHOTOS
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_001.jpg	BULLS TAKEAWAYS
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_002.jpg	Suitable for framing
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_003.jpg	With dunk, White put Wembanyama on a poster to lead comeback win
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_004.jpg	By Julia Poe
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_005.jpg	Chicago Tribune
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_006.jpg	Coby White is not a dunker.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_007.jpg	Before Monday’s 114-110 win over the San Antonio Spurs, the Bulls guard had attempted only two dunks this season. He dunked only 14 times last season. It’s just not a part of his bag that White likes to dig into often.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_008.jpg	Maybe, he joked after the win, he would try it more if he had the athleticism of Zach LaVine or the wingspan of Patrick Williams.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_009.jpg	But in the final 30 seconds, with a one-point lead over the Spurs, White didn't have another choice. So he put his head down and charged to the rim. And if the tallest man in the NBA was in his way? Well, all the better.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_010.jpg	Victor Wembanyama already had blocked three of White's shots before the final minute, but that didn't slow White's approach. It couldn't. After falling behind by 19 points in the first half, the Bulls couldn't collapse at the rim just because the 7-foot-3 Wembanyama was waiting.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_011.jpg	In the locker room at halftime, the entire Bulls roster shared the same sentiment: We have to do something.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_012.jpg	For White, that something meant attacking the rim. Even if he got blocked. And in the final minute, that fearlessness paid off twofold.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_013.jpg	White slung an up-and-under shot that arched all the way over Wembanyama's 8-foot wingspan and into the basket to give the Bulls the lead with 47 seconds remaining. And 32 seconds later, White took an even more aggressive approach, getting Jeremy Sochan to bite on a pump fake and launching himself skyward.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_014.jpg	Wembanyama rotated over a second late. That didn't matter either. For White, the poster-perfect shot – his arm cocked back, Wembanyama's body crashing into his torso, the ball already searing downward toward the
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_015.jpg	Bulls guard Zach LaVine slides on the court during the fourth quarter against the Spurs on Monday night.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_016.jpg	rim – was the only option to clinch the game.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_017.jpg	“I just told myself the next time I was going to go, I was going to try to dunk it,” White said. “I knew when I was driving, if I was going to lay it in, finger-roll it, anything, he was going to block it. So I just was like – I’ve got to dunk it. I just wanted to make sure that I got the basket.”
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_018.jpg	Here are two other takeaways from the win.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_019.jpg	1. Wemby vs. Vooch: The toughest matchup of the game was between Wembanyama and Bulls center Nikola Vučević. From the start, it was a challenge for Vučević — Wembanyama blocked him twice in the first four minutes, enforcing his will under and around the rim.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_020.jpg	Wembanyama didn't affect Vučević too deeply on the offensive side. The veteran extended his consistent scoring, tallying 24 points despite an off night (2-for-9) behind the 3-point arc. But Wembanyama proved equally vexing on the other end, making four 3-pointers to score 23 points.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_021.jpg	In the final two minutes of the second quarter, Vučević chased Wembanyama out to the 3-point line, doggedly latched onto the Spurs center’s hip until the shot clock expired. But in the final half-second, Wembanyama pulled up and fired off a 3-pointer that rattled through the rim –his
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_022.jpg	second of the night.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_023.jpg	Vučević could only drop his arms and tip his head back in stupefied frustration, jogging back before answering with his own 3-pointer several possessions later.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_024.jpg	Wembanyama closed the game with an identical play in the final 10 seconds. This time, the shot rattled out. And for Vučević, that was victory enough – forcing Wembanyama into his worst shot for the game-winner.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_025.jpg	2. Zach LaVine brought the high-lights: It's hard to fly under the radar while consistently putting up 30 or more points, but LaVine is achieving just that. He finished just shy of a triple-double by tallying 35 points, 11 rebounds and eight assists – and threw down a few spectacle dunks of his own – to once again power the Bulls offense.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_026.jpg	His scoring was vital on a cold night from 3-point range. The Bulls went 13-for-39 behind the arc — and LaVine and White combined for six of those makes. The Spurs were able to utilize Wembanyama's overwhelming length around the basket to crash closeouts heavily, funneling the Bulls offense toward their center at the rim.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_027.jpg	LaVine was the offense's key spark inside the arc, pushing the ball in transition to utilize his speed advantage.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_028.jpg	CHICAGO BASKETBALL REPORT
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_029.jpg	No All-Stars for Bulls yet again?
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_030.jpg	Sky hire former Dream head coach as assistant under Marsh
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_031.jpg	By Julia Poe
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_032.jpg	Chicago Tribune
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_033.jpg	The early returns of the NBA All-Star fan voting do not look good for the Chicago Bulls.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_034.jpg	No Bulls player was listed in the first returns of fan voting, which will run until Jan. 20. While the Boston Celtics and the Philadelphia 76ers are dominating the fan vote, actual selections could vary greatly — fan voting is only weighted as 50% of total voting. Media and player selections will also contribute to the final 2025 All-Star team rosters.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_035.jpg	The Bulls have not sent a player to the All-Star game since 2023, when DeMar DeRozan was the team's sole representative in the game. Last season broke a three- year streak of consecutive All-Star appearances from either DeRozan or Zach LaVine. The team's lack of presence at the All-Star game has often been a visible reflection of a lack of relevance in the league at large.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_036.jpg	This season, veterans LaVine and Nikola Vučević might be flying under the radar as potential selections for the All-Star game. Vučević is boosting his scoring with the best 3-point shooting season of his career. And LaVine is averaging the 12th-most points of any player in the Eastern Conference (ninth of backcourt players in the East) while showcasing his efficiency in a revised version of his role as the offensive leader for the Bulls.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_037.jpg	All-Star starters will be announced on Jan. 23 and the reserve team will be announced on Jan. 30. This year's All-Star game, played in San Francisco, will feature a new format involving a tournament of four eight- player teams. Three of those teams will consist of All-Stars while the fourth will be the winning team from the Rising Stars tournament for rookie, second-year and G League players.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_038.jpg	Chicago Sky hire former Atlanta head coach Tanisha Wright: New Sky coach Tyler Marsh made his second addition to his bench this week, hiring former Atlanta Dream head coach Tanisha Wright as an assistant coach.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_039.jpg	Wright is a 15-year veteran of the WNBA as a coach and a player. She helmed the Dream from 2021 before she was released by the team this fall following a 15-25 season. Wright was named the AP Coach of the Year in 2022.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_040.jpg	Prior to Atlanta, Wright was an assistant coach with the Las Vegas Aces, although she departed for the Dream head coaching position before current Aces coach Becky Hammon — who was a crucial mentor to Marsh — took over the program. Wright will bring a variety of experience to Marsh's bench including international scouting.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_041.jpg	Ayo Dosunmu faces uncertain return timeline: The Bulls might have to fare without Ayo Dosunmu for an even longer stretch of the season than expected. The guard initially suffered a calf strain in a loss to the Milwaukee Bucks on Dec. 23 and has been unable to progress to full-speed sprints after an extended 10-day period of rest.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_042.jpg	Dosunmu ramped up to sprint-
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_043.jpg	Former Dream head coach Tanisha Wright reacts during the first half of a first-round WNBA playoff game against the Liberty on Sept. 24 in New York. ADAM HUNGER/AP
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_044.jpg	ing on a treadmill over the weekend but reported discomfort when attempting to reach full speed, coach Billy Donovan said. He will not attempt another ramp-up until next week. This means the Bulls could play at least five more games without Dosunmu, who has been a crucial defensive anchor and secondary point guard option for the Bulls this season.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_045.jpg	In addition to his contributions on the court, Dosunmu is a key trade asset for the Bulls as they approach a crucial selling season ahead of the deadline in February. While he might not be the center of a trade offer, the Bulls could utilize Dosunmu's considerable upside and favorable contract to sweeten a deal.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_046.jpg	Before the injury, Dosunmu was averaging 12.6 points, 4.8 assists and 3.9 rebounds per game this season.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_047.jpg	Illinois climbs to top 15 ranking: In a matter of weeks, the Fighting Illini clawed their way from being unranked to sitting just outside the top 10 in NCAA men's basketball with a No. 13 ranking in the AP Top 25. This climb was bolstered by the team's offensive thrashing of No. 15 Oregon, who they beat 109-77 last Thursday.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_048.jpg	Illinois has won its last four games and posted a 3-1 start to the Big Ten season. And two of their three losses came at the hands of a higher-ranked opponent – a two-point loss to No. 1 Tennessee and an early season loss to No. 5 Alabama. Their lone Big Ten loss – an upset by Northwestern – will continue to sting. But Illinois is carving out a space for itself as a top contender in the conference, jostling with Indiana, Michigan and Michigan State for the top spot.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_049.jpg	Number of the week: 4
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_050.jpg	Zach LaVine is the fourth most efficient 3-point shooter in the NBA. In big and inconsequential moments alike, LaVine just can't seem to miss. He's shooting $44.7\%$ from behind the arc on 7.4 attempts per game.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_051.jpg	This is a somewhat muted volume despite the high rate at which the Bulls are shooting the ball from deep this season – but it’s also an anomaly for any player to maintain this high of an efficiency while taking this many shots. Only one other player (Kyrie Irving) shooting above $44\%$ has taken 200 or more attempts from 3-point range this season.
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_052.jpg	Oregon guard Jadrian Tracey, left, and Illinois guard Tre White go for a rebound during the first half of a game in Eugene, Ore. on Thursday. THOMAS BOYD/AP
test/images/newspaper_Chicago Tribune_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_032_line_054.jpg	2 Chicago Tribune | Section 3 | Wednesday, January 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_000.jpg	YOUR MONEY
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_001.jpg	A NEW year is the prime time to get into healthier habits - including saving for the future.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_002.jpg	According to financial information website Moneyfacts, the choice of cash savings deals on the market reached
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_003.jpg	the highest point in December since its records started in 2007. More than 2,000 products were available, so there's a lot to unpick.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_004.jpg	Whether you are a seasoned saver or are
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_005.jpg	taking your first steps into the world of investment, Clare Francis,director of savings and investments at Barclays Smart Investor,above,has tips to help grow a handy pot of cash:
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_006.jpg	1 Create and stick to a budget
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_007.jpg	" The importance of a well-structured budget really cannot be overstated ," says Clare.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_008.jpg	She suggests reviewing your monthly income and outgoings to identify areas where you can cut back on non-essential spending .
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_009.jpg	"The money you save can be used to pay off debts , moved into a savings account , or even invested for your future ," Clare adds.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_010.jpg	"By taking a proactive approach to your budgeting , you not only ensure that every pound is working as hard as possible for you , but that you also cultivate a mindset for long-term financial success."
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_011.jpg	There are plenty of free budgeting tools available online that could help , such as the budget planner from the government-backed MoneyHelper service at moneyhelper.org.uk.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_012.jpg	It takes about 10 minutes to complete and can give you a fresh outlook and new understanding of your cash.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_013.jpg	2 Think about your financial goals
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_014.jpg	"You don't need to have all your life goals nailed down perfectly , but having a rough idea of what you' re aiming for and how much money you ' ll need is helpful ," says Clare.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_015.jpg	" It provides a useful starting point to weigh up the best options available to you.”
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_016.jpg	Many people keep all their cash in savings accounts.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_017.jpg	But Clare suggests that if you've got money you can afford to put away for the longer term,perhaps for five years or more, then investing could be worth considering.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_018.jpg	Money held in investments may potentially see stronger growth over the longer-term than savings held in cash .
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_019.jpg	But people putting money into investments will need to be comfortable with the risks and be aware that they could potentially get less money back than they put in .
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_020.jpg	Analysis by Barclays indicates that many savers could be missing out on potentially higher returns .
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_021.jpg	It estimates that 13million UK adults hold £430billion of " possible investments " in cash savings , so they could be missing out on a lot of extra
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_022.jpg	money if only they felt more confident and empowered to invest.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_023.jpg	"Pound cost averaging can help smooth out overall returns and reduce the risk
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_024.jpg	3 Consider how hands-on you want to be
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_025.jpg	Choosing where and how to invest money can feel daunting,which may for some people be a barrier to moving some of their money from cash savings into investments.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_026.jpg	“Many people face a choice paralysis,where they are afraid of making the wrong decision,and it prevents them from taking the next step,” Clare explains.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_027.jpg	She also highlights the fact many providers offer ready-made investments
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_028.jpg	funds, which may be a good place to start for some people.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_029.jpg	"These types of funds provide global diversification and invest in a mixture of cash , bonds and shares but the levels of risk vary ," explains Clare.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_030.jpg	“So,all you need to do is to pick a fund with a level of risk that you're comfortable with"
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_031.jpg	4 Look for financial guidance from reputable sources
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_032.jpg	Clare says :" Our recent research found that a quarter of Brits are now turning to social media , community messaging apps and online forums for information about investing ."
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_033.jpg	Clare suggests that when looking at content from a financial influencer, or "finfluencer,” check out their bio to see
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_034.jpg	if they have a background in finance or experience in the investment field.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_035.jpg	"If you struggle to reassure yourself that they know what they are talking about, be cautious about acting on their guidance," she says.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_036.jpg	She also warns that, with many investment scams starting on social media, it is important to "sense check" any information that is being shared online.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_037.jpg	Consider whether claims about high investment returns, for example,seem too good to be true.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_038.jpg	It is crucial to keep in mind as well that fraudsters may use deepfake videos or other bogus information to give the false impression that an investment scheme has been endorsed by celebrities.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_039.jpg	If you're unsure about a particular firm, remember that regulator the
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_040.jpg	Financial Conduct Authorityhas a warning list of firms on its website as well as a financial services register of firms and people involved in regulated activities (fca.org.uk).
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_041.jpg	5 Regular saving and investing
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_042.jpg	“Just as saving monthly is a good habit to get into,so is investing regularly," says Clare.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_043.jpg	“Many people think you need a large amount before you can start, but that's not the case."
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_044.jpg	Dripping regular amounts of money into investments over time
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_046.jpg	DM1ST
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_047.jpg	DAILY MIRROR WEDNESDAY 08.01.2025
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_034_line_048.jpg	DM1ST

test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_000.jpg	My life as a witch
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_001.jpg	The modern Molly Weasley who is using chat-brooms to work magic
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_002.jpg	FAMILY Harry, Holly, Emma and Steve
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_003.jpg	EXCLUSIVE
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_004.jpg	BY MATTHEW BARBOUR
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_005.jpg	At the age of four, while her little pals were running about, laughing and giggling, Emma Griffin remembers “playing with dead people”.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_006.jpg	Another childhood memory is of Sunday afternoons, when other mums and dads were out for a stroll after lunch and her parents, Pat and Pip, would be holding a seance to commune with the other side.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_007.jpg	Emma, 48, now a full-time witch, says: “It seemed normal for me to see – and play with – dead people from the age of just four.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_008.jpg	“My first time was at Hampton Court, but ever since then, it’s happened regularly. My younger brother Ben and I knew our parents were different, but then, aren’t everyone's?"
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_009.jpg	She says she inherited her mum’s magical powers and “came out of the closet – the broom closet” after her parents died from cancer in 2019 and 2020.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_010.jpg	She is now a full-time witch, charging £100 for a “one- to-one guidance medicine reading”. She says: “I’m the real-life Molly Weasley. Everyone calls me that.”
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_011.jpg	But, unlike the Harry Potter witch, she is a thoroughly modern Molly, with 96,000 followers on Instagram and 40,000 on TikTok.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_012.jpg	And she recently cast her spell on 262 would-be magic makers from across the world, initiating them as witches in one big, free Zoom session.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_013.jpg	Emma did not discover her mum was a witch until she was 17, when she rushed home in tears after visiting a psychic fair with Steve, now her husband.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_014.jpg	A tarot reader had told her she and Steve, then also 17 and an art student like her, would never have children.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_015.jpg	Determined to help, her mum confessed she was actually a sorceress and would teach Emma the skills to prove the reader wrong.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_016.jpg	In 1999, Emma and Steve had
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_017.jpg	daughter Holly, then son Harry three years later.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_018.jpg	It seemed normal to me to see dead people
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_019.jpg	EMMA GRIFFIN WITCH ON PARANORMAL POWER
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_020.jpg	Soon after, moving from London to Cornwall to be closer to nature, Emma began giving
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_021.jpg	clients spiritual guidance, while working in jobs ranging from fashion design to illustration.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_022.jpg	She became a full-time witch
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_023.jpg	around five years ago and initiated nursery manager Holly as a sea witch in 2021, making a third generation of witches in her family.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_024.jpg	Emma says: “When I tell people I’m a witch, the majority are fascinated and love hearing about what I do, from helping people find happiness to healing them, even helping them conceive, like I myself did 25 years ago.”
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_025.jpg	Talking of the Zoom witch initiation, which included women from as far away as Finland and Australia, she says: “I guided them through the ceremony, helping them become witches, telling them to spread the word far and wide.”
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_026.jpg	FUN Movie Harry Potter & Molly and Arthur Weasley
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_027.jpg	She has faced hostility from people saying she will “go to hell for working for the devil”, but Emma says being a witch is “positive and natural”. And she
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_028.jpg	has always been open with her kids about being a witch. She says: “They were attracted to the spirituality of it.”
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_029.jpg	Her husband is also on “team witch”, says Emma, whose website has attracted clients wanting help with everything from mental health to fertility issues.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_030.jpg	She says: “I intend to keep spreading the word. Those muggles (people without magical talents) are really missing out.”
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_031.jpg	■ Follow Emma at instagram.com/ emmagriffinwitch. Her book, The Witch's Way Home, is out now
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_032.jpg	WEDNESDAY 08.01.2025 DAILY MIRROR
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_033.jpg	mirror.co.uk
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_034.jpg	features@mirror.co.uk X@DailyMirror
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_035.jpg	WEDNESDAY 08.01.2025 DAILY MIRROR 29
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mirror 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_029_line_036.jpg	DM1ST
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_000.jpg	NOW HE SAYS 'SORRY'
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_001.jpg	Suspect stunned to see video of himself torching woman: documents
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_002.jpg	BY JOHN ANNESE
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_003.jpg	NEW YORK DAILY NEWS
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_004.jpg	Sebastian Zapeta, the migrant accused of fatally setting homeless woman Debrina Kawam on fire on a Coney Island subway train, pleaded not guilty Tuesday — even as court papers revealed he said, “Oh, damn, that’s me,” when shown the horrific video of the attack that shocked the city.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_005.jpg	Zapeta, a 33-year-old Guatemalan migrant living in a Brooklyn men's shelter, was arraigned before Brooklyn Supreme Court Justice Danny Chun on first-degree murder and arson charges. Chun ordered he remain held without bail until his next court appearance March 12.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_006.jpg	Until recently, 57-year-old Kawam was listed as a “Jane Doe” on the indictment against Zapeta. Authorities identified her last week and Assistant District Attorney Matthew Perry on Tuesday had the document amended to include her name.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_007.jpg	After Zapeta’s arrest, he told a trio of NYPD detectives – one of whom translated from Spanish – that he had no memory of what he’d done and that he often drank so much he would “erase the memory” of what happened and wake up at home.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_008.jpg	The detectives confronted him with the video, with one telling him, “The best thing for us here, we want to know why
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_009.jpg	you went to that woman in front of you and set fire to her. Why did you do it?”
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_010.jpg	"To tell you the truth, to be honest, I don't remember," Zapeta replied.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_011.jpg	When the detectives showed Zapeta video of himself as he lit Kawam ablaze, he responded with surprise, according to court documents.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_012.jpg	"Who is that person?" the Spanish-speaking detective asks, and Zapeta responds, "Oh, damn, that's me."
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_013.jpg	"That's you, right?," the cop continues, and Zapeta responds, "Yes."
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_014.jpg	"Do you see what's happening now? Do you see what you're doing? You are setting that woman on fire. Do you remember that or not?" the detective asks.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_015.jpg	"No, I really don't remember," Zapeta responds.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_016.jpg	“But that's you, right?” the detective asks.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_017.jpg	“Yes,” Zapeta replies, according to the court documents.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_018.jpg	When asked again why he did it, Zapeta responds, “I am very sorry. I didn’t mean to. But I really don’t know. I don’t know what happened, but I’m very sorry for that woman.”
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_019.jpg	Zapeta approached Kawam as she slept in an F train stopped at the end of the line at Coney Island-Stillwell Ave. station at about 7:30 a.m. Dec. 22,
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_020.jpg	according to cops. They appeared to be strangers, police said.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_021.jpg	He said nothing to her as he set her clothing ablaze with a lighter, then fanned the fire with a shirt as the flames engulfed her, according to prosecutors.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_022.jpg	Chilling video shows Zapeta sitting on a bench on the train platform as Kawam burned to death as she stood helplessly near the subway car's open door.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_023.jpg	Surveillance video later recovered by cops shows Zapeta inside the subway car and a police officer's body cam got a clear shot of his face, cops said. The NYPD quickly released images of the suspect to the media and a trio of high schoolers spotted him on another train later in the day and called 911, the commissioner said.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_024.jpg	Zapeta was arrested by Customs and Border Patrol in Arizona on June 2, 2018, sources said, then removed by Immigration and Customs Enforcement officials and returned to Guatemala. But he made his way back to the U.S. sometime after.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_025.jpg	Zapeta told the detectives he came back to the U.S. illegally about five years ago, arriving in Queens with his wife's nephew. Relatives took him in but that living situation fell apart during the COVID-19 pandemic.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_026.jpg	“I stayed with them, but in the
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_027.jpg	pandemic, they threw me out because of my brother-in-law. Okay, after the pandemic I went out to work and my brother- in-law called them and told him that... my brother-in-law was working with us, and he said that all the guys that worked there were infected with coronavirus.”
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_028.jpg	Up until his arrest, he was doing construction work in Queens, meeting his employer, who he refers to as “the Mexican,” outside the boss’ house in Jamaica before a job.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_029.jpg	“He has a lot of work. He takes us to Brooklyn, or in Queens ... or Wall Street,” he told the detectives.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_030.jpg	He finished work the day before the attack about 5:30 p.m., then after his employer picked him up and drove him back to Queens, he went to a bar in Jamaica to buy beer and get drunk, he told detectives.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_031.jpg	Zapeta had \$178.80 on him when police arrested him and told the cops processing him, “That’s all I have.”
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_032.jpg	He had to hand over his sweater and a bracelet and when the officers noted he was wearing two pairs of pants he explained, “I have two because of the cold weather.”
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_033.jpg	His lawyer, Andrew Friedman, once again reserved the right to apply for bail at a later date.
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_007_line_034.jpg	With Emma Seiwell

But in 2021, they reunited in the public eye once again. By April 2022 they were engaged again, and then in July 2022 they got married at a secret wedding ceremony in Las Vegas.

test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_041.jpg	Wednesday, January 8,2025 21
test/images/newspaper_NY Daily News_August 1@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_042.jpg	DAILY NEWS NYDailyNews.com
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_000.jpg	U.S. says genocide under way in Sudan
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_001.jpg	Secretary of State announces sanctions against RSF commander, and seven companies controlled by the militia
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_002.jpg	GEOFFREY YORK
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_003.jpg	AFRICA BUREAU CHIEF JOHANNESBURG
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_004.jpg	Sudan's powerful Rapid Support Forces militia has committed genocide by systematically perpetrating murder and sexual violence against adults and children from minority ethnic groups, the United States says.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_005.jpg	The long-awaited genocide declaration had been contemplated by the Joe Biden administration for many months, but it was finally announced on Tuesday as one of the administration's last acts, less than two weeks before Mr. Biden hands over the presidency to Donald Trump.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_006.jpg	“The RSF and allied militias have systematically murdered men and boys - even infants - on an ethnic basis, and deliberately targeted women and girls from certain ethnic groups for rape and other forms of brutal sexual violence,” U.S. Secretary of State Antony Blinken said in a statement.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_007.jpg	“Those same militias have targeted fleeing civilians, murdering innocent people escaping conflict, and prevented remaining civilians from accessing lifesaving supplies,” he said. "Based on this information, I have now concluded that members of the RSF and allied militias have committed genocide in Sudan."
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_008.jpg	The Biden administration also announced sanctions against the RSF's long-time commander, General Mohamed Hamdan Dagalo, known as Hemedti, along with seven companies controlled by the RSF in the United Arab Emirates, a wealthy Gulf state that is reportedly supplying weapons to the paramilitary
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_009.jpg	People displaced by conflict in Sudan ride aboard a bus Tuesday to return home to the southern city of Singah in Sennar province, which was retaken by the Sudanese army forces from the Rapid Support Forces in November, 2024. AFP VIA GETTY IMAGES
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_010.jpg	group.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_011.jpg	Several of the UAE-based companies were providing weapons or money to the RSF while another was buying gold from the RSF's mining operations in Sudan, the U.S. Treasury Department said in its sanctions decision.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_012.jpg	The war in Sudan, which erupted 21 months ago in a power struggle between the RSF and the Sudanese military, has killed tens of thousands and triggered the world's biggest humanitarian catastrophe. More than 12 million people have been forced from their homes and 30 million are in dire need of emergency aid.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_013.jpg	A year ago, Mr. Blinken said the RSF and the Sudanese military had both committed war crimes. But he had delayed his decision on a genocide declaration, reportedly because it could have triggered an RSF pullout from ceasefire negotiations,which ultimately failed. He may have calculated that Mr. Trump is unlikely to focus on Sudan in the early months of his presidency, so the genocide declaration and sanctions against Gen. Dagalo will be the last
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_014.jpg	chance for Washington to apply pressure on the RSF for the foreseeable future.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_015.jpg	Last year, U.S. envoy Tom Perriello said the Sudan war had killed as many as 150,000 people. His estimate was reinforced by a British study, which found that 61,000 people had died in Khartoum alone in the first 14 months of the war.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_016.jpg	Sudan is also the site of the world's only officially declared famine. Late last month, an ex- pert committee said the famine had expanded to five areas of the country, with more than 630,000 people at risk of starvation and a further five areas likely to fall into famine by May.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_017.jpg	Tuesday's announcement is the second time in 21 years that the United States has declared the existence of a genocide in Sudan. The previous declaration,in 2004, focused on the RSF's predecessor, known as the Janjaweed, which committed widespread atrocities in the Darfur region of western Sudan to suppress a rebellion. Despite the evidence of genocide, the RSF was later
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_018.jpg	brought into Sudan's government and built a vast arsenal of weapons and fighters.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_019.jpg	Human rights groups welcomed the U.S.announcement. “RSF leader General Hemedti is ultimately responsible for some of the most heinous atrocities being committed anywhere in the world today,”said John Prendergast, co-founder of The Sentry,a U.S.-based watchdog group that focuses on Sudan and South Sudan.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_020.jpg	“Without serious measures of legal and financial accountability, the RSF will continue its rampage,” he said.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_021.jpg	The Montreal-based Raoul Wallenberg Centre for Human Rights said the sanctions were “a significant step toward justice.” It called on Canada and other countries to impose similar sanctions on Gen. Dagalo.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_022.jpg	Some critics noted that the U.S. announcement came more than eight months after the Wallenberg Centre had made a similar finding of genocide, based on its own research on the killings in Darfur. Wallenberg Centre legal adviser Mutasim Ali, in a social media post, said the U.S.decision to make the genocide declaration "should have been taken sooner.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_023.jpg	Cameron Hudson,an Africa analyst at the U.S.-based Center for Strategic and International Studies, said the U.S.announcement was long overdue. In a social media post, Mr. Hudson said the sanctions and genocide declaration will make it harder for the RSF to position itself as a legitimate political actor in postwar Sudan.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_024.jpg	In recent days, the RSF has been working on a plan to establish a government in the territory that it controls - mainly Darfur and some sections of Khartoum and east-central Sudan. This government, operating parallel to Sudan's official government, could pave the way for an unofficial partition of the country, similar to the situation in neighbouring Libya, where two rival governments are operating.

test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_033.jpg	BRIDGE
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_034.jpg	BY STEVE BECKER
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_035.jpg	WEDNESDAY, JANUARY 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_036.jpg	South dealer. Neither side vulnerable.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_037.jpg	The bidding:
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_038.jpg	Opening Lead - nine of clubs.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_039.jpg	There are many worthwhile conventions that enable a good defensive pair to score all the tricks to which they're entitled. Some players ignore these conventions, relying more on instinct than science, but this is a dangerous way to play the game.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_040.jpg	Take the present deal, which features top-notch partnership defence. West leads a club, East winning dummy's queen with the ace. East, diagnosing the lead as a possible singleton, returns the three of clubs, ruffed by West with the deuce.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_041.jpg	East's return of the three, rather than just any club, is conventional. It says that, as be-tween hearts and diamonds,he would prefer that West lead the lower-ranking suit next. (Had East preferred a heart return, he would have led the eight of clubs to ask for the higher-ranking suit.)
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_042.jpg	Accordingly，West leads the diamond three to East's queen and South's ace. Declarer plays a trump to dummy's jack and East's ace, and now East has a genuine problem.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_043.jpg	If he thinks West has another trump, East should return a club; but if he thinks West has no more trumps, his best chance is a diamond return. With only his own 13 cards to rely on, East might easily go wrong and hand South the contract. But another con-vention, the trump echo, comes to his rescue.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_044.jpg	West's ruff with the two of spades, followed later by the play of the four, denies a holding of three trumps. If West had started with the 6-4-2, for example, he would have ruffed the club at trick two with the four and later played the deuce to indicate possession of a third trump.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_045.jpg	East therefore returns a diamond at trick five,and four spades goes down two. With a club return, South makes the contract.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_014_line_047.jpg	O THE GLOBE AND MAIL | WEDNESDAY, JANUARY 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_000.jpg	Canadian exports to U.S. rose in November
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_001.jpg	Surplus may have political significance amid Trump's complaints about the trade deficit
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_002.jpg	MARK RENDELL
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_003.jpg	Canada posted its ninth-consecutive trade deficit in November, but its trade surplus with the United States grew that month, highlighting a key economic dynamic that has political implications ahead of Donald Trump's return to the White House later this month.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_004.jpg	Canada's total merchandise trade exports increased 2.2 per cent in November, while imports increased 1.8 per cent, narrowing the country's trade deficit with the rest of the world to \$323-million from a downwardly revised \$544-million the month before, Statistics Canada reported Tuesday.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_005.jpg	At the same time, the long- standing goods trade surplus with the United States increased to \$8.2-billion from \$6.6-billion in October, with exports to the U.S. up 6.8 per cent month-to-month and imports up 4.1 per cent.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_006.jpg	That monthly trade surplus – the result of Canada exporting more to the United States than it imports – is on the small side, compared to the average in re
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_007.jpg	Canada-U.S. merchandise trade
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_008.jpg	Monthly imports and exports, in billions of dollars
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_009.jpg	THE GLOBE AND MAIL, SOURCE: STATISTICS CANADA
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_010.jpg	cent years. However, it may carry larger political significance than usual, given Mr. Trump's complaints about the U.S. trade deficit with Canada and his threats of tariffs.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_011.jpg	The president-elect has promised to slap 25-per-cent tariffs on Canadian imports if Ottawa does not address American concerns about border security. Mr. Trump has also complained about his country's merchandise trade deficit with Canada, which has averaged a little over \$100-billion annually for the past three years on around \$1.1-trillion in annual two-way trade. The trade deficit is
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_012.jpg	smaller when services are taken into account.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_013.jpg	"We lose in trade deficits. We're losing massive," Mr. Trump said of trade with Canada in a news conference on Tuesday. "You know, they make 20 per cent of our cars. We don't need that. I'd rather make them in Detroit. We don't need their cars. We don't need their lumber. ... We don't need their dairy products. We have more than they have. We don't need anything."
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_014.jpg	Canada's trade-oriented economy relies heavily on access to the U.S. market. It's the destination for around 75 per cent of Can
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_015.jpg	adian exports, including \$166-billion worth of oil and gas exports and \$85-billion worth of vehicle and auto parts exports in 2023. High and broad-based tariffs could push Canada into a recession and stunt business investment.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_016.jpg	“The outlook for Canadian trade in 2025 is cloudy at best,” Toronto-Dominion Bank economist Marc Ercolao wrote in a note to clients. “Our base case is that Canada largely avoids a full-scale implementation of Trump’s tariff plan, given our energy-heavy relationship with the U.S. – though we acknowledge that any tariff levied against Canada has negative consequences for economic growth.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_017.jpg	The increase in overall Canadian exports was broad-based in November, with metals and non-metallic ores up 10.5 per cent, industrial machinery and parts up 3.8 per cent and aircraft and transportation equipment up 6.7 per cent. Energy exports rose 2.2 per cent, with a 4.8-per-cent jump in crude oil exports on stronger prices.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_018.jpg	Motor-vehicle exports, by contrast, contracted 1.2 per cent in November and remain considerably lower than a year ago.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_019.jpg	Canada's exports to the U.S. have been helped in recent months by a depreciation in the Canadian dollar against the U.S. dollar, which makes Canadian products more appealing to American buyers.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_020.jpg	Exports may also be getting a boost as companies try to “front-run” potential tariffs by moving goods across the border pre-emptively. A survey of more than 300 Canadian companies in December, conducted by the organization Canadian Manufacturers & Exporters, found that about one-third are accelerating shipments to the U.S.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_021.jpg	In volume terms, total exports were up 0.5 per cent in November.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_022.jpg	“The third consecutive rise in export volumes in November provides further evidence that the economy was gaining mo- mentum at the end of last year,” Stephen Brown, deputy chief North America economist at Cap- ital Economics, wrote in a note to clients. “U.S. tariffs could cause the recovery to go into reverse this year, but that threat might provide some further support in the near term if U.S. importers try to front-run the tariffs.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_023.jpg	Merchandise exports to countries other than the U.S. decreased 10.3 per cent in November, driven by lower shipments of gold to Hong Kong and nickel to Norway. Canada's trade deficit with countries other than the United States widened to \$8.5-billion in November from \$7.2-billion in October.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_024.jpg	Service exports, meanwhile, contracted 0.2 per cent, leaving the services trade deficit at about \$300-million, essentially unchanged from the previous month.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_025.jpg	Over a 36-year career at CIBC, John Hunkin led the bank's expansion in the U.S. market, and oversaw a lucrative push into financing technology companies. PATTI GOWER/THE GLOBE AND MAIL
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_026.jpg	Former CIBC CEO Hunkin dies at 79
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_027.jpg	ANDREW WILLIS
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_028.jpg	JAMES BRADSHAW
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_029.jpg	Former Canadian Imperial Bank of Commerce chief executive officer John Hunkin, who tore down silos in financial services, died Monday at the age of 79.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_030.jpg	Several sources said Mr. Hunkin died of a heart attack.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_031.jpg	In a message to CIBC employees, chief executive officer Victor Dodig said: “John was an inspiring leader at our bank, who emphasized our potential for growth and believed deeply in our ability to lead.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_032.jpg	Over a 36-year career at CIBC, Mr. Hunkin led the bank's expansion in the U.S. market and directed the union of investment dealer Wood Gundy, acquired after the 1987 stock market crash, with CIBC's traditional corporate lender.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_033.jpg	As the bank's CEO from 1999 to 2005, Mr. Hunkin oversaw a lucrative push into financing technology companies. He also dealt with the fallout from the collapse of Enron Corp., with CIBC paying billions to settle lawsuits filed by investors in the failed energy trader.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_034.jpg	“John was willing to be innovative and creative and made bold moves in the capital markets businesses which are responsible for the strong franchise CIBC has today,” said Richard Venn, a former CIBC executive and founding partner at Further Global Capital Management.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_035.jpg	Mr. Hunkin grew up in the bank's culture – his father was CIBC's head of human resources. Mr. Hunkin joined CIBC in 1969 after earning an undergraduate degree from the University of Manitoba in 1967 and his MBA from York University two years later.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_036.jpg	He supported York throughout his career, as a member of its board, foundation and business school's advisory council. He also volunteered at downtown Toron
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_037.jpg	to's St. Michael's Hospital.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_038.jpg	“John was a remarkable person in so many ways,” said Melissa Martin, the chair of the St. Mi- chael's Hospital Foundation. “He had an extraordinary business ca- reer, but beyond that he was a magnificent fundraiser.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_039.jpg	In 2011, Mr. Hunkin played a key role in securing a \$25-million donation to St. Michael’s from Hong Kong billionaire Li Kashing, who had a significant investment in CIBC. Ms. Martin said the donation ”ignited the hospital’s research initiatives and elevated our scientists’ work to new heights.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_040.jpg	Mr. Hunkin also supported Toronto's Centre for Addiction and Mental Health and the Montreal Museum of Fine Arts Foundation. In 2019, Governor-General Julie Payette awarded him the Order of Canada.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_041.jpg	Early in his banking career, he moved to London to help open CIBC's European credit division, new territory for the bank. The pioneering role caught the eye of CIBC executive Don Fullerton, who subsequently became CEO. He tasked Mr. Hunkin and colleague Al Flood with setting a strategy for U.S. expansion. The two bankers moved to New York and built a successful corporate lending team, run by Mr. Flood, who went on to succeed Mr. Fullerton as the bank's CEO and Mr. Hunkin's predecessor.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_042.jpg	In the late 1980s, the federal government liberalized financial regulations, allowing banks to own investment dealers. CIBC acquired Wood Gundy after the dealer lost much of its capital in the 1987 market crash. Initially, the two cultures clashed.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_043.jpg	To bridge the gulf, Mr. Hunkin quit his corporate lending job at CIBC, giving up his seniority at the bank, to become head of the investment banking team. Under his leadership, CIBC became the first domestic lender to bring corporate and investment banking under one roof.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_044.jpg	“John was the first banker to cross the line, to turn his back on his career as a corporate banker and jump into investment dealer, and he did it well,” said David Kassie, head of investment banking at CIBC and former chair and CEO at Canaccord Genuity Group Inc. “John excelled at building client relationships and was a really good manager of people.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_045.jpg	“Putting an investment dealer and a bank together was uncharted territory, but John made it happen, mainly because everyone trusted him,” said Tom Woods, CIBC’s former chief financial officer. “He was not afraid to take risks. CIBC’s electronic banking initiative in the U.S. was years ahead of its time, but ultimately had to be wound up.”
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_046.jpg	In 1999, Mr. Flood retired as CEO, and the CIBC board faced a choice between Mr. Hunkin and head of retail banking Holger Kluge as his successor. When Mr. Hunkin got the nod, Mr. Kluge departed.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_047.jpg	Over the first half of Mr. Hunkin's tenure as CEO, CIBC outperformed rivals, cashing in on investments in client companies such as fibre-optic network Global Crossing Ltd. CIBC became a leader in wealth management by acquiring the retail brokerage network of Merrill Lynch Canada Inc. for \$600-million.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_048.jpg	Mr. Hunkin spent the final years of his banking career dealing with Enron litigation. His successor, Gerry McCaughey, eventually settled the lawsuits for \$2.5-billion.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_049.jpg	Mr. Hunkin retired in 2005, and he and his wife, Susan Crocker, a former CIBC executive and CEO of the Healthcare of Ontario Pension Plan, moved to Chester, N.S. That summer, he skippered his newly acquired 48-foot yacht in the biennial Marblehead to Halifax Ocean Race, a 360-mile event from Massachusetts to Nova Scotia. He and his 12-person crew finished first among Canadian entrants.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_050.jpg	Ottawa to release letter clarifying rules on private pay for primary care
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_051.jpg	CHRIS HANNAY
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_052.jpg	Ottawa says it is still working on a long-promised letter clarifying the rules around out-of-pocket pay for primary health care services despite political upheaval in the capital, continuing months of delays that have created uncertainty for private providers.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_053.jpg	An estimated six million Canadians are not connected to a family doctor, and many have turned to alternate health care providers such as virtual care companies that may charge subscription or membership fees for services that would otherwise be covered by public insurance.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_054.jpg	Nearly two years ago, then-minister of health Jean-Yves Duclos said he was “very concerned” about recent increases in Canadians paying out-of-pocket for medically necessary care.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_055.jpg	He pledged to release an interpretation letter outlining the rules governing nurse practitioners and other non-physician health care providers as they relate to the Canada Health Act, which was passed in 1984 and only explicitly covers services from physicians and in hospitals.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_056.jpg	In the intervening years, nurse practitioners, pharmacists and other health professionals have been granted new authorities to order tests, prescribe medications and provide other services that would otherwise come from a primary-care physician. But they have been left in a grey area that allows them to operate as both public and private providers in a way physicians are barred from.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_057.jpg	Mark Holland, the Health Minister since July, 2023, has continued to promise legal clarity, but a letter has so far not been released.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_058.jpg	On Tuesday, after Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's move to prorogue Parliament and announcement that he will resign as Liberal Leader once a successor is determined, Mr. Holland's office provided few details when contacted by The Globe and Mail.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_059.jpg	“We hope to have more to say on the upcoming letter soon,” spokesperson Matthew Kronberg said in an e-mail.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_060.jpg	The delays have caused continued uncertainty for both those calling for more private health care options and those who say it is a drain on the publicly funded system.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_061.jpg	Brett Belchetz, a Toronto physician and chief executive officer of telemedicine platform Maple, has said such companies are necessary for Canadians who don’t have a family doctor.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_062.jpg	He said he is opposed to the type of user fees that “paywall” access to health care providers who then bill public insurance for their services. However, services that are funded 100 per cent by patients or private insurance should be welcomed, he added.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_063.jpg	“This is how options like employer-funded virtual care have come to be, which millions of Canadians rely on when they can’t find support in our overburdened public health system,” Dr. Belchetz wrote in a LinkedIn post last year.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_064.jpg	The Canadian Medical Association has recommended that the federal government ban membership fees, user fees or other sorts of payment for primary-care services covered by public insurance.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_065.jpg	CMA president Joss Reimer, who is a physician in Manitoba, said the association's top concern is that the growth of private providers is siphoning professionals away from the publicly funded system. That could lead to worsening work force shortages in the public system, which leads to even less access to public services, in a negative feedback loop.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_066.jpg	"Whenever we have providers that are using out-of-pocket, or private pay options, they are being pulled out of the public system to provide that service," Dr. Reimer said.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_067.jpg	Ontario Health Minister Sylvia Jones wrote to her federal counterpart last April recommending that non-physician health care providers be banned from charging fees to provide primary-care services.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_068.jpg	“I am asking for your department and your government consult and work with provinces and territories on a Canada-wide solution to close this loophole, to guard against unintended consequences, and prohibit nonphysicians from charging for publicly funded services,” the letter says.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_069.jpg	Ema Popovic, spokesperson for Ms. Jones, said Ontario is still waiting on a response from Ottawa to their letter.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_070.jpg	Whenever we have providers that are using out-of-pocket, or private pay options, they are being pulled out of the public system to provide that service.
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_071.jpg	DR. JOSS REIMER
test/images/newspaper_The Globe and Mail - 2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_072.jpg	CANADIAN MEDICAL ASSOCIATION PRESIDENT
