test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_002.jpg	BOUNDING on to the stage and jumping in the air with arms outstretched, Elon Musk looked like a man who'd just won the lottery.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_003.jpg	Donald Trump had asked him to join him on stage for the first time at an election rally in Butler, Pennsylvania, last October – the town where, just a few months earlier, the ex-president had narrowly survived an assassin's bullet.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_004.jpg	'He is a truly incredible guy - and I don't say that that often,' Trump declared of the tycoon who'd sunk no less than £211 million into his second presidential campaign.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_005.jpg	'As you can see I'm not just Maga [Make America Great Again], I'm dark Maga,' Musk told the cheering crowd as he pointed to his Trump cap – not the usual red, but black.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_006.jpg	It was clearly a joke but, since then, his critics might say that never a truer word was spoken in jest as Musk has behaved even more pugnaciously and wildly than Trump himself.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_007.jpg	The mercurial Musk has picked fights with politicians around the globe –from Republicans in Washington over government spending to European administrations, notably Keir Starmer over the grooming gang scandal in northern England and also Germany over immigration and crime.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_008.jpg	Less than two weeks before Trump is sworn in, Musk has effectively hijacked his presidency, soaking up the world's attention with his endless outbursts on X, his social media platform on which he has 211 million followers.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_009.jpg	But has the ebullient tech tycoon, who tweets through the night while running a raft of global businesses, already over-played his hand– risking killing his ‘bromance’ with the president-elect before the second term has even started?
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_010.jpg	That intriguing possibility has now been raised by Trump biographer and New York Times reporter Maggie Haberman.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_011.jpg	Dubbed the ‘Trump Whisperer’ for her deep connections in the court of The Donald, Haberman claims to have
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_012.jpg	been told that Trump is already tiring of the company of the world's richest man.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_013.jpg	ONLY two years ago, Musk said he wished Trump would ‘hang up his hat and sail into the sunset’. But Haberman says she is not surprised that Musk has ensconced himself in the heart of Trump’s inner circle, as he ‘has an enormous amount of money that he put to use helping Trump’ and ‘Trump equates wealth with intelligence’.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_014.jpg	However, speaking to the podcast series On With Kara Swisher, Haberman added: 'How long it lasts, I think, is the open question. A long-time Trump friend said something to me recently about how Trump is a "one-ring circus". I'm not sure that Musk has figured that out yet. And Trump does complain a bit to people
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_015.jpg	about how Musk is around a lot.’
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_016.jpg	Amid all the talk about President Musk, is Donald already tiring of his First Buddy?
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_017.jpg	Grabbing the limelight: Elon Musk and Donald Trump watch a SpaceX Starship launch
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_018.jpg	Musk is ‘around a lot’ primarily because, since the election in November, he has rented a ‘cottage’– which usually costs at least £1,600 a night – on the grounds of Trump’s main home, his private Mar-a-Lago club in Palm Beach, Florida. Consequently, he is only a couple of hundred feet from the mansion in which Trump is forming his next administration.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_019.jpg	Musk has been able to drop in to meetings and dinners with the president-elect, such as one he recently held with Amazon tycoon Jeff Bezos, and join his telephone calls.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_020.jpg	Haberman said that by moving into the Mar-a-Lago compound, Musk had ‘parked himself in Trump's face'.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_021.jpg	She added that taunts by Democrats about ‘President Musk’ and Trump’s First Lady being named ‘Elonia’ are increasingly riling the 78-year-old. ‘It definitely bothers him. The “President Musk” line
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_022.jpg	was always going to be a way to get him,' she said. 'Trump's not a wind-up toy, but there certainly are very specific things that can zotz [infuriate] him.'
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_023.jpg	She also observed that Musk seems to have been ‘more willing to irritate Trump than a lot of others have been – and less concerned about what it might mean’.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_024.jpg	TRUMP insiders have said much the same to news website Mediaite, claiming the president-elect was tiring of Musk's 'omnipresence' and the attention he was drawing.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_025.jpg	'100 per cent, Trump is annoyed,' said a source who worked on the 2024 election campaign. "There's a Chinese saying: "Two tigers cannot live on one mountaintop".'
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_026.jpg	Another insider was quoted as saying: ‘Someone that is around that much and having influence
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_027.jpg	would be a bother... I mean, the guy came in, gave a boatload of money and wants to take over.'
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_028.jpg	It's worth noting that Musk has made a fair few enemies in the Trump camp with his reportedly aggressive behaviour towards them, so there may be a strong element of wishful thinking that he's on his way out.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_029.jpg	For the moment, there's no public sign of a rift. Trump has asked Musk to co-lead (with fellow conservative entrepreneur Vivek Ramaswamy) a new 'Department of Government Efficiency', or Doge, although sceptics point out that only Congress can create new federal agencies and that this department is unlikely to get off the ground.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_030.jpg	That might give Trump the perfect excuse to avoid his ‘First Buddy’ – as the tycoon calls himself – although Musk could always ask for something else. After all, while opponents complain he’s
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_031.jpg	never been elected to public office (and so shouldn't be given a big post now), nor had Trump's son-in-law Jared Kushner when the ambitious young property developer was put in charge of leading US efforts to bring peace to the Middle East during his first term.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_032.jpg	Pundits predict that Musk will want to stay as physically close to Trump as possible - which, from January 20, means an office on the White House campus. And if not that, at least have one of the treasured 'access all areas' security passes that Trump used to give out to favoured cronies during his first term and which would allow Musk to wander around the White House at will.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_033.jpg	Haberman doesn't believe Musk will get the office or the pass - he has too many enemies and Trump insiders don't want him, of all people, 'running around this way', she says. But at the same time, she acknowledges Trump is traditionally slow to dispense with people who have been useful to him and rarely exiles anyone entirely.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_034.jpg	Indeed, some argue that Trump cannot afford to antagonise Musk. That's not only because of his vast wealth – and willingness to plough it into politics – or his ownership of the social media platform that remains the most powerful communication channel in the US.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_035.jpg	AMERICA has become increasingly reliant - some say too reliant - on Musk businesses. The 53-year-old, estimated to be worth £340billion, controls the Starlink satellite network which plays a crucial defence role and on which Ukraine's military depends for internet coverage.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_036.jpg	Nasa needs Musk's SpaceX rockets to get US equipment and astronauts into space. Having accepted his shilling and let him into the gang, Trump may find Musk - who knows how to bear a grudge just as much as he does - difficult to shrug off. There will certainly be many in Whitehall and other European governments who sincerely hope that Trump does rein him in - and soon.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_037.jpg	Some Washington experts say this is bound to happen. While it may be fine to drop in on the president-elect at Mar-a-Lago, once he's Leader of the Free World, this becomes a lot harder. Insiders claim that if and when Musk does officially become part of the next Trump administration, he will have to abide by the usual constraints on presidential officials.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_038.jpg	However, Musk is not great at restraint while Trump is not great at following the rules. And neither man, alike in more ways than just their huge egotism, likes sharing the stage.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_039.jpg	As Americans head into one of the most unpredictable presidencies in living memory, they can at least reassure themselves of one certainty. Born in South Africa to non-US parents, Musk is barred under the Constitution from running for the presidency himself.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_040.jpg	Kemi's push for vote on abuse inquiry today

The PM's official spokesman also said further inquiries were unnecessary, adding that Sir Keir was 'completely focused on' tackling sexual abuse.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_053.jpg	Daily Mail, Wednesday, January 8, 2025

test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_000.jpg	Howe puts one foot in final as Arteta hits a black-and-white wall
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_001.jpg	(2nd leg: February 5,St James' Park)
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_002.jpg	HERE'S what you could have won, Arsenal: Alexander Isak, Newcastle's supreme centre forward who is emerging as one of the deadliest goalscorers in Europe.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_003.jpg	And Anthony Gordon — the multi-faceted England winger whose reputation as one of this country's most impressive attacking talents is burgeoning.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_004.jpg	Both players have been courted by Arsenal over the past 12 months. Both struck ruthlessly here to plunge a dagger into the Gunners' hopes of winning the Carabao Cup for the first time in 32 years.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_005.jpg	It is only half-time, of course, and Arsenal — being Arsenal — will believe they can pull off the turnaround at St James’ Park in the second leg next month.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_006.jpg	But on the evidence of this showing, they haven't got a cat in hell's chance. Not if they let Isak run riot like he did here.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_007.jpg	That's not to say this was a one-man show. Far from it.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_008.jpg	This was a Newcastle away performance bursting with ability, discipline, work ethic and pride. Isak's showing was merely the icing on the cake.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_009.jpg	Arsenal will know they will need to find an extra gear — maybe two — in the second leg. This was not a display in keeping with the reputation Mikel Arteta has built here.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_010.jpg	If they can find those levels, then they can head north with hope in their hearts. This was a bruising night for them, but not fatal; they will cling on to that optimism.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_011.jpg	The League Cup has taken a battering in its various guises over the years. People even say playing at Wembley doesn't mean as much as it once did. Try telling that to the 22 players who started the game here.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_012.jpg	Try telling that to the jam-packed Emirates crowd who were gripped and hyped from the very start.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_013.jpg	Try telling that to Jurrien Timber, William Saliba, Lewis Hall and Gabriel Martinelli, who were all agonised after missing first-half opportunities to open the scoring.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_014.jpg	Try telling that to Isak, who did open the scoring. Winning trophies matters and playing Wembley is as magical as it ever was.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_015.jpg	Arsenal's start to the game was rip-roaring. Declan Rice's inswinging corners were causing Newcastle all manner of trouble and Timber should really have dispatched a header in the 13th minute from his team-mate's whipped set-piece.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_016.jpg	For the opening 20 minutes, it was all hands to pump for the Geordies as Arsenal tried to seize the initiative.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_017.jpg	But gradually Newcastle grew. As did the decibel level from the away end after Hall's effort was blocked following brilliant centre-forward play from Isak.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_018.jpg	Arsenal's early flow had been stifled. Joelinton's clear remit of sticking like glue to Arsenal captain Martin Odegaard was integral to Newcastle finding their feet.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_019.jpg	Yet had Martinelli not struck the post with just Martin Dubravka to beat following
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_020.jpg	Leandro Trossard's sumptuous touch in the 29th minute, perhaps the opening 45 minutes would have gone in Arsenal's favour. It didn't, though — thanks to Isak.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_021.jpg	Newcastle's opener was route-one football at its most effective. Dubravka's drilled punt forward was flicked on by Sven Botman, Jacob Murphy bundled it on and the ball was hammered home by Isak.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_022.jpg	Arsenal's interest in the Swede is no secret. If the Gunners can make the finances work there is
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_023.jpg	every chance Isak will be wheeling away in celebration at the Emirates more frequently next season. But that's for then. The present is far more pressing.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_024.jpg	Gabriel Magalhaes was thwarted towards the end of the first half as Arsenal strived to pull level.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_025.jpg	The instructions from Arteta at half-time became abundantly clear during the opening the stages of the second period. The pace was slower, far more controlled by the Gunners. Like
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_026.jpg	chalk and cheese in comparison to the whirlwind first half.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_027.jpg	It made sense for Arsenal to take the sting out of the game. Going toe-to-toe with Newcastle did not reap the rewards they had hoped for in the first half.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_028.jpg	As it happens, nor did the second-half approach. Again Isak took centre stage when somehow, with three Arsenal players around him, he forced David Raya into a save to leave Gordon the simple task of firing in at the back post.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_029.jpg	Newcastle were euphoric. It's a
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_030.jpg	long journey down to London from up there but the return trip will not seem quite so arduous.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_031.jpg	Arsenal—simply put—couldn't deal with Isak. Saliba and Gabriel are a formidable pairing but the striker was making them look ordinary here.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_032.jpg	Kai Havertz missed a glorious opportunity just before the hour to half the deficit. Unmarked from six yards, he headed wide; replays showed his effort actually came off his shoulder.
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_033.jpg	Arteta had seen enough, the
test/images/newspaper_Daily Mail_2022-08-01@magazinesclubnew_page_068_line_035.jpg	Daily Mail, Wednesday, January 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_000.jpg	Why Trump's Greenland pitch makes good sense
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_001.jpg	In US hands, the minerals and seaboard of the world's biggest island would strengthen the West
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_002.jpg	Roger Boyes
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_003.jpg	@ROGERBOYES
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_004.jpg	If there is still a globe in the Oval Office after January 20, President Trump will no doubt give it a spin and wonder again about the huge, frozen landmass within shooting distance of American cities. Greenland — two million square kilometres of it — is in geopolitical contention again.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_005.jpg	China craves its rare earth minerals. It imagines the world's biggest island becoming part of its Polar Silk Road, a crucial stopover for its vessels as they exploit the melting Arctic ice to boost trade between Asia and the West.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_006.jpg	Trump has an answer to that: he wants to buy the place. Rebuffed by Denmark and Greenlanders in his first term, he is ready for a second go. Donald Trump Jr has just touched down in Greenland to start the ball rolling. Two questions, then. Will he succeed? And what changes if the United States, literally, grows?
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_007.jpg	His first attempt was treated as a joke, an act of flamboyant Trumpian overreach. Greenland’s prime minister — it has home rule while remaining part of Denmark, which runs its foreign policy and hands out subsidies — declared the island was not for sale. But Trump understood this as a bargaining manoeuvre and never gave up on the idea. After all, Harry Truman considered buying
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_008.jpg	Greenland after the Second World War as a way of cutting off a potential Soviet attack. The purchase never went ahead but America was allowed to build a network of bases, including Thule, which became a key early missile warning station. That gave both Washington and the Greenlanders a sense of security.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_009.jpg	The end of the Cold War in the 1990s thinned the American presence. Greenlanders missed the jobs and the dollars but they did not miss the Americans: the thunder of aircraft, the pregnant girls and the occasional scare, such as in January 1968 when a B-52 carrying four hydrogen bombs caught fire and plunged into sea ice just outside
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_010.jpg	Understandably, the Danes do not want to lose face or land
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_011.jpg	Thule. The Danes furiously demanded that all the sunken unexploded bombs and the irradiated ice be packed up and flown back to the US for dismantling. If not, Denmark would shut down the Thule base. The US grudgingly agreed.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_012.jpg	The world, however, has changed for the US, Denmark and the Greenlanders. Since Trump won the election, China has been flexing its muscles. It has conducted its largest naval exercises in decades, launched the world's biggest amphibious warship, been accused of sabotaging undersea cables in Asia and Europe and hacking the US Treasury, unveiled four new advanced aircraft, rehearsed a naval blockade near
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_013.jpg	Japanese islands for the first time and intensified espionage activity in the West. The point? To help Beijing negotiate with Trump from a position of strength.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_014.jpg	To counter this, Trump needs to evolve an approach of his own. The Greenland ploy, coupled perhaps with attempts to regain control of the sensitive Panama Canal, represents a kind of recognition by Trump that the global climate really is changing the strategic environment. After all, a melting Arctic means easier mining access to nickel and cobalt, essential for batteries. And it will break western dependency on China for the rare metals needed for microchips and sonar systems. That represents a learning process from the days of Trump 1.0.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_015.jpg	Expanding America might seem like a vanity project — the acquisition of Greenland would be almost as significant as the Louisiana purchase of 1803. It would certainly be a sign of a confident superpower. But if not negotiated with sensitivity, it could put him in the same basket as land-grabbing Vladimir Putin's pursuit of a New Russia.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_016.jpg	At the moment, he seems to be trusting in his powers of persuasion over Greenland's puny population of 56,000. An old signpost at Kangerlussuaq airport points to Copenhagen (4 hours 15 minutes' flying time), North Pole (3 hours and 15 minutes) and New York (4 hours).
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_017.jpg	Trump may bet he can make the whole island more prosperous with US investment; make a shopping weekend in New York as feasible as it became for the Icelandic middle
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_018.jpg	class. Denmark subsidises Greenland but locals say it comes with too much thinly disguised interference. Above all, there is deep resentment about the colonial haughtiness of the Danes. The latest spat is about forced contraception that was pushed on teenage native Greenlanders in the 1970s by the Danish authorities.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_019.jpg	Denmark, understandably, does not want to lose face or land. King Frederik has recently adjusted the royal coat of arms to include a much larger polar bear representing Greenland. Islanders regarded this as a bit of trolling.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_020.jpg	Trump could be placing his bets on an independence referendum being held in April. If the Greenlanders vote for it persuasively, it might open another debate: whether the island, with more power to determine its foreign policy, could in some way affiliate with the US.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_021.jpg	What a signal to China! Maga made flesh. And yet there will be some around Trump who may caution him that facilitating a split with a Nato ally is not a good look. It is, after all, a longstanding ambition of Putin to rule by splitting the western alliance.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_022.jpg	A more rational goal for the America-expanders may be to reach an arrangement with Greenland and Denmark that allows the US to use those western Greenland territories closest to the US eastern seaboard as a security cushion. Prosperity for Greenlanders, enhanced security for the US and a partial loss of sovereignty for Denmark offset by the offer of a deeper relationship with Washington. A grand bargain?
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_023.jpg	Tom Whipple Notebook
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_024.jpg	Ignorance of history was bliss for Bill, Ted and me
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_025.jpg	Bill & Ted's Excellent Adventure is, to my mind, a most bodacious film, which was why I feared showing it to my children. A joy of parenting is introducing your kids to things you love. A sadness is seeing them endure the things you love out of politeness.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_026.jpg	Had it aged badly? Nervously, I watched their reaction.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_027.jpg	A primer, for those whose cultural education inexplicably passed over this 1980s classic: for the sake of the future of the universe, for reasons that really don't matter, the teenage Ted has to pass his school history project. To achieve this, time travellers from the future help him meet key figures from the past — Genghis Khan, Sigmund Freud, Napoleon, Socrates, Joan of Arc.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_028.jpg	Would my sons consider this conceit — a ludicrous worry, I know — contrived? Worse, as we lament the shallowness of history
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_029.jpg	curriculums, would they understand the references?
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_030.jpg	I needn't have worried. What I realised was, far from my children not understanding, it was me who hadn't. Nine-year-old me enjoyed the slapstick of Genghis Khan wrecking a sports shop. I hadn't known, however, who Genghis Khan was, even with the narrative exposition. "This is a dude who, 700 years ago, totally ravaged China," explained Ted, "and who, two hours ago, totally ravaged Oshman's Sporting Goods."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_031.jpg	My former self laughed at a short Frenchman cheating at bowling then planning an invasion of Russia. But if you had asked me who Napoleon was? Not a clue.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_032.jpg	My own children had no such confusion. The reason? Another TV show, *Horrible Histories*. Thanks to the CBBC series, they have better basic history knowledge than I do today. So do their friends.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_033.jpg	Imagine if one of Michael Gove’s education reforms had produced this level of improvement in subject knowledge. Education ministers would travel from around the world to copy it.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_034.jpg	Now I hear that *Horrible Science* is coming to TV. It will be, as Ted would say, most triumphant.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_035.jpg	Clean thinking
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_036.jpg	During my morning shower I might just have resolved, for myself at least, that centuries-old philosophical battle: whether or not free will is an illusion.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_037.jpg	Inside the shower, the world is nice. Outside, it is cold and, in my house, beset by children. So,
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_038.jpg	leaving it is hard. What I find interesting — and I have spoken to others who find the same — is that leaving happens outside of my conscious thought. I turn off the
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_039.jpg	shower and, only a half-second later, do I
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_040.jpg	realise, with regret, that I have done so. I would think I was mad if this didn't accord with some — debated —
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_041.jpg	neuroscience. In the 1980s a scientist named Benjamin
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_042.jpg	Libet asked people to flex a finger. He also asked them to closely watch a clock and remember the time they made the decision to do so. He showed, using
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_043.jpg	probes in their brains, that the motor cortex started preparing for the movement before the person claimed to have told it to do so. Conscious control was an illusion.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_044.jpg	Maybe, in our cacophony of thoughts, this is missed. Perhaps it is only with simple decisions, such as switching off a shower or flicking a finger, our brain can glimpse the shocking truth about itself: it is nothing more than chemistry.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_045.jpg	Everyday miracle
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_046.jpg	Each year, at least once, our family try to see the starlings murmurate. In a damp, grey wetland at a damp, grey dusk, we wait. There is always a moment I think it won't happen. Then, in small groups, they arrive. Soon there is a boiling blob of thousands, shifting and morphing. It is otherworldly, a fluttering, black collective against skeletal trees. For 20 minutes they dance, then, as one, their wings beat with the noise of a sudden storm and they drop into the reeds to sleep.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_047.jpg	It feels like the sort of spectacle that happens in remote places frequented by the BBC natural history unit. But this isn't the Serengeti. It's here, every winter, in Theale or Twyford or somewhere near you. And most evenings we go, we're the only ones watching.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_048.jpg	Matthew Parris is away
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_049.jpg	We should recall what prompted the Jay inquiry Harvey Proctor
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_050.jpg	Professor Alexis Jay, chair of the Independent Inquiry into Child Sexual Abuse (IICSA), declared her dismay yesterday at the "politicisation of child sexual abuse". While her observation is timely, it begs a fundamental question: where was her criticism of the politicians who relentlessly drove this inquiry into existence for their own ends?
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_051.jpg	The origins of the IICSA are rooted not in impartial concern for justice but in the politically charged fervour whipped up by individuals such as Tom Watson, John Mann and Zac Goldsmith, three figures now elevated to the House of Lords. Each used their position to push falsehoods and fuel a national moral panic under the guise of exposing a so-called "VIP paedophile network".
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_052.jpg	Watson, in particular, stands out for his questionable use of his parliamentary privilege. He peddled the lies of Carl Beech, a convicted fraudster and paedophile whose lurid claims became the backbone of Operation Midland. Watson's baseless accusations tarnished the reputations of dedicated public servants including the D-Day hero Lord Bramall, falsely accusing him of heinous crimes such as child abuse.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_053.jpg	Theresa May, as home secretary, irresponsibly echoed this hysteria by stating in November 2014 that Operation Midland was merely “the tip of the iceberg”. It wasn’t even the tip of an ice cube.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_054.jpg	Let us not forget that the IICSA was a product of political pressure, launched not to investigate grooming gangs nor to address systemic failures but to appease MPs hoping to make political capital by pushing for answers to the fabricated claims of abuse at the hands of VIPs. When Operation Midland collapsed, the inquiry scrambled to pivot its focus elsewhere, including grooming gangs — an area in which its work was neither comprehensive nor free from political undertones.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_055.jpg	If Jay is genuinely concerned about politicisation, she must confront the uncomfortable reality that her inquiry, too, was born from it. While the IICSA may have brought some clarity, it left key questions unanswered: chiefly, why Pakistani grooming gangs were allowed to operate with impunity for so long.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_056.jpg	Communities in northern towns and cities deserve justice for the systemic failures that enabled such horrors. Yet these same failures remain largely unexamined. It is time to focus on meaningful reforms as well as focused and truly independent inquiries, not on perpetuating the culture of suspicion and slander that brought about this inquiry in the first place.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_057.jpg	Harvey Proctor is a former Conservative MP and president of Falsely Accused Carers and Teachers (Fact)
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_058.jpg	Comment
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_020_line_059.jpg	Wednesday January 8 2025 | THE TIMES
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_000.jpg	How budget will affect labour costs
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_001.jpg	Source: Next
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_002.jpg	In a detailed report, Next also warned of the challenges to the wider UK economy, cautioning that growth was likely to slow as "employer tax increases, and their potential impact on prices and employment, begin to filter through into the company".
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_003.jpg	Next said the rise in national insurance for employers “creates a disproportionate increase in the cost of part-time work in stores", which makes up a significant proportion of employment across the
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_004.jpg	industry. To highlight the point, Next included a table in its trading update which showed the national insurance changes on three levels of salaries. The graph showed that a £9,520 part-time national living wage salary will increase by 6.5 per cent, while a full-time wage of £22,310 would increase by 3.5 per cent and a £60,000 salary by 2 per cent.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_005.jpg	Higher costs of employing low-paid, part-time workers could deter retailers from hiring, reducing opportunities for those
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_006.jpg	seeking flexible roles to re-enter the workforce. This risks undermining social mobility and getting more people back into the workplace.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_007.jpg	The high street leader has added to a growing number of retail businesses, including AO and Currys, warning over price rises to combat cost increases. The British Chambers of Commerce said earlier this week that more than half of companies were planning to lift prices over the next three months due to cost pressures.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_008.jpg	Asda trails the pack for festive sales
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_009.jpg	Emma Taggart
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_010.jpg	Debt-laden Asda was the worst-performing supermarket chain over the Christmas period.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_011.jpg	The private equity-controlled group suffered a 5.8 per cent fall in sales in the 12 weeks to December 29, driving its market share down from 13.5 per cent to 12.5 per cent, data from the market research group Kantar showed.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_012.jpg	Asda said it had seen improvements to its sales and market share in the four weeks to December 29 compared with the same period in 2023.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_013.jpg	The Leeds-based grocer has struggled with slowing sales since it was bought by the private equity firm TDR and the billionaire Issa brothers in 2021 in a highly leveraged £6.8 billion take-over deal. Asda's large debt pile has also prevented it from keeping up with lower-priced competitors such as the German discounters Aldi and Lidl.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_014.jpg	In November Lord Rose of Monewden, then chairman of Asda, admitted that the group had “not been as sharp on our trading stance as we should have been... we have lost a bit of market share and a bit of momentum".
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_015.jpg	The fall in Asda's market share comes as inflation across supermarkets climbed to its highest level since March last year. Grocery price inflation rose to 3.7 per cent last month, up from 2.6 per cent in November, according to Kantar.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_016.jpg	Overall grocery sales climbed 2.1 per cent in the four weeks to December 29, as households spent an average of £460 on groceries during the period.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_017.jpg	Fraser McKevitt, the head of retail and consumer insight at Kantar, said: “It was a solid Christmas at the supermarkets with sales surpassing ￡13 billion during the four weeks of December for the first time ever."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_018.jpg	Tesco continued to solidify its position as Britain's biggest supermarket chain as it grew its market share by 0.8 percentage points to 28.5 percent in 12 weeks to December 29.Sales rose 5 per cent in the festive period.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_019.jpg	Sainsbury's reported its largest market share since December 2019 at 16 per cent. Sales at Britain's second biggest supermarket group outpaced the wider market to rise 3.5 per cent in the 12 weeks to December 29.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_020.jpg	The German discount chains Aldi and Lidl both recorded their highest market share for the festive quarter, at 10 per cent and 7.3 per cent respectively.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_021.jpg	Telegraph boss thought sale would go quicker
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_022.jpg	James Hurley
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_023.jpg	The chief executive of Telegraph Media Group has sought to reassure staff after the newspaper's editor warned of an “inevitable sense of drift” over the protracted auction of the business.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_024.jpg	Anna Jones, who took over last year amid a potential sale of The Telegraph, admitted that the group's executives thought that an acquisition of the newspaper would have been “done and dusted" by now.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_025.jpg	She said that there was “lots of pressure" on the business as a result of a sale
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_026.jpg	process, which was called “the newspaper auction from hell” by the Columbia Journalism Review, but said staff were able to “shut out the noise".
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_027.jpg	Jones's intervention followed a new year message to staff from Chris Evans, The Telegraph's editor-in-chief, in which he described the situation as unsatisfactory. “Last year was supposed to be the year when The Telegraph was sold. We were told we would be sold by the summer, then by autumn and, at the latest,by Christmas,” he wrote. "But.. we don't know when it might be sold."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_028.jpg	He added: “There's obviously a limit to what we can do in these circumstances but we're going to try to do everything we can to recover momentum and to defeat the inevitable sense of drift."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_029.jpg	RedBird IMI, a venture part-funded by an Emirati royal, has been in control of The Telegraph's future for nearly eight months but is struggling to find a buyer. The fund is seeking full repayment of the money it initially invested in the debt secured against The Telegraph and The Spectator, its sister publication, which was part of a plan for a full takeover of the titles.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_030.jpg	Ministers blocked its attempts to convert its debt into equity ownership amid concerns about foreign powers buying UK media assets. RedBird IMI sold The Spectator to Paul Marshall,the GB News owner, for £100 million but is seeking to recoup a total of £600 million. Lord Saatchi said RedBird had “over-paid" when his £350 million offer for The Telegraph was rejected in August.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_031.jpg	The Telegraph has reported calls from senior politicians for Lisa Nandy, the culture secretary, to intervene.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_032.jpg	Yesterday Jones wrote on LinkedIn: “The Telegraph Media Group has been for sale for 18 months now and I've been CEO for almost a year. To be candid, we thought it would be done and dusted within six months."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_033.jpg	Jones, a former chief executive of Hearst Magazines, added: “As someone who's been through these processes before and understands how challenging it can be... I remind everyone that yes there's lots of pressure but not all of it is real ... Listening to too many outside voices can become exhausting so we've been mindful to shut out the noise and focus on creating our own destiny."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_034.jpg	Wolfson tidings spell trouble ahead
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_035.jpg	BUSINESS COMMENTARY Alistair Osborne
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_036.jpg	Christmas traditions aren't always fair. Take the one that allows Lord Wolfson of Aspley Guise to parade his festive figures ahead of just about every other retailer. How are the rest supposed to keep up with the Next boss: a best-in-class shopkeeper, who once again has upgraded profits guidance?
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_037.jpg	Typically, Santa delivered for Next: full-price sales up an underlying 5.7 per cent over Wolfson's nine weeks of Noel and enough jingling in the tills to nudge the full-year profit forecasts another £5 million higher to £1.01 billion pre-tax. As for the shares, they rose 4 per cent to £99.12.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_038.jpg	So, all's well with the world? Not exactly. Wolfson's seasonal update was crowned in thorns: a looming £67 million jump in wage costs, mainly due to Rachel Reeves's budget, and a warning that the UK economy is in for a“lacklustre" year. As he put it: “We believe that UK growth is likely to slow, as employer tax increases, and their potential impact on prices and employment, begin to filter through into the economy." As ever with Wolfson, too, he brought some forensics to the subject-the sort seemingly beyond most chief executives, let alone government ministers.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_039.jpg	The chancellor's budget raised staff costs in two ways. First by upping the national living wage. Then by lifting the main rate of employers' national insurance contributions (NICs) to 15 percent from 13.8 per cent and also lowering the threshold at which the tax kicks in to £5,000 from £9,100. And it's over the threshold that Wolfson's analysis is particularly telling.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_040.jpg	Most employees in Next's 458 UK shops are part-timers, working when the stores are busiest. They span mothers with school-age kids, student sand people between jobs. And when the tax changes take effect in April it's those people who'll be pricier to employ — £20 million more a year in Next's case.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_041.jpg	As Wolfson shows, the impact of the higher living wage and the NIC changes on a part-time worker earning £9,520 a year for a 16-hour week tots up to 13.2 per cent of their wage packet. Contrast a full-time staffer on £60,000 a year: just 4 per cent.As he points out, the lower threshold "creates a disproportionate increase in the cost of part-time work in stores".
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_042.jpg	In short, Reeves's budget has the biggest impact on poorer workers, Labour's typical constituency. As Wolfson explains: “I don't object to it [the lower threshold] in principle. It's just the speed and scale at which these changes are coming in that is damaging. I don't think they were well thought through. It would have been more sensible to phase them in." Worse, they will “exacerbate” a labour market that was “already softening". This Christmas Next had 20 per cent fewer vacancies but “25 per cent more people applying".
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_043.jpg	Next can cope.Wolfson will offset higher staff costs with productivity savings and a 1 per cent rise in prices. But, with consumer spending under pressure, he still sees lower growth this year — full-price sales up 3.5 per cent and profits 3.6 per
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_044.jpg	cent versus 2024-25’s 5.5 per cent and 10 percent. True, he's renowned for downplaying his forecasts, even if he says: “It's better than the alternative". Yet, if a retailer as well-run and financed as Next sees trouble ahead, what does that mean for the rest of the sector? Probably not the happiest new year.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_045.jpg	A starter for six
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_046.jpg	Being a self-styled “investor", “blackjack card counter" and “chess master" are no obvious qualifications for successfully sacking the entire boards of seven UK investment trusts. But, they should at least give Saba Capital's founder boss Boaz Weinstein a decent appreciation of odds. Hence, one thought: surely even he isn't expecting victory at all of them.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_047.jpg	The biggest clue? That he's nominated one of his sidekicks to the board of no less than six of them. Who he? Well, not Max, Saba's chief happiness officer, a key member of the US hedge fund's seven-strong team, right down to his bow tie, even if he happens to be a dog — but Paul Kazarian, a partner & portfolio manager. As you hardly need one of the under-fire trusts, CQS Natural Resources, to point out: “Membership of six UK-quoted company boards is a case of ‘over-boarding', which most voting advisory bodies would consider to be a breach of the usual standards of corporate governance."
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_048.jpg	They would. Lob in Kazarian's existing director or trusteeships at three US-listed companies and he would be quite a busy bee,flitting around to endless board meetings. And that's before you consider just how conflicted he'd be, doing all that while still managing Saba portfolios . As CQS also notes: "Saba’s materials state that Paul Kazarian has extensive experience as an investment trust director. In fact, according to Companies House records, it appears he has never acted as a director of a UK investment trust, nor even a UK company of any sort." Starting out with six would be kind of pushing it. Even Weinstein must know that.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_049.jpg	Reeves in a pickle
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_050.jpg	No one's yet hit an iceberg — or any other sort of lettuce. But look at the latest from the gilts market: yields on the 30-year have hit a 27-year high, up 0.07 percentage points to 5.24 per cent. Even the mini-budget of Liz Truss and Kwasi Kwarcrash failed to hit such heady heights.
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_051.jpg	It leaves Rachel Reeves in a bit of a pickle. With borrowing costs rocketing, she's now tight on fiscal headroom, with all but £1 billion of the £9.9 billion wiped out on Capital Economics estimates. The upshot? She could face, it reckons, the “nasty choice of breaking her fiscal rules or announcing more tax rises and/or spending restraint at a time when the economy is already weak". How wilting would that be for Reeves and her boss, Starmergeddon?
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_052.jpg	alistair.osborne@thetimes.co.uk
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_031_line_053.jpg	THE TIMES | Wednesday January 8 2025

test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_007.jpg	Killer sudoku
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_009.jpg	The normal rules of sudoku apply : fill each row , column and 3x3 box with all the numbers from 1 to 9. In addition , the digits in each inner shape ( marked by dots ) must add up to the number in the top corner of that box . No digit can be repeated within an inner shape .
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_010.jpg	Medium
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_011.jpg	Codeword
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_012.jpg	Each letter of the alphabet makes at least one appearance in the grid , and is represented by the same number wherever it appears . The letters decoded should help you to identify other letters and words in the grid .
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_013.jpg	Guardian cryptic crossword No 29,586 set by Pasquale
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_052_line_014.jpg	Across

(8) 9 Wreath made specially with a name on the front (6) 10 Crustacean, a sort confused with fish (8) 11 Mad Parisian, top bod acquiring condemnation (14) 13 Opposing team with angry thought? test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_083_line_052.jpg	RIVIERA TRAVEL
test/images/newspaper_The Times UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_083_line_053.jpg	0808 291 4718 thetimes.com/rtravel
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_000.jpg	PERSONAL JOURNAL.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_001.jpg	BY ANDREA PETERSEN
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_002.jpg	Researchers Dig Into Risks Of Ultra-Processed Foods
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_003.jpg	How packaged products are made could hold answers to why some are more unhealthy
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_004.jpg	A new study is helping to answer a pressing nutrition question: Which ultra-processed foods are harming our health-and which might not be so bad?
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_005.jpg	The problem is the way many packaged foods are made, researchers believe. Products such as many frozen pizzas, cereals and chips pack more calories per gram than less-processed foods do. And most ultra-processed foods have combinations of salt,fat, sugar and carbohydrates that aren't generally found in nature, which can make us crave them. Diets high in packaged foods without those traits-such as canned peaches or refried beans-don't seem to lead people to overeat and gain weight, at least not as much.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_006.jpg	Those are the findings so far of a continuing study investigating how ultra-processed food affects our bodies.Scientists presented their interim data at a workshop put on by the National Institutes of Health and the U.S.Food and Drug Administration in December.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_007.jpg	“There might be a way to create the quote,unquote healthy ultra- processed food that's still convenient,”said Kevin D.Hall, the principal investigator of the study and a scientist at the NIH, giving an example of a frozen meal with brown rice,beans and a lot of vegetables.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_008.jpg	Ultra-processed foods make up about 58% of the calories that U.S. adults and children ages 1 and older consume in a day,according to an analysis of federal data. Foods are generally considered ul- tra-processed if they contain in- gredients that you wouldn't find in a home kitchen,such as high- fructose corn syrup and emulsifiers. Yet many scientists believe not all these foods are equally bad-and some might be reasonably healthy.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_009.jpg	Recent studies have linked diets high in ultra-processed foods to increased risks of obesity, Type 2 diabetes, cancer, cardiovascular disease and depression. Robert F. Kennedy Jr., the president-elect's nominee to lead the Department of Health and Human Services,blames chemicals and additives in food for widespread health problems.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_010.jpg	'Energy dense'
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_011.jpg	When companies make, say,an energy bar, they often break down the cellular structure of the raw ingredients and remove the water, Hall said.Getting rid of water makes sense for food safety,Hall said,since moisture promotes the growth of bacteria.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_012.jpg	The process also makes foods more energy dense-meaning they contain more calories per gram than many minimally processed foods do.In the study, the meals with energy-dense foods had about two calories per gram, compared with about one calorie per gram for the meals with fewer energy-dense foods.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_013.jpg	In addition,many packaged foods combine fat, sugar,sodium and carbs in ways typically not found in nature.For instance, bagels are usually high in carbs and
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_014.jpg	sodium,muffins are high in fat and sugar,and french fries are high in fat and sodium.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_015.jpg	Researchers call these types of foods “hyperpalatable”and they have a strong effect on our brain's reward system,said Tera Fazzino, associate director of the Cofrin Logan Center for Addiction Research and Treatment at the University of Kansas.This makes it difficult to
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_016.jpg	stop eating them, Fazzino said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_017.jpg	Many seemingly healthy foods are hyperpalatable,including many full-fat flavored yogurts (high fat, high sugar). Salted nuts are hyperpalatable (high sodium,high fat), but plain,unsalted nuts aren't.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_018.jpg	Plain nuts “are good, they are pleasant to consume,but I don't eat the whole can in one sitting,” Fazzino said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_019.jpg	WHERE TO FIND HEALTHIER OPTIONS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_020.jpg	Preliminary study results and other research suggest that healthier choices for ultra-processed foods include
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_021.jpg	► Canned peaches ▶ Canned refried beans ► Canned fruit cocktail ► Jarred salsa ► Low-sodium soups ▶ Low-fat flavored yogurt
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_022.jpg	The study
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_023.jpg	In the current NIH study, 36 participants live for a month at a facility where their meals are tightly controlled.They spend each week on one of four diets.They are offered about 6,000 calories a day and can eat as much as they like.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_024.jpg	For one week, they eat minimally processed foods such as plain oatmeal with strawberries and walnuts.For the other weeks, they get versions of an ultra-processed diet.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_025.jpg	In one,the meals are energy dense-such as packaged oatmeal with added protein powder,sugar and cream and sweetened cranber-ries-with most calories coming from hard-to-resist hyperpalatable foods,like honey-roasted peanuts.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_026.jpg	In a second ultra-processed diet,meals are energy dense, but fewer calories come from those
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_027.jpg	hard-to-resist foods. The third diet included foods such as scrambled eggs from a liquid egg product with spinach,flavored yogurt and packaged oatmeal with fiber powder and heavy cream.Those meals weren't energy-dense or hard to resist.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_028.jpg	The diets contain roughly the same amounts of calories, fat, sugar, sodium and fiber.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_029.jpg	In interim results,which came from 18 people,participants reported feeling just as full and satisfied on all the diets.But when they were on the energy-dense diet with foods such as honey- roasted peanuts,they ate about 1,000 more calories a day than they did while eating the diet with plain oatmeal with strawberries and walnuts.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_030.jpg	Not all the ultra-processed diets had such dramatic calorie differences. When people were on the ultra-processed diet that was neither energy dense nor hyperpalatable, they consumed only about 170 calories more a day.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_031.jpg	People gained about 1 pound after a week on the energy-dense and hard-to-resist diet.They lost about a half a pound on the minimally processed diet-and also on the one with the liquid-scrambled egg product.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_032.jpg	The findings suggest that eating packaged foods doesn't automatically result in overeating or weight gain,as long as yous tick to foods such as canned refried beans or fruit cocktail,which are low in calories per gram and don't have the problematic combinations of fat.salt.carbs and sugar.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_033.jpg	There are other concerns about convenience foods beyond weight gain.Some of the additives that many contain have been linked to health problems.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_034.jpg	What you can do
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_035.jpg	The NIH research isn't complete. But its findings so far suggest you can choose healthier packaged foods by checking labels and cal- culating calories per gram.Avoid foods that clock in at two calories per gram or more,such as frozen meatballs,and stick to those that are around one calorie per gram, like low-fat flavored yogurt.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_036.jpg	Or you can combine foods to dilute the overall energy density of your mealHall said. Instead of eating chicken fingers with mac and cheesehave them with a big salad.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_037.jpg	To avoid hyperpalatable foods, look for products that are low in sodium, Fazzino says.This can include items such as low-sodium frozen meals and soups,she says.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_038.jpg	Find Your Digital Photos to Organize
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_039.jpg	BY SHARA TIBKEN
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_040.jpg	Sort through old photos. It's that to-do list item that keeps getting pushed down to make room for tasks that aren't so over-whelming.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_041.jpg	Who even knows where all those digital files are? There are tens of thousands of images in your iCloud. And more in your Google and Facebook accounts. And your dusty old hard drives. And even those old photo CDs you got from Walgreens a million years ago.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_042.jpg	But you've got to start somewhere,and now is the time. Plan to download photo files from wherever they might be stashed. Set up a system一such as a spreadsheet or sticky notes-to keep track of all the places you've checked, advises David Shor, co-founder of Tidy Haus,a home- and digital-organization company based in Fort Worth. Texas.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_043.jpg	‘You want to have a little bit of project management in place so you don't mess up," he says.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_044.jpg	Here's a checklist for making sure you don't miss anything.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_045.jpg	Search your Mac or PC
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_046.jpg	Think all your photos are stored in that folder neatly labeled Pictures? Think again. Odds are high that images are lurking in a Downloads folder or other random corners of your laptop.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_047.jpg	On a Mac, you can search by kind and select “image"or even specific image file types, including JPEG, TIFF,GIF, PNG, BMP and HEIC. (You can also search for "movie" files.) On Windows,you can search inside the taskbar,and can narrow down your query by specific file types.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_048.jpg	Download from Google
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_049.jpg	If you use the Google Photos app on your phone or computer, it's easy to download the stored files using Google's Takeout tool.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_050.jpg	On your computer,open a Google webpage and click your picture in the top right corner. Choose “Manage Your Google Account," then select "Data & privacy." Choose "Download your data,"select what items you want to receive and how you want to receive them (such as a download link). You'll get a zip file you can then open and sift through.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_051.jpg	If you select Google Drive in the tool,you'll also download all other files you saved there. It might be too much.If you think you have images hiding in Google Drive,skip Takeout and just look around by filtering your search to"Photos & images."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_052.jpg	Collect iCloud photos
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_053.jpg	If you have an iPhone, you almost certainly have photos saved in iCloud. You can see and download them by going to iCloud.com/photos and signing in with your Apple ID.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_054.jpg	▲ Photos are often forgotten on Flickr, Dropbox and other accounts. Make a list to check for them.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_055.jpg	You can also find them in the Photos app on your Mac, if you have iCloud photo sharing turned on.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_056.jpg	Check Facebook
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_057.jpg	Many of us have had Facebook accounts for over a decade and probably shared photos from old cameras we no longer own.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_058.jpg	Navigate to Meta's Accounts Center to download your photos. Choose“Your information and permissions" from the left toolbar and then select “Download your information." Choose which account you want to download (for instance, Facebook and/or Instagram),then decide how much data you want一 everything available or specific types of information.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_059.jpg	Photos isn't a download category of its own. You have to select Posts to get images, then choose the time frame and media quality you want to download.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_060.jpg	Look at old accounts
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_061.jpg	Did you ever keep photos in Flickr, Shutterfly, Dropbox ora similar storage service? If you haven't logged in lately, those photos may be purged, so log in and check.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_062.jpg	In the case of Shutterfly, if you don't make a purchase every 18 months, your photos will be archived. You can still see them and
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_063.jpg	buy prints, but you can't share or download photos until you make a purchase. Shutterfly-owned Snapfish has the same policy.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_064.jpg	Flickr limits free accounts to 1,000 items and 50 nonpublic images. If you uploaded more than that back when Flickr was more widely used and had higher free-storage limits, the excess images are probably gone.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_065.jpg	Hunt through old drives and CDs
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_066.jpg	You probably have an old hard drive, flash drive or stack of CDs and DVDs somewhere with photos from your wedding, the birth of your first child or the birthday of a long-qone loved one.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_067.jpg	Most laptops today don't have CD drives-or even the older USB-A ports. You might need to invest in a dongle adapter and an external CD/ DVD drive. Luckily, neither of those will break the bank.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_068.jpg	In some cases,you might find a dead hard drive or even an old phone or tablet that you can't turn on but you suspect might contain some precious memories. If irreplaceable images are on the line, you may want to visit a tech repair shop.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_069.jpg	Once you have all your digital photos in one place, backing them up to an external drive or cataloging them in your Photos app will be a lot easier. Then you can start worrying about all the photo prints you have stuffed in boxes in the attic.

test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_071.jpg	ISTOCK
