test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_001.jpg	Judge Bars Release of Report on Trump
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_002.jpg	A separate bid to delay his coming criminal sentencing in New York isdenied
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_003.jpg	BY C. RYAN BARBER AND CORINNE RAMEY
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_004.jpg	A federal judge on Tuesday barred the Justice Department from releasing special counsel Jack Smith's report on his investigation into Donald Trump, while a New York appeals judge denied the president-elect's separate bid to delay his coming criminal sentencing for covering up hush money paid to an adult-film star.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_005.jpg	In the Smith case, U.S. District Judge Aileen Cannon in Florida issued an order preventing the Justice Department from “releasing, sharing, or transmitting ”the special counsel's final report or otherwise distributing information or conclusions from the document while an appeals court considers the matter.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_006.jpg	Cannon, a Trump appointee, previously tossed Smith's prosecution of the former president on allegations that
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_007.jpg	F.SD/AFP/GETTY IMAGES
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_008.jpg	JACQUELYN MARTIN/ASSOCIATED PRESS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_009.jpg	Judge Aileen Cannon issued an order preventing the Justice Department from 'releasing, sharing, or transmitting' special counsel Jack Smith's final report on his Trump investigation.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_010.jpg	he mishandled classified documents after he left office.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_011.jpg	Smith had been appealing that ruling and separately prosecuting Trump in Washington on allegations that he unlawfully interfered with the 2020 election.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_012.jpg	The special counsel dropped both efforts after Trump won in November, in light of a Justice Department policy against prosecuting a sitting president.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_013.jpg	In New York, Justice Ellen
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_014.jpg	Gesmer held a hearing Tuesday on whether Trump's hush-money sentencing, set for Friday, could go forward.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_015.jpg	Shortly after the hearing concluded, she issued a one-sentence order rejecting Trump's request to stop the proceedings on the grounds of presidential immunity.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_016.jpg	“Do you have any support for the notion that presidential immunity extends to presidential elects?" Gesmer asked Trump lawyer Todd Blanche
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_017.jpg	during the hearing.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_018.jpg	“There has never been a case like this before, so no," Blanche replied.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_019.jpg	Steven Wu, a lawyer for the Manhattan district attorney's office, called Trump's arguments baseless.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_020.jpg	“The theory of president-elect immunity is inconsistent with the idea that there is one president at a time,” he told the judge.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_021.jpg	The proceedings come as courts and prosecutors are
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_022.jpg	scrambling to wrap up their unprecedented cases against Trump before Inauguration Day.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_023.jpg	In the federal matters, Smith was finalizing a report on his investigation, which he is required to submit to Attorney General Merrick Garland under Justice Department regulations. And Garland had previously indicated that he would make it public.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_024.jpg	Trump and his co-defendants in the documents case, Walt Nauta and Carlos De Oliveira, have been seeking to block the report's release. Trump's lawyers in a Monday letter to Garland called the report a political stunt.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_025.jpg	They also called for Garland to remove Smith as special counsel. Among the letter's signatories were lawyers Trump has picked for top Justice Department posts, including Blanche, his choice for deputy attorney general.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_026.jpg	"Releasing Smith's report is obviously not in the public interest–particularly in light of President Trump's commanding victory in the election and the sensitive nature of the on-going transition process," Trump's lawyers wrote.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_027.jpg	A Justice Department spokeswoman declined to comment.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_028.jpg	Smith's team, in a court filing Tuesday, said it was working to finalize a two-volume report and that, if Garland released it, that wouldn't happen before Friday. In a court filing later on Tuesday, Smith said his office would file a more detailed response in the 11th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_029.jpg	In the New York case, a jury last year convicted Trump of 34 felonies for covering up hush money paid to a porn star on the eve of the 2016 election.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_030.jpg	In a surprise ruling last week, Justice Juan Merchan, who presided over the trial, said he would sentence Trump before the start of his second term, but made clear that jail time wasn't a possibility.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_031.jpg	Merchan on Monday declined to postpone the sentencing, prompting Trump's lawyers to ask a state appeals court to intervene.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_032.jpg	It wasn't immediately clear what legal options Trump may have remaining to try to head off Friday's proceedings.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_033.jpg	New Threat On Panama, Greenland
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_034.jpg	bid to take over Greenland. He also questioned whether Denmark has any legal right to control the territory.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_035.jpg	“People really don't even know if Denmark has any legal right to it, but if they do, they should give it up because we need it for national security,” Trump, a Republican, said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_036.jpg	Representatives for Denmark's foreign ministry and Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen didn't respond to requests for comment. Frederiksen said in a Danish television interview on Tuesday that Greenland isn't for sale. “Greenland belongs to the people of Greenland. Our future and fight for independence is our business," Múte Egede, Greenland's prime minister, said in a statement.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_037.jpg	Greenland is an autonomous Danish territory with self-government and its own parliament. Denmark, a U.S. ally and member of the North Atlantic Treaty Organization, maintains some control over the island, including its foreign policy. NATO representatives didn't respond to reauests for comment.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_038.jpg	The president-elect said he would consider using economic coercion to persuade Canada to become part of the U.S., but he
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_039.jpg	ruled out using military force. “There isn't a snowball's chance in hell that Canada would become part of the United States,” Trudeau wrote on X on Tuesday afternoon.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_040.jpg	“The sovereignty of our canal is not negotiable,” said Javier Martinez-Acha，Panama's foreign minister. “It is part of our history of struggle and an irreversible conquest."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_041.jpg	Trump has accused Panama of charging U.S. shipping and naval vessels high fees for passage through the canal.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_042.jpg	The canal, through which about $4\%$ of global trade passes, is crucial for the global economy and U.S.consumers. Control of the canal was gradu-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_043.jpg	ally given back to Panama as a result of a 1977 treaty signed by then-President Jimmy Carter. Trump pointed to the canal as the reason for Carter's loss in the 1980 presidential election.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_044.jpg	Trump also said his administration would change the name of the Gulf of Mexico to the Gulf of America. “What a beautiful name, and it's appropriate,” he said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_045.jpg	Ahead of Trump's news conference, Trump's eldest son, Donald Trump Jr., arrived in Greenland–in a Trump-branded plane. The elder Trump wrote in a social-media post, “This is a deal that must happen. MAGA. MAKE GREENLAND GREAT AGAIN!"
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_046.jpg	'People really don't even know if Denmark has any legal right to it,' Trump said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_047.jpg	The president-elect called into a lunchtime gathering of his son and others in Greenland, saying over a speaker phone，“It's a very special place. It needs security for itself, but it also needs security very much for the world."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_048.jpg	The younger Trump arrived on Tuesday in Nuuk, Greenland's capital, with an entourage that included conservative activist Charlie Kirk; James Blair, political director of the 2024 campaign and incoming senior White House adviser; and Sergio Gor, who will oversee the White House Office of Presidential Personnel.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_049.jpg	Trump's national-security team has had preliminary discussions about
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_050.jpg	a process by which Trump would direct the State Department to undertake negotiations with the governments of Denmark and Greenland, according to people close to the
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_051.jpg	transition. That could potentially happen through a Compacts of Free Association agreement–a financial assistance pact similar to what the U.S. currently has with several Pacific Island nations.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_052.jpg	Trump's advisers hope to launch potential negotiations once the president-elect takes office, the people said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_053.jpg	But the Trump administration is likely to pursue a negotiation that is more nuanced
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_054.jpg	EMIL STACH/RITZAU SCANPIX/AP
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_055.jpg	Donald Trump Jr. arrived in Nuuk, Greenland, on Tuesday ahead of his father's news conference.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_056.jpg	than a real-estate purchase or sweeping annexation, according to the people close to the transition.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_057.jpg	During his first term, Trump expressed varying levels of interest in purchasing Greenland after hearing from his aides about its abundant resources and geopolitical importance.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_058.jpg	When The Wall Street Journal first reported of Trump's interest in the purchase, Greenland's Ministry of Foreign Affairs said that the island wasn't on the market.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_059.jpg	Trump's Tuesday news conference touched on several other topics. On the negotiations to release hostages
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_060.jpg	seized in Israel by Hamas militants in Gaza, Trump said, “If they're not back by the time I get into office, all hell will break out in the Middle East, and it will not be good for Hamas, and it will not be good, frankly, for anyone.” Trump has appointed Steve Witkoff, a realestate executive and close ally, as his envoy to the Middle East. On Wednesday, Witkoff is expected to join talks in Doha, Qatar, for a cease-fire in Gaza in exchange for the releases of hostages held by Hamas.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_061.jpg	Four years after the Jan. 6, 2021, attack on the U.S. Capitol, the president-elect said he is looking at potential pardons
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_062.jpg	for participants charged in the riot, including those charged with violent offenses. “People that were doing some bad things weren't prosecuted, and people that didn't even walk into the building are in jail right now,” he said. “So we'll be looking at the whole thing, but I'll be making major pardons."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_063.jpg	The president-elect said he is open to pushing his legislative agenda through Congress with either a single large bill or two separate bills, weighing in on an issue that has been a point of debate for lawmakers on Capitol Hill. Trump is expected to visit Capitol Hill on Wednesday.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_064.jpg	Debt-Ceiling Battle This Time Around Offers New Twists
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_065.jpg	BY RICHARD RUBIN
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_066.jpg	U.S. debt held by the public
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_067.jpg	Source: Treasury Department
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_068.jpg	ALLISON ROBBERT/BLOOMBERG NEWS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_069.jpg	Rep. Jodey Arrington (R., Texas) said Republicans should raise the debt limit as part of their broader fiscal legislation.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_070.jpg	How the Debt Limit Works
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_071.jpg	The debt limit is a weird creature. Congress created the limit before World War II to make debt management smoother while setting a cap on overall red ink to keep the legislature involved. But now the debt limit is a source of friction; failing to raise it threatens a global financial crisis.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_072.jpg	Raising or freezing the debt limit doesn't change the U.S.'s fiscal trajectory.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_073.jpg	Instead, raising the limit just lets the Treasury Department borrow money to implement Congress's prior tax and spending decisions.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_074.jpg	Without a debt-limit increase, the U.S. would be unable to pay all its bills, at a minimum risking missed payments to federal workers or Medicare providers and potentially triggering damaging disruptions to the Treasury market.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_075.jpg	WASHINGTON – Here we go again.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_076.jpg	The U.S. government is hitting the debt limit, triggering a familiar cycle of brinkmanship, bipartisan negotiation and possible crisis. This round, however, has a few twists.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_077.jpg	Republicans have the power to raise the debt ceiling by themselves, and they will try to do that in their tax, border, energy and spending-cuts legislation that would wrap many of President-elect Donald Trump's priorities into one bill.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_078.jpg	"With all that we're doing to restore fiscal health and prosperity, we ought to put the debt ceiling in there as well, because those are the fiscal reforms that I think should be required to do it," said Rep. Jodey Arrington (R., Texas), chairman of the House Budget Committee.“And Trump's committed to it."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_079.jpg	If Republicans' efforts collapse, they will need to turn to Democrats to avert a potential government default around the middle of the year, possibly setting the stage for more deadline drama. While many Democrats have made clear they won't sign off on a GOP plan that kicks the debt ceiling to the next Congress or next president, they have sig-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_080.jpg	naled support for effectively getting rid of the debt-ceiling threat altogether.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_081.jpg	Trump's recent call for a long-term or permanent debt-ceiling increase – which fell short among Republicans – could become the basis for an agreement with Democrats, said Rep. Brendan Boyle (D., Pa.). Boyle said he is open to working with Trump on a bill that would permanently alter the debt limit, aiming to prevent Republicans from wielding it against a future Democratic president like they did in 2011 and 2023.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_082.jpg	"Both in our political interest and for the good of the country, this is a great opportunity for Democrats to once and for all permanently resolve this issue so it can never again be used as a weapon," said Boyle, the top Democrat on the Budget Committee, who has introduced legislation that would let the administration raise the debt limit unless Congress objects with veto-proof majorities.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_083.jpg	Trump, he said, “may actually be someone that Democrats can work with in order to achieve our long-term goals."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_084.jpg	House Speaker Mike Johnson (R., La.) has said he wants to include a debt-limit increase in the "one big beautiful bill" that Republicans will
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_085.jpg	try to move through Congress using a procedure known as budget reconciliation, which averts the 60-vote Senate filibuster threshold. Republicans have a narrow 219-215 majority in the House, which is expected to temporarily shrink to 217-215 when two members join the administration.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_086.jpg	Johnson reiterated Tuesday that Republicans want to handle the debt ceiling without relying on Democrats, but he acknowledged there was "abroad range of opinion” about the borrowing limit among GOP colleagues.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_087.jpg	For decades, Congress periodically raised the limit, often tying it to budget agreements with few threats of a breach. Modern debt-limit politics started in 2011, when the Republican House insisted that any debt-ceiling increase be paired with spending cuts.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_088.jpg	“They've certainly become more frequent, more often down to the wire and more political in nature,” said Shai
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_089.jpg	Akabas, who tracks debt-limit debates for the Bipartisan Policy Center. “It has not proved to be an effective tool to force Congress to enact debt-reduction policies."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_090.jpg	That 2011 negotiation with then-President Barack Obama brought the U.S. to the edge of default and led to a down-graded credit rating. House
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_091.jpg	Republicans repeated that strategy with President Biden in 2023. Both times, Republicans won concessions and Democrats insisted they would never again negotiate on the debt ceiling.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_092.jpg	That 2023 deal suspended the limit until this month. Potential missed payments won't happen until later because the
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_093.jpg	Treasury Department can use tax revenue and extraordinary measures–moving money around government accounts–to stave off the so-called X date for a few more months.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_094.jpg	Trump, seeing the ceiling on the horizon, asked Congress to include a debt-limit increase in an end-of-year spending bill in late 2024, a month before his inauguration. The vast majority of House Republicans voted for a two-year debt-limit suspension, even without spending cuts attached.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_095.jpg	But enough House Republicans objected–joined by Democrats–to prevent that bill from passing, and the ultimate spending bill lacked a debt-limit increase. Trump asked Congress again to raise or eliminate the debt ceiling before he takes office Jan. 20, but lawmakers haven't acted.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_096.jpg	“All I want to see is no default because nobody knows what would happen if there is a default,” Trump said Tuesday. “It could be 1929 or it could be nothing.”
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_097.jpg	Debt-limit votes can be politically difficult, because lawmakers don't like to be seen as enabling higher debt and deficits. The cuts demanded by some Republicans along with a debt-limit increase might prove unacceptable to other Republicans.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_098.jpg	“When your House majority is one seat, it's just going to be really, really difficult to get a debt limit over the finish line simply with Republican votes,” said Paul Winfree, who was a Trump aide in the first term and is now president of the Economic Policy Innovation Center.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_099.jpg	* * * * *
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_100.jpg	Wednesday, January 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_101.jpg	P W L C 10 11 12 H T G K R A M 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8 9 0 I X X
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_102.jpg	THE WALL STREET JOURNAL.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_004_line_104.jpg	Continued from Page One

test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_001.jpg	These indexes cite notable references to most parent companies and businesspeople in today's edition. Articles on regional page inserts aren't cited in these indexes.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_002.jpg	INDEX TOPEOPLE
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_003.jpg	BUSINESS & FINANCE
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_004.jpg	GUERIN CHARLES/ABACA/ZUMA PRESS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_005.jpg	Heavy-duty trucks must pay \$21.60 each time they enter Manhattan south of 60th Street between 5 a.m. and 9 p.m. on weekdays.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_006.jpg	Congestion Fee Hits Truckers
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_007.jpg	Continued from page B1
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_008.jpg	Miller said trucking companies will absorb some of the extra costs from the tolls, but they will have to pass on a portion of the fees to customers such as restaurants and bars that already operate on thin margins.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_009.jpg	“I have heard a lot of concern about losing customers because of increased rates,” he said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_010.jpg	Freight companies and their customers say the charges will pile on costs for cargo that can't reach its destination any other way.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_011.jpg	“A restaurant’s fish order is not taking the subway from the Bronx to Midtown Manhattan,” said Andrew Rigie, executive director of New York City Hospitality Alliance, which represents restaurants, bars and nightclubs.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_012.jpg	“That truck has to come into the zone and our suppliers have told us this is going to increase their cost of doing business,” Rigie said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_013.jpg	About 120,000 trucks and commercial vehicles enter the city each day, according to the
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_014.jpg	city transportation department.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_015.jpg	John Luciani, an executive at A. Duie Pyle, a West Chester, Pa.-based trucking company focused on the Northeast that consolidates shipments from multiple customers in a single trailer, says New York City is already the most difficult and expensive city it operates in.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_016.jpg	Luciani said that because of traffic, A. Duie Pyle's drivers there average eight deliveries and pickups a day, compared with an average of 23 daily deliveries and pickups across the wider region. He said average toll costs per delivery are almost three times higher in the city than regionwide.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_017.jpg	“The cost of doing business
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_018.jpg	in NYC is significant,” he said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_019.jpg	City and state transportation officials say truckers can reduce costs by delivering overnight. The congestion toll for heavy-duty trucks drops to \$5.40 during off-peak hours.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_020.jpg	Carriers say they already have plenty of incentives to deliver overnight.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_021.jpg	Traffic is lighter and curb space is available. But they say many customers can't accept overnight deliveries because they don't have over-night staff or a secure location for unattended deliveries.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_022.jpg	“It’s just another tax and another cost of doing business in the Big Apple,” Luciani said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_023.jpg	Law Firms Prepare for Trump
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_024.jpg	Continued from page B1 trade adviser.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_025.jpg	Top firms across the U.S. are also bracing for major changes in the antitrust landscape. President Biden appointed officials, including Federal Trade Commission Chair Lina Khan, who sought to increase enforcement to levels not seen in decades. That created a lucrative environment for antitrust lawyers as companies struggled to secure government clearance for a range of proposed mergers and acquisitions.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_026.jpg	Trump's new administration likely means a more hands-off approach, which in some ways could reduce the windfall for antitrust litigators and counselors. But the more predictable and favorable regulatory climate, along with economic factors, could drive new corporate dealmaking and provide a healthy stream of legal revenue.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_027.jpg	Ropes & Gray antitrust partner Jonathan Klarfeld said he expects his practice area to return to a normal baseline after years of drawn-out enforcement built on progressive and untested legal theories.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_028.jpg	"Some of the excesses of recent enforcement are likely to be pared back," Klarfeld said, but added, "There is more than enough work to occupy our time."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_029.jpg	The charged political environment is spurring specialty practice areas designed to help clients navigate it.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_030.jpg	Labor-law specialist firm Seyfarth Shaw created a cultural-flashpoints task force in 2023 to help clients navigate workplace challenges and respond to rising pressure for companies to take a stance on
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_031.jpg	MICHAEL NAGLE/BLOOMBERG NEWS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_032.jpg	Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said his company wouldn't work with any firm that hires certain Biden-era regulators.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_033.jpg	social issues, said Lorie Almon, the firm's chair and managing partner.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_034.jpg	The firm is now marketing the practice anew after the divisiveness of the 2024 election and a backlash against some policies embraced by progressives. The group helps employers "lower the temperature" around hot-button social and political issues, Almon said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_035.jpg	Law firm Gibson Dunn has built a specialty out of counseling companies that have implemented policies and programs designed to promote diversity and inclu-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_036.jpg	sion in the workplace. Such initiatives have been under increasing fire since the Supreme Court in 2023 ended affirmative action in higher education. Trump's election turns up the heat even more because he has campaigned against such initiatives.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_037.jpg	"We're definitely seeing and anticipating increased demand and opportunities for

test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_039.jpg	us to help our clients navigate this in a thoughtful, practical and legally defensible way," said firm partner Jason Schwartz.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_040.jpg	Firms themselves also face political challenges. One early example came in December, when Coinbase CEO Brian Armstrong said his company wouldn't work with anyone who hires Bidenera officials who were part
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_041.jpg	of the administration's aggressive enforcement efforts against crypto companies. And he singled out law firm Milbank by name for hiring the Securities and
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_042.jpg	Exchange Commission’s recent enforcement director.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_043.jpg	"Let your law firms know that hiring these folks means losing you as a client," Armstrong wrote on X.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_044.jpg	Milbank didn't respond to a request for comment.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_045.jpg	More broadly, law firms are beginning to be more hesitant about taking public stances that could anger
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_046.jpg	Trump and his allies, after years in which firms weren't shy about speaking out on issues including abortion rights, racial justice and the war in the Middle East.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_047.jpg	"They are more sensitive to this now than they have been in the past," said law firm consultant Peter Zeughauser. "They are more careful."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_048.jpg	Four years ago, some firms sought to distance themselves from Trump after he sought to overturn Joe Biden's 2020 electoral victory. The positioning across the industry is far different now, and former Trump officials have become highly prized assets.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_049.jpg	"Firms are peacocking their Trump feathers in a way we haven’t seen in recent memory," said Dan Binstock, a legal recruiter based in Washington.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_050.jpg	Among the firms eager to champion its D.C. ties is Akin, which has been pitching itself as particularly well placed for the coming administration. Its marketing tools include a new biweekly policy publication on how clients can navigate the postelection landscape.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_051.jpg	The firm's roster of D.C. insiders includes former Rep. Kevin Brady, who as Republican chair of the House Ways and Means Committee during the first Trump administration helped usher in a sweeping package of tax cuts—many of which are set to expire at the end of this year.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_052.jpg	"We have a pulse on all things Washington," said Abid Qureshi, Akin's co-chair-elect.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_053.jpg	Even before the election, top firms were engaged in a poaching war for star partners with expertise that could bring in new portfolios of business. Trump's victory— and the policy upheavals that come with it—only adds to that pressure.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_054.jpg	"If law firms haven’t invested in the regulatory space, it may be too late to recruit top talent," said Yvette Ostolaza, chair of Sidley Austin’s management committee.

Most of that has come from sales to businesses.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_062.jpg	OpenAI told investors when closing its October round that it expected to generate \$3.7 billion in revenue last year, in large part from sales of the premium version of ChatGPT to consumers.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_063.jpg	GFL Environmental to Sell Services Business In \$5.6 Billion Deal
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_064.jpg	BY DENNY JACOB
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_065.jpg	GFL Environmental has reached an agreement to sell its environmental services business in a deal valued at 8 billion Canadian dollars, equivalent to about \$5.59 billion, though it would retain an equity interest.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_066.jpg	The Canadian company, which transports and recycles waste, among other services, said it reached the agreement with funds managed by affiliates of Apollo and BC Partners. It would retain a $44\%$ equity interest in the business, while funds managed by affiliates of Apollo and BC each would hold a $28\%$ equity interest.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_067.jpg	GFL said its board, when excluding interested directors who have recused themselves, has unanimously approved the transaction. The deal is ex-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_068.jpg	pected to close in the first quarter.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_069.jpg	GFL expects cash proceeds from the deal to be around C\$6.2 billion. It intends to use up to C\$3.75 billion of the net proceeds to repay debt, leaving up to C\$2.25 billion for share repurchases. The balance will go to transaction fees and general corporate purposes.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_070.jpg	Chief Executive Patrick Dovigi said a deleveraged balance sheet will provide GFL with more financial flexibility to deploy capital into organic growth initiatives and solid waste M&A and allow for a greater return of capital to shareholders.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_071.jpg	Dovigi said the company will maintain an option to repurchase the environmental services business within five years of closing.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_072.jpg	RICHARD B.LEVINE/LEVINE ROBERTS/NEWSCOM/ZUMA PRESS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_073.jpg	The latest proposal from Cintas, a maker of workplace products, was initially given to UniFirst's board in November.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_074.jpg	Cintas Bids For Rival UniFirst
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_075.jpg	engage with UniFirst, including by stating its willingness to consider increasing its offer price, a subsequent letter says.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_076.jpg	UniFirst has told Cintas that its board unanimously concluded that a deal wasn't in the best interest of the company and its stakeholders, according to correspondence in December and November.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_077.jpg	In October, UniFirst had said in a news release that it was excited about the investments it was making and believed its business had "significant long-term potential."
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_078.jpg	For Cintas, a deal would allow the combined companies to serve more customers and make greater use of its

test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_080.jpg	Cintas also thinks investors would like the deal: $79\%$ of UniFirst common shares are held by investors who also own Cintas's stock, according to a Cintas analysis included in the letters delivered to UniFirst's board.

test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_083.jpg	Monitor the Market
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_084.jpg	EXPLORE WSJ.com/buildwatchlist
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_085.jpg	THE WALL STREET JOURNAL.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_086.jpg	Wednesday, January 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_087.jpg	**Scan this code** for a video on conservative activist Robby Starbuck, who claims success for pushing public companies to change or abandon DEI and LGBTQ policies. WSJ looks at how he and his team do their work.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_089.jpg	$\copyright$ 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc. All rights reserved. TDJ0231
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_090.jpg	Continued from page B1
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_018_line_091.jpg	Continued from page B1
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_000.jpg	A Brief 10 Year History of Cartooning
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_001.jpg	An extinction- level reversal for truth-seeking
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_002.jpg	Chris Stokel-Walker
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_003.jpg	Social media has always acted as something of a funhouse mirror to society as a whole. The algorithms and amplifications of an always online existence have helped accentuate the worst parts of our lives, while tucking in and hiding the best. It's part of why we're so polarised today.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_004.jpg	Which is what makes a declaration by one titan of big tech this week so worrying. Less than two weeks before Donald Trump returns to the White House, Meta, the parent company of Facebook, WhatsApp, Instagram and Threads, has made major changes to content moderation, and in doing so appears to align itself with the views of the incoming president.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_005.jpg	In a bizarre video message posted to his personal
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_006.jpg	Facebook page yesterday, Meta's chief executive officer, Mark Zuckerberg, announced that the platform is getting rid of its factcheckers. In their place? Mob rule.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_007.jpg	Zuckerberg has said that the platform, which has more than 3 billion people worldwide logging on to its apps every day, will be adopting an Elon Musk-style community notes format for policing what is and isn't acceptable speech on its platforms. Starting in the US, the company will be dramatically shifting the Overton window towards whoever can shout the loudest.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_008.jpg	The Meta CEO all but admitted that the move was politically motivated. “It’s time to get back to our roots around free expression,” he said, confessing that “restrictions on topics like immigration and gender [...] are out of touch with mainstream discourse”. He admitted to past “censorship mistakes” - here, probably meaning the past four years of tamping down political speech while a Democratic president was in office - and said he would “work with President Trump to push back against foreign governments going after American companies to censor more”.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_009.jpg	The most dog-whistle comment was a throwaway remark that Meta would be moving what remained of its trust and safety and content moderation teams out of liberal California, and its US content moderation would now be based in staunchly Republican Texas.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_010.jpg	To be completely clear: all businesspeople make shrewd moves to accommodate the changing political weather. But few people's decisions matter more than Zuckerberg's. He has found himself, in the past 21 years, a central part of western society. Where Meta goes, the world - both online and offline - follows. And Meta has just decided to take a drastic, dramatic handbrake turn to the right.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_011.jpg	Don't believe me - believe the watchdogs. “Meta's announcement today is a retreat from any sane and
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_012.jpg	safe approach to content moderation,” said the Real Facebook Oversight Board, an independent, self-appointed arbiter of Meta’s moves.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_013.jpg	Outrage and lies can spread easily on social media, and have only been kept partly in check by platforms' ability to intervene when things get out of hand. Social networks have always struggled to moderate speech. Those platforms haven't helped themselves with chronic underinvestment in favour of growing their business at all costs. They have long said effective moderation is an unsolvable issue of scale, but it's a problem they created with an untrammelled pursuit of scale at all costs.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_014.jpg	Certainly, policing online discourse is difficult, and certainly, content moderation at the level firms like Meta have been trying to operate has not worked. But forswearing it entirely in favour of community notes is not the answer. Suggesting that it is a rational, evidence-based decision hides the reality: this is an expedient move for a man who this week saw the departure of a self-described “radical” centrist, Nick Clegg, as his global policy chief in favour of a Republican-leaning one. And who also appointed Dana White, the Ultimate Fighting Championship CEO and close Trump ally, to Meta’s board.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_015.jpg	In many ways, you can't blame Zuckerberg for bending the knee to Donald Trump. The problem is his decision has huge ramifications. This is an extinction-level event for the idea of objective truth on social media - an organism that was already on life support, but was clinging on in part because Meta was willing to fund independent factchecking organisations in order to try to maintain some element of truthfulness, free from political bias. Night is day. Up is down. Meta is X. Mark Zuckerberg is Elon Musk. Buckle up for a turbulent, vitriolic and fact-free four years online.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_016.jpg	The Guardian Wednesday 8 January 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_018.jpg	Opinion
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_048_line_019.jpg	* Chris Stokel-Walker is the author of TikTok Boom: The Inside Story of the World's Favourite App
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_000.jpg	BUSINESS & FINANCE
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_001.jpg	Blackstone Takes Stake in Audit Firm
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_002.jpg	BY MARK MAURER
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_003.jpg	New Mountain Capital is selling its stake in accounting firm Citrin Cooperman to an investor group led by Blackstone , the first flip of an audit firm by a private - equity investor .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_004.jpg	The deal , which would value Citrin Cooperman at more than \$2 billion , is the latest in a series of stakes that private - equity firms are taking in midsize audit and consulting firms in the U . S . Accounting firms are hungry for more money to pay for technology and talent . Many private - equity funds consider account - ing firms to be low - risk investments that could net a sizable return based in part on recurring revenue streams .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_005.jpg	The Blackstone - led group would take a more than two - thirds ownership stake in Citrin , and Blackstone itself holds an interest in the range of $40\%$ to $45\%$ in the group , people familiar with the transaction said .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_006.jpg	Citrin Cooperman , a New York - based accounting firm , in October 2021 sold a majority stake to New Mountain , which has about \$55 billion in assets under management .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_007.jpg	Citrin Cooperman has grown significantly in the roughly four years since the
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_008.jpg	sale . The firm reported \$351.8 million in revenue in 2021. Its 2024 revenue totaled about \$900 million, in part due to acquisitions of smaller firms, people familiar said.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_009.jpg	Blackstone,which manages about \$55 billion in assets, would be one of the largest private-equity firms to have a stake in an accounting firm. A group led by Hellman & Friedman,which managed \$120 billion in assets at the end of 2023,bought a stake in Baker Tilly in June. Blackstone participated in the debt on that deal, providing \$450 million of a \$1 billion financing package.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_010.jpg	The sale allows New Mountain to focus on boosting revenue at Grant Thornton ' s U . S . unit , in which it acquired a stake last May . New Mountain aims to merge Grant Thornton ' s nonaudit services in the U . S . and more than 10 other international units over the next five years , primarily in Europe and Asia , a person familiar with the plans said .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_011.jpg	Regulators say they are concerned about potential conflicts of interest from the accounting profession ' s growing ties to private equity , given that an auditor ' s objectivity is critical to the business of accounting firms .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_012.jpg	The Financial Times first reported on the deal .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_013.jpg	Sixth Street Strikes a Deal To Manage Insurer's Assets
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_014.jpg	Northwestern Mutual to take a small minority stake in investment firm
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_015.jpg	BY MIRIAM GOTTFRIED
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_016.jpg	Investment firm Sixth Street is forging a partnership with Northwestern Mutual to manage \$13 billion of the insurer ' s assets , executives from the companies said . The money will primarily be invested in asset - based finance , a hot area that encompasses a swath of consumer lending , including mortgages , credit-card loans and auto loans .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_017.jpg	Milwaukee - based North - western Mutual also will take a small minority stake in Sixth Street , bolstering the investment firm ' s balance sheet to help it fund growth . The stake is of a similar size to the roughly $10\%$ holding Sixth Street bought back from private - equity firm TPG last year , according to people familiar with the matter .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_018.jpg	Wall Street ' s big push into insurance got under way after Apollo Global Management began managing the assets of Athene , an insurance com -
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_019.jpg	pany it helped create in the aftermath of the 2008-2009 financial crisis. Other private-equity firms such as KKR, Blackstone and Brookfield have since bought insurers or struck deals to manage their assets.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_020.jpg	For many firms, private credit has largely been synonymous with lending to midsize companies,often to finance their buyouts by a private-eq-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_021.jpg	uity firm.As managers increasingly take in cash from in- surance companies，they are moving deeper into strategies aimed at providing better returns than corporate bonds while remaining rela-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_022.jpg	tively low-risk.Insurers invest customers' premiums with the goal of earning more than they must pay out to policy- holders.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_023.jpg	Unlike most of the deals done by its publicly traded rivals, Sixth Street's partnership with Northwestern Mutual will happen in phases,with money being invested as the
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_024.jpg	\$13B
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_025.jpg	Amount of Northwestern Mutual's assets involved in the deal
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_026.jpg	best opportunities arise.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_027.jpg	“If someone gave me \$13 billion and said go invest it tomorrow, I might be able to do it, but I probably wouldn’t be able to do it in the best risk- adjusted-return way,” said Michael Muscolino, a Sixth Street co-founder who leads its financial-services efforts.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_028.jpg	Based in San Francisco, Sixth Street will manage more than \$100 billion in assets af-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_029.jpg	ter the North- western Mutual commitment. The firm was founded in 2009 by 10 partners, many of whom had previously worked together under Chief Executive Alan Waxman at Goldman
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_030.jpg	Sachs. The partners were initially backed by TPG but separated from it in 2020.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_031.jpg	Sixth Street built a team of 40 people focused on asset-based finance, hiring Michael Dryden from Credit Suisse in 2022 to lead its expansion. The firm had historically invested in asset-based finance in a more opportunistic way,
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_032.jpg	snapping up a portfolio of mortgages in the aftermath of the financial crisis , for example . Now it is investing more consistently as banks have scaled back in response to regulatory pressure .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_033.jpg	Dryden ' s group last month announced a deal to buy as much as \$4 billion of loans from buy - now - pay - later platform Affirm .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_034.jpg	Sixth Street has a separate team of 25 people solely focused on insurance . It owns and advises on the investments for Talcott Financial Group , an annuities company with \$127 billion in assets .
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_035.jpg	Northwestern Mutual is owned by its policyholders. The company, which was founded more than 165 years ago, provides life, disability-income and long-term-care insurance, annuities, and brokerage and wealth advisory. It manages the bulk of its $627 billion in assets internally and has invested in private markets for decades.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_036.jpg	But Northwestern Mutual didn't have the capability of finding and structuring asset-based finance deals, a labor-intensive process, according to Jeb Bentley, its chief investment officer.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_037.jpg	CFTC Chairman to Step Down When Trump Takes Office
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_038.jpg	BY MENGQI SUN
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_039.jpg	Commodity Futures Trading Commission Chairman Rostin Behnam said he would step down from his position on Jan. 20, as the country's top derivatives markets regulator prepares for a second Trump term.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_040.jpg	Behnam, a Democrat,has served as the CFTC's chairman since 2022. He previously served as a commissioner be-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_041.jpg	tween 2017 and 2021 and was named acting chairman in 2021 after his predecessor, Heath Tarbert, resigned when President Biden took office.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_042.jpg	Behnam said that under his leadership, the CFTC shifted its focus from implementing the 2010 Dodd-Frank Act to adapting its oversight respon- sibilities to address the new issues brought on by evolving technology in the derivatives markets and to tackle regula-
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_043.jpg	tory gaps.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_044.jpg	Behnam has sought a leading role for the CFTC in regulating the cryptocurrency market, which has defied easy categorization within the fi-nancial regulatory system. That has put the derivatives regulator in a potential conflict with the Securities and Exchange Commission around jurisdictional power over the nascent industry.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_045.jpg	The CFTC has cracked
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_046.jpg	down on misconduct and fraud related to cryptocurrency over the past few years, suing some of the highest-profile companies in the industry. The regulator in August obtained \$12.7 billion in a judgment against now-bankrupt crypto exchange FTX and crypto trading firm Alameda Research. The CFTC also sued crypto exchange Binance for evading U.S.rules in 2023 and imposed a \$2.7 billion fine on
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_047.jpg	the company later that year. When a group of regulators announced the deal with Binance at the time, Behnam joined Attorney General Merrick Garland and Treasury Secretary Janet Yellen on the stage.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_048.jpg	Behnam said his last day at the agency will be Feb.7, adding that he would work closely with President-elect Donald Trump's team to ensure a smooth transition.
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_049.jpg	BRENDAN MCDERMID / REUTERS
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_050.jpg	The CFTC's Rostin Behnam.

test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_057.jpg	Wednesday, January 8, 2025
test/images/newspaper_The Wall Street Journal - 2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_021_line_058.jpg	THE WALL STREET JOURNAL.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_000.jpg	FILE/BARRY CHIN/ GLOBE STAFF
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_001.jpg	Caroline Doherty became Hingham's all-time top goal-scorer, boy or girl, on Dec.24.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_002.jpg	HIGH SCHOOL GIRLS' HOCKEY NOTEBOOK
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_003.jpg	Doherty's special path
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_004.jpg	Hingham captain shows MIAA way to college play
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_005.jpg	By Mike Puzzanghera
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_006.jpg	GLOBE CORRESPONDENT
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_007.jpg	Caroline Doherty is evidence that there's no set path to a dream.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_008.jpg	Hingham's star senior forward – committed to play at Holy Cross after a post-grad year – has done it all while playing for the town she grew up in. She's the school's all-time leading scorer, a two-time Patriot League MVP, and one of the state's most talented players.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_009.jpg	She takes immense pride in the career she's built at Hingham.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_010.jpg	“Personally, I don't think it matters where you go to school, as long as you put the work in," Doherty said. “I get to play with my sister [Brenna] and all the people I grew up playing with. I think the ‘public pride' part of it is honestly my favorite part.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_011.jpg	"I play in front of the little girls who all look up to all of us. That's probably the best part of it, too.”
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_012.jpg	Doherty leads a Harborwomen team, ranked No.1 in the Globe's Top 20, that has eyes on its first title since 2011. They're off to a 6-0-0 start, with Doherty scoring 14 goals.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_013.jpg	“She could probably go out here in some of these games and score 17 goals," third-year Hingham coach Courtney Turner said. “But she's looking for her teammates after the first one or two. I think that says more about her character than her scoring."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_014.jpg	Doherty burst onto the scene as a freshman, then exploded for 48 goals and 28 assists as a sophomore. After another 36 points a year ago and 17 to start the new season, she's at 190 for
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_015.jpg	her career. Of those, 126 are goals - the 122nd of which set the Hingham record on Christmas Eve, assisted by her sister.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_016.jpg	It's easy to see the talent Doherty possesses. She can take out defenders with her stick-handling and snipe a corner like clockwork.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_017.jpg	But it's her work ethic and drive to be better that have gotten her here. Monday afternoon, she takes the ice more than 30 minutes before Hingham's practice begins, getting extra work in with fellow captain Addy Garrity.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_018.jpg	“She puts in a lot of work behind the scenes that allows her to be a goal scorer,” Turner said. “She also just goes to the gym all the time. Her shot is a testament to how much work she puts in at the gym.”
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_019.jpg	With her talent, Doherty could have easily taken the prep school route. But in sticking with the Harborwomen, she displayed that talent and hard work – not the school on your
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_020.jpg	sweater – make a college career possible.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_021.jpg	“My first year here, we had the prep school discussion," Turner said. “She wanted to stay here. I'm very happy she did, and I know she'll have these friends for life."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_022.jpg	Doherty made a switch at the club level this year, moving to the Massachusetts Spitfires – ranked as one of the top programs not just in the state, but in the country. The move was all Doherty's doing – she reached out to coach Joe Hulbig to set up an opportunity, and even though the Spitfires had a full roster, she impressed enough to make the team. At their first tournament of the year over Labor Day weekend, Hulbig said every college coach he talked to wanted to know about her
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_023.jpg	Of the 19 players on the roster, she's the only one at an MIAA program. Despite this, she finished the fall season as the team's top scorer with nine goals.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_024.jpg	Globe Top 20
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_025.jpg	“She's a rare athlete that has a natural ability to keep focus and discipline,” Hulbig said. “She wants it, and that's what's been able to make her successful.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_026.jpg	“Someday, she's going to look back at this and say, 'I'm glad I finished it out at Hingham,' because it's a pretty special thing that she's doing."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_027.jpg	Doherty committed to Holy Cross in September and will join up with the Crusaders in fall 2026 to play for coach Katie Lachapelle at an up-and-coming program in Hockey East. She'll join Spitfires teammate Violet Carroll (Williston Northampton) in Worcester. (She's still undecided where she will skate next season.)
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_028.jpg	“I fell in love with the school itself, which I think is bigger than the hockey part,” Doherty said. “When the hockey part fell into place, it was just a perfect fit."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_029.jpg	That's all in the future – at the moment, the 5-foot-6-inch sniper is focused on enjoying time with her friends in her hometown.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_030.jpg	“I really just want to take in these last few months,”Doherty said. “Playing with my sister, all the seniors I won't get to play with again, and all these girls. I just want to have fun and enjoy it.”
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_031.jpg	Ice chips
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_032.jpg	■ After years of preaching a defense-first mentality, King Philip coach Ken Assad is finally seeing his vision come to fruition.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_033.jpg	Since dropping their season opener to Smithfield (R.I.), the Warriors have won five in a row, and they have given up one goal or less in four of the five victories.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_034.jpg	“For the last three years, I've been focusing on team defense, and it really hasn't clicked until this year,”Assad said. “These girls get what team defense is all about."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_035.jpg	King Philip's new identity is led by sophomore Calleigh Brady, junior Grace Leehan-Allen, and freshman Laney O'Shea on the blue line, and junior netminder Kiki Lynch, who has allowed just six goals through six games.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_036.jpg	After winning three consecutive games by one-goal margins, the Warriors secured their most impressive victory to date in a 3-0 win over No.20 Canton on Saturday.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_037.jpg	“It's tribute to all the backcheckers, defenders, goaltending, you name it, they're all just coming together and playing as one,” Assad said. “It's a different brand of hockey that they're putting out this year.”
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_038.jpg	■ Canton got off to a 3-0-1 start under first-year coach Marissa Massaro, prior to dropping games this week to King Phillip and Notre Dame (Hingham).
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_039.jpg	One key weapon for the Bulldogs is sophomore forward Lauren Raffetto – who, when
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_040.jpg	she is not playing top-line minutes for the girls' hockey team, is one of the state's best cross-country runners.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_041.jpg	Raffetto finished 13th in the MIAA Division 2 Meet of Champions race in November, and she also owns Canton's 5K school record time by 45 seconds.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_042.jpg	Raffetto scored a goal in Canton's 3-1 victory over No.12 Nauset on Thursday, and her cross-country skill-set has proven to be an asset.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_043.jpg	“Her endurance is incredible. She can play as many minutes as I possibly need," Massaro said. “I think at one point [against Nauset], she played three minutes straight and got off the ice and wasn't tired. She's skilled and she's aggressive in the best way possible, and she can go and go and never worry about running out of steam."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_025_line_044.jpg	Correspondent Matty Wasserman contributed to this story.
