test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_000.jpg	10 new restaurants to try in the new year
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_001.jpg	►RESTAURANTS
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_002.jpg	Continued from Page G1
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_003.jpg	pork belly bao buns and tacos, miso eggplant, and grilled steak with chimichurri sauce.Chef Jesus Preciado's menu draws from Asia and Latin America (including his native Colombia). On the cocktail list,find drinks like Beet the Heat (made with spicy pisco and beet syrup)and Amarillo Passion (in which tequila meets yuzu liqueur,passion fruit syrup,and the aji amarillo chile).
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_004.jpg	647 Tremont St.,South End, Boston, 617-286-2333，www.desnudacoci- na.com.Smaller plates $\$$6-$\$$18, larger plates $\$$18-$\$$32,cocktails $\$$14-$\$$17.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_006.jpg	The latest from Xenia Greek Hospitality,which brought us Bar Vlaha, Krasi,and more.This time,culinary director Brendan Pelley and team whisk diners to the Aegean (with a South End mind-set) via dishes like sea bream crudo with fermented squash broth;barley toasts with beets,pears,and vegan feta; black truffle spanakopita;and grilled whole fish.The wine list is devoted solely to selections from the Aegean and Eastern Mediterranean islands.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_007.jpg	370 Harrison Ave.,South End,Boston,617-514-0700, www.kaiasouthend.com.Dishes $\$$16-$\$$44.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_008.jpg	Masons Steakhouse
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_009.jpg	Here's what happens when a group of powerhouse South Shore culinary pros (including Jimmy Liang of JP Fuji Group and Kevin Long of Tosca) join forces.This swank Quincy steakhouse is located in a former Masonic temple and serves up lobster bisque,oysters Rockefeller,pappardelle bolognese,salmon with spiced cauliflower—and,of course,beautiful steaks.The also-beautiful,creative desserts are from pastry chef and Masons co-owner Robert Gon-zalez,who is behind Quincy's Niveaux Patisserie.In a landscape (increasingly) stocked with steakhouses,it is nice to see a new one open with local ownership.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_010.jpg	1170 Hancock St., Quincy,617-689-8818,www.masonssteakhouse.com.Appetizers $\$$14-$\$$32,entrees $\$$29-$\$$45, steaks $\$$52-$\$$160,sides $\$$6-$\$$14,desserts $\$$12-$\$$19.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_011.jpg	McGonagle's Pub
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_012.jpg	If you appreciate the Dubliner, bringing excellent fish and chips and Irish hospitality to downtown Boston, you will likely feel the same about McGonagle's in Dorchester.Pigs in a blanket made with sausage from Adams Village
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_013.jpg	MICHAEL DISKIN
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_014.jpg	Desnuda Cocina & Bar(above)and Russ & Mimi's (right) are among the many exciting restaurants arriving on the scene.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_015.jpg	Butcher Shop,smoky seafood chowder with Irish soda bread,shepherd's pie, bangers and mash,and other pub favorites are here,along with wood-fired pizza,brunch (yes,full Irish breakfast), and festive cocktails.Or just drop in for live music and a proper pint of Guinness.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_016.jpg	367 Neponset Ave.,Dorchester,617- 514-4689,www.mcgonagles.com.Appetizers $\$$8-$\$$16,pizza $\$$18-$\$$19,entrees $\$$18-$\$$34.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_017.jpg	Mikiya Wagyu Shabu House
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_018.jpg	If you're starving,cold,and enjoy wagyu beef,Mikiya is calling you.This Chinatown restaurant serves all-you- can-eat wagyu hot pot at a variety of price points;you have 90 minutes to cook a variety of cuts in the broths of your choosing,while eating specialty dishes such as sweet shrimp sashimi and wagyu bone marrow,plus fruit and ice cream for dessert. Mikiya is a project from Chubby Group,whose Kevin Liu is also behind Matsunori Handroll Bar.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_019.jpg	JONATHAN WIGGS/GLOBE STAFF
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_020.jpg	Wagyu sets $\$$45-$\$$98(less with annual membership;free or half-price for kids).
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_021.jpg	Russ & Mimi's
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_022.jpg	Named for the parents of owner Kelly Walsh (Sweet Cheeks,Tiger Mama), this Roslindale market offers wine (it's in the former Solera space),olive oil,hot sauce,and other must-haves.But it's the food that has the neighborhood most
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_023.jpg	excited.Start the morning with egg wraps,overnight oats,cheesy grits,and cumin-spiced black beans with fried egg,salsa,and avocado; then segue into the “Cubano,veggie grinder,muffuletta, charred broccoli Caesar"part of the day. Chef Stephen Marcaurelle (the Nautilus Pier4, Tres Gatos) helped design the menu.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_024.jpg	16 Birch St., Roslindale,617-833- 6950,www.russandmimis.com.Dishes
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_025.jpg	$8-$16.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_026.jpg	Soul on Shawmut
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_027.jpg	Roxbury-raised chef Aadam Abdul-Musawwir began serving prepared meals and pop-up dinners as Aadam's Halal Meals.Now, he and two siblings have opened Soul on Shawmut to feed their community. Lightly updated soul food classics are on the menu,along with Buffalo chicken egg rolls,grilled cheese burgers,and more.Fried chicken with Old Bay honey,smoked mac and cheese,slow-cooked collard greens with sauteed cabbage,sweet potato cheesecake,and hibiscus watermelon juice sounds pretty unbeatable for dinner tonight.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_028.jpg	722A Shawmut Ave., Roxbury, 617- 238-7525,www.soulonshawmut.com. Sandwiches $\$$10-$\$$18,fried chicken $\$$7-$$\$18,sides $\$$5,desserts $\$$3-$\$$6.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_029.jpg	Standard Italian
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_030.jpg	Where others fail, there is Italian food.Standard Italian,in the Eastern Standard complex looming over Fenway,replaces the former All That Fish + Oyster,and it's a smart move.Lasagna bolognese, porchetta, and espresso martinis could just get us all through the winter.Culinary director Brian Rae is in his element,having previously worked at Coppa, Faccia a Faccia,and Rialto.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_031.jpg	771 Beacon St.,Fenway, Boston,857-305-3095,www.standarditalian.com. Appetizers $\$$5-$\$$20, entrees $\$$28-$\$$42, desserts $\$$9-$\$$12.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_032.jpg	Zurito
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_033.jpg	It's named for the small glass of beer that often accompanies pintxos,the Basque take on tapas,and this Beacon Hill restaurant serves up plenty: Gildas, skewers of anchovies,peppers,and olives; griddled shrimp with garlic and chile oil; mini croissants stuffed with jamon iberico; squid ink risotto.Cocktails such as the sherry-based Perfect Bamboo and fizzy glasses of tzakolina are perfect accompaniments.Zurito,located in the longtime Bin 26 space,is run by chef Jamie Bissonnette, Babak Bina, and Andy Cartin of BCB3 Hospitality, also behind Somaek,Temple Records, and Sushi @ Temple Records.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_034.jpg	26 Charles St.,Beacon Hill,Boston, 857-305-3177,www.zuritoboston.com Pintxos $\$$4.50 – $\$$12 ,raciones $\$$9 – $\$$19, platos especiales $\$$17-$\$$125.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_035.jpg	Devra First can be reached at devra.first@globe.com.Follow her @devrafirst.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_036.jpg	Restaurants continue to innovate in mocktail realm
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_037.jpg	MOCKTAILS
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_038.jpg	Continued from Page G1
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_039.jpg	there are a plethora of base options that can really make a nonalcoholic drink look and taste nearly identical to an alcoholic one,"says Joel Atlas,a mixologist at the Avery Bar at the Ritz-Carlton,Boston.“The bloom of options has really picked up in the last year and a half to two years."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_040.jpg	“[Mocktails] are certainly marketed more in January, but the truth of it all is that the nonalcoholic or low-ABV consumer is sustaining through much of the year,”says Nick Moniz, general manager of Banners Kitchen and Tap and Hub Hall.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_041.jpg	“For so long,a mocktail was limited to just a soda water and cranberry,or a sugar bomb of juices made with whatever was lying around behind the bar. Consumers wanted and deserved more than that."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_042.jpg	In that spirit- or lack there- of-here are 12 mocktail desti- nations to try every month of the year.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_043.jpg	Abe & Louie's
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_044.jpg	The storied steakhouse offers a $14 mocktail menu.Their New Year,New Spritz combines zero- proof sparkling Cava with non-alcoholic tequila,cucumber, lime,and mint for a spa-like pour. Good for: expense-account dining.793 Boylston St.,Boston, 617-536-6300
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_045.jpg	Alcove
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_046.jpg	In the West End,cozy Alcove does an $\$$11 mocktail menu with unusual offerings like Papi's Punch,with grilled pineapple syrup and Athletic Free Wave nonalcoholic IPA,or a Queen's Bee,made with the herbaceous nonalcoholic spirit Garden 108 and honey. Good for: T.D.Garden pre-game hangouts.50 Love- joy Wharf,Boston,617-248-0050
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_047.jpg	Avery Bar at the Ritz-Carlton, Boston
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_048.jpg	This might be one of Boston's most classic of cocktail refuges, but it also caters to modern mores.A nonalcoholic Old-Fash
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_049.jpg	ioned employs decaf English breakfast tea;a Cucumber Gimless is an aromatic booze-free gin-and-cucumber palate cleanser. Good for: sophisticated post-work drinks.10 Avery St.,Boston,617-574-7176
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_050.jpg	Baleia
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_051.jpg	This stylish new Portuguese restaurant in the South End, from the team behind Gufo and SRV,welcomes a cosmopolitan crowd eager to experiment with nonalcoholic delights.Try the Tropical Amargo,an aperitif made with coconut-chai, orange, lime,pineapple,and coffee,plus spirit-free Campari. Good for: date night.264 East Berkeley St., Boston, 617-505-3243
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_052.jpg	Bistro du Midi
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_053.jpg	This French restaurant enjoys a scenic perch overlooking the
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_054.jpg	Above:Warming mocktails at Capo in South Boston. Left:A cosmopolitan at Bistro du Midi.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_055.jpg	Public Garden,and it serves far more than wine.A $10 mocktail menu has spins on classics like tiki punch,a cosmopolitan,and a spicy citrus mule. Good for: dinner with the parents. 272 Boylston St.,Boston,617-279-8000
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_057.jpg	This South Boston red-sauce lair might seem like the type of spot to throw back icy martinis (they do host Sinatra Sundays,after all) paired with big plates of rigatoni Bolognese,but they also have warming $\$$9 mocktails,like the Far From the Tree,with toasty apple cider,cardamom, allspice,thyme,and egg whites. In addition to catering to non- drinkers,they accommodate a range of palates,with gluten-free pasta on request.Good for:a night out with a group.443 West Broadway,Boston,617-993-8080
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_058.jpg	Hunter's
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_059.jpg	This is your spot for biscuits, fried chicken, mac-and-cheese ... in other words,resolution- breaking meals.That said: This dark,cozy bar and kitchen also pays attention to mocktails, which are a relative steal at $6. Wash down your honey-butter,
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_060.jpg	bacon-topped cornbread with refreshing house-made watermelon juice and soda. Good for: comfort food.110 Dorchester St, Boston,617-221-4200
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_061.jpg	La Padrona
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_062.jpg	Stylish,swanky,and with a stellar chef attached (Jody Adams),this Italian destination inside Raffles Boston is the ultimate in special-occasion dining: The silken tagliatelle alone is worth the trip up that grand staircase.Sure,there's also an extensive martini menu,but spirit- free drinks also get proper due. A nonalcoholic tangerine margarita with spirit-free tequila and lime is worth toasting.Not quite ready to abstain?There's also an extensive low-ABV list featuring treats like a Lambrusco Cobbler, made with black currant and berries.Good for:a celebratory splurge.38 Trinity Place,Boston, 617-221-4200
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_063.jpg	Midnight Morning
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_064.jpg	This cheery Roslindale bistro specializes in comfort food: sau- sage dip,mushroom-and-cream- cheese wontons,burgers,and fried pickles.It makes nondrinkers feel comfy,too,with a nice $\$$12 selection of zero-proof drinks: The Regret Nothing, with nonalcoholic Ritual Proof tequila,jalapeno,and lime,is true to its name.Good for:a laid- back weeknight dinner. 14 Birch St.,Roslindale,617-390-5830
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_065.jpg	Trina's Starlite Lounge
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_066.jpg	This industry hangout takes dive-bar food to another level- they pioneered dressed-up tater tots and mac-and-cheese before it was fashionable.Here,$\$$5 cans of Miller High Life share real estate with cheeky mocktails like the Phony Negroni,a nonalcoholic version of the classic,and Island Life,Baby,made with a booze-free,floral Fluere Botanic Blend. Good for: dive-bar-dining without the buzz.3 Beacon St., Somerville,617-576-0006
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_067.jpg	Tuscan Brands
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_068.jpg	This Italian mini-empire
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_069.jpg	serves reliable food in conve- nient locations and a thoughtfully curated list of spirit-free sips, like a Tea Party with coconut water,chai tea,and brown sugar, and the Cos-No-Politan,made with honey water,cranberry, rooibos tea,and lime.Beverage director Jose Luis Betancur also has a fondness for tart Italian sodas like Chinotto or Stappi Gassosa. Good for: pleasing a crowd (or kids).64 Seaport Boulevard, Boston,617-303-7300,and other locations
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_070.jpg	Woods Hill Pier 4
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_071.jpg	Owner Kristin Canty is known for serving pasture
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_033_line_072.jpg	raised meat and other fresh, healthy ingredients from her Farm at Woods Hill in New Hampshire at this waterfront branch of her West Concord original. Mocktails (most are $8 and up)also showcase ingredients and garnishes from her farm.Sip a Fly-Tai,made with pineapple,orange,lime,almond orgeat,and ginger ale. Good for: entertaining out-of-towners.300 Pier 4 Blvd.,Boston,617-981- 4577

When a shocking decision is made by their father, the family must find new meaning in what makes a home. Tickets at lyricstage.com or by calling the box office at 617-585-5678

test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_002.jpg	Norovirus cases in Mass. surged in 2024
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_003.jpg	By Camilo Fonseca
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_004.jpg	GLOBE STAFF
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_005.jpg	Mirroring a national trend,cases of the norovirus stomach bug are surging in Massachusetts this winter,according to state data.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_006.jpg	The state reported 2,080 labora-tory-confirmed cases of the virus in 2024 in preliminary data released last week by the Massachusetts Department of Public Health.That represents a jump of roughly 66 percent from the previous year, when the state reported 1,252 cases.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_007.jpg	Cases over the winter have so far outpaced the previous year, with 134 cases reported in November and 279 reported in December.In 2023,there were 52 cases reported in November and 112 in December.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_008.jpg	Officials say $66\%$ jump in a year reflects US trend; call handwashing best defense
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_009.jpg	Public health officials say they have been monitoring the increase in cases.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_010.jpg	Nationally, cases of the virus are surging in parts of the United States this winter,according to government data.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_011.jpg	The most recent numbers from the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention show there were 91 outbreaks of norovirus reported during the week of Dec.5, up from 69 out- breaks the last week of November.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_012.jpg	Numbers from the past few years show a maximum of 65 outbreaks reported during that first week of December.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_013.jpg	Norovirus symptoms include nausea,vomiting,diarrhea,and abdominal cramps,according to state health officials.Despite being one of the most common causes of food-borne illness,the virus is difficult to detect in a laboratory,and specialized norovirus tests are usually conducted only in certain circumstances.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_014.jpg	A spokesperson for the DPH said the uptick in cases may be attributed in part to more modern testing methods which can detect multiple pathogens at once.They also noted that certain test devices were recently found to be prone to false
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_015.jpg	positives of norovirus.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_016.jpg	Illness caused by norovirus typically starts suddenly,with symptoms developing 12 to 48 hours following exposure to the virus.Most people get better within one to three days and recover fully.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_017.jpg	People of all ages can get infected and fall sick from norovirus. Young children,older people,and those with weakened immune systems are most at risk,with dehydration from vomiting and diarrhea the top concern.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_018.jpg	There is no medication to treat norovirus. Rehydration is recommended by drinking water and other liquids,with the exception of coffee,tea,and alcohol.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_019.jpg	Health officials also recommend
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_020.jpg	NOROVIRUS,Page B3
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_021.jpg	CHILLIN' OUT
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_022.jpg	DAVID L. RYAN/GLOBE STAFF
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_023.jpg	Pedestrians donned an assortment of winter gear as they walked in downtown Boston Tuesday.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_024.jpg	Boston traffic ranked fourth worst in nation
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_025.jpg	By Shannon Larson
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_026.jpg	GLOBE STAFF
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_027.jpg	In what will come as no surprise to any Boston driver: We're still stuck in traffic.The city continues to rank in the top-five most congested cities in the United States and among the worst worldwide.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_028.jpg	Still, Boston traffic is generally improving.Unless,that is, you're heading south on I-93 after work.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_029.jpg	The average motorist traversing Boston spent 79 hours stuck in traffic in 2024, according to an annual study from INRIX,a transportation analytics company.In 2023,that average driver lost 88 hours.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_030.jpg	That 10 percent drop is the largest for any of the top 25 most congested American cities.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_031.jpg	Study finds drivers here spent an average 79 hours stuck in traffic last year
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_032.jpg	Some key factors,according to the Mass.Department of Transportation,include investments in infrastructure, such as optimized traffic signals and dedicated bus lines; public transit enhancements; more people choosing biking or walking; and folks adjusting their commuting schedules.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_033.jpg	“We're encouraged by this progress,but know there's still work to be done,”said the state highway administrator,Jonathan Gulliver.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_034.jpg	Worldwide,congestion again edged closer to prepandemic levels as more than half of the urban ar-
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_035.jpg	eas studied experienced an increase in traffic-a trend driven,in part, by workers returning to the office. Istanbul,Mexico City,London,and Paris were among the most congested cities,according to the study.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_036.jpg	In the US last year, the average worker lost 43 hours to traffic jams, the equivalent of a work week,IN- RIX found.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_037.jpg	“Traffic can be an indicator of economic boon,but ironically, it's a hamper on economies in of itself. Each minute spent waiting in traffic results in money and productivity lost,”said Bob Pishue,transpor-
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_038.jpg	tation analyst at INRIX and author of the report, which determined that \$74 billion was lost to congestion nationwide.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_039.jpg	For another year running,New York was the most congested city in the US,according to the scorecard, with the average New York commuter losing 102 hours,followed by Chicago drivers who also lost 102 hours,and Los Angeles motorists, who lost 88 hours.People behind the wheel in Boston came in fourth nationwide,the same position it was ranked by INRIX in 2023.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_040.jpg	In Boston,congestion resulted in the average speed of a downtown trip crawling along at 13 miles per hour- the second-slowest in the
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_041.jpg	TRAFFIC, Page B4
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_042.jpg	Population rise in Mass. is largest in 60 years
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_043.jpg	Census figures show increase largely driven by influx of immigrants
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_044.jpg	By Sam Drysdale
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_045.jpg	STATE HOUSE NEWS
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_046.jpg	Fueled by the highest immigration levels in decades, Massachusetts saw its largest population increase in 60 years between 2023 and 2024, and the rate of domestic outmigration has significantly slowed,according to US Census data released last month.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_047.jpg	Between July 1,2023 and July 1,2024, the state's population increased 69,603 from 7,066,568 to 7,136,171-an increase of just under 1 percent (0.985 percent).
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_048.jpg	That's the largest annual percentage increase that Massachusetts has experienced since between 2012 and 2013,according to a UMass Donahue Institute analysis of the December US Census population estimates.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_049.jpg	Numbers-wise, it's the largest annual population increase since the end of the baby boom in 1964.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_050.jpg	Those gains made Massachusetts the fastest growing state in New England and the second-fastest growing state in the Northeast after New Jersey,according to the institute.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_051.jpg	90,217
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_052.jpg	The estimated number of immigrants that came to Massachusetts between 2023 and 2024
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_053.jpg	SOURCE: US Census data
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_054.jpg	The largest driver: immigration, estimated at 90,217 incoming immigrants between 2023 and 2024.The
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_055.jpg	census estimates that's the highest immigration level since at least 1990.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_056.jpg	“While immigration is the single largest driver of population change in Massachusetts... other factors also play a role,"according to the institute's analysis.“In 2024,Massachusetts saw more births (67,851) than deaths (61,133),contributing 6,718,on net,to the population growth in Massachusetts."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_057.jpg	Massachusetts has seen an influx of thousands of refugees and migrants- seeking asylum from wars,political unrest,and environmental disasters - who have sought temporary housing in the state's emergency family shelter system,straining the physical and financial limits of the system. The census data does not delineate between immigrants legally seeking asylum or refuge and those otherwise entering the country.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_058.jpg	Domestic outmigration, the term used to describe people moving out of one state to another state,has been a contentious political topic for the past several years,as many young adults and families left Massachusetts in the early days of the pandemic,settling in states with lower costs of living.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_059.jpg	Conservatives point to high taxes as the reason for domestic outmigration,calling on Democrats to lower tax burdens to keep Bay Staters from moving.Progressives,on the other hand,have pointed to high housing and child care costs,and called for more state investment in programs to
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_060.jpg	POPULATION,Page B4
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_061.jpg	INSIDE

test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_066.jpg	ALISON FIELD 1966-2024
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_067.jpg	Brown professor conducted groundbreaking research into eating disorders and obesity
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_068.jpg	By Bryan Marquard
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_069.jpg	GLOBE STAFF
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_070.jpg	Alison Field traced her early insights into obesity and eating disorders to a job in the late 1980s that seemed far removed from the international reputation she would earn as a professor and researcher.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_071.jpg	While attending college in California,she was a consultant to a Jenny Craig weight loss clinic and began honing skills she would bring to decades of studies: listening to those trying to lose weight,and watching carefully and compassionately to see how nuanced differences among people could splinter a sweeping diagnosis into subcategories.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_072.jpg	“There probably isn't one magic bullet for obesity”Dr.Field said in 2018,when she was lead author of a paper published in the journal Obesity that analyzed data from more than 2,400 patients who had undergone bariatric weight-loss surgery.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_073.jpg	“If there is a magic bullet,” she added, “it's going to be different for different groups of people."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_074.jpg	Dr.Field,an epidemiology professor and associate dean for faculty affairs at the Brown University School of Public Health who formerly taught at Harvard Medical School, died Oct.10 in her home in the Newton section of Chestnut Hill.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_075.jpg	She was 58 and had survived cancer three times during the
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_076.jpg	FIELD, Page B3
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_077.jpg	ERIC SENUNAS
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_078.jpg	Dr. Field, who also taught at Harvard Medical School, began honing her skills while consulting at a weight loss clinic.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_079.jpg	BOSTONGLOBE.COM/BUSINESS
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_080.jpg	Business
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_081.jpg	Nurses union requests state monitors at Saint Vincent Hospital
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_011_line_082.jpg	PAGE B5
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_000.jpg	The World
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_001.jpg	Strong earthquake hits remote Tibet
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_002.jpg	Dozens killed as cold complicates rescue efforts
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_003.jpg	By David Pierson, Berry Wang, and Bhadra Sharma
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_004.jpg	NEW YORK TIMES
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_005.jpg	HONG KONG —Using their hands and shovels in frigid conditions, rescue workers dug through the rubble in the search for survivors after a deadly 7.1 magnitude earthquake toppled houses and jolted people awake in a remote part of Tibet on Tuesday near the northern foothills of Mount Everest.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_006.jpg	At least 126 people have died and 188 were injured in the quake,which struck shortly after 9 a.m.at a depth of 6.2 miles in Dingri County,near one of Tibet's most historic cities, in western China, state media reported. The quake was the country's deadliest since December 2023, when 151 people were killed in a 6.2 magnitude temblor in the northwestern provinces of Gansu and Qinghai.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_007.jpg	China's state broadcaster reported that more than 1,000 houses had experienced some form of damage in Dingri County, where the average altitude is around 15,000 feet, along the Himalayan border with Nepal.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_008.jpg	Frantic rescue efforts were being conducted without heavy equipment, underscoring the challenge of delivering resources to the largely isolated communities damaged by the quake. With temperatures in the region dipping as low as 5 degrees Fahrenheit, rescue workers have a short window in which to locate survivors. It was not immediately clear how many residents had been left homeless.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_009.jpg	Several aftershocks were felt in the area, including in Nepal. The quake had a magnitude of 7.1, according to the US Geological Survey, though it was measured as 6.8 by the China Earthquake Networks Center.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_010.jpg	The nearest city to the earthquake's epicenter was Shigatse, the second-largest city in Tibet,
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_011.jpg	HU ZIKUI / XINHUA VIA ASSOCIATED PRESS
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_012.jpg	First responders performed rescue work in Changsuo Township,Tibet, western China,on Tuesday
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_013.jpg	with a population of 640,000. Shigatse is home to the vast, centuries-old Tashilhunpo Monastery, the traditional seat of the Panchen Lama,one of the most senior figures in Tibetan Buddhism. State media said no damage to the monastery had been reported so far.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_014.jpg	Scenes of destruction were broadcast on state media and shared on social media. A tourist not far from Shigatse who spoke to The New York Times
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_015.jpg	said she was in her hotel room when the earthquake started shaking her building.She said the electricity went out and that she and a friend had squatted between the beds.When the shaking stopped, they ran out of the building.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_016.jpg	The tourist, who only gave her surname, Xu, shared a video showing several single-story brick buildings with collapsed walls. Video posted on Chinese social media showed streets strewed with rubble, cars crushed by fallen bricks, and roads split open by the shifted ground. Xu said she grabbed her down jacket before she ran out.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_017.jpg	China's top leader,Xi Jinping,ordered officials to minimize casualties and resettle survivors.Chinese authorities deployed 3,400 rescuers and more than 340 medical workers for the search effort,and dis-
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_018.jpg	patched tents,folding beds, winter coats,and quilts,state media reported.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_019.jpg	Rescue efforts could be affected by bottlenecks caused by damaged roads, said Robert Barnett,a professor from SOAS University of London, who has visited the region and described it as looking like “vast moonscape.”
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_020.jpg	“This is harsh, high-altitude land," Barnett said. “The roads are quite few and susceptible to landslides."
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_021.jpg	That said,Barnett added that there could be new roads connecting some of Dingri County's most remote border villages built in recent years to assert China's sovereignty along its periphery.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_022.jpg	The Himalayan region is prone to powerful earthquakes. In 2015, a quake in Nepal with a magnitude of 7.8 killed nearly 9,000 people. In Kathmandu,
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_023.jpg	Nepal's capital,residents streamed out of their homes in the morning as the earthquake rattled buildings.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_024.jpg	At least two people, one in Kathmandu and another one in Sindhupalchowk, a district north of Kathmandu, sustained minor injuries from the quake, according to Nepalese police.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_025.jpg	Nepal sent more than 2,500 police officers to assess damage and look for victims.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_026.jpg	“Based on the magnitude of the earthquake,there could be some damage in mountains of eastern Nepal,”said Lok Bijaya Adhikari,a senior seismologist at Nepal's National Earthquake Monitoring and Research Center.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_027.jpg	Most residents from Nepal's high mountain regions such as Everest, Makalu, Rolwaling, and Kanchenjunga have migrated to lowland areas to avoid the extreme cold of winter.

test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_033.jpg	The Israeli military said it targeted militants who had taken part in the Oct.7, 2023, attack that sparked the war, without providing evidence.Israel said it took steps to lessen the risk of hurting civilians and blamed Hamas for the civilian casualties.

test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_035.jpg	Displaced Palestinians in the Gaza Strip still have hope that Israel's 15-month war with Hamas will end soon.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_036.jpg	“What we are living is not a life. Nobody could bear the situation we're experiencing for a single day," said Munawar al-Bik, a displaced woman from Gaza City.

test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_039.jpg	“We wake up at night to the sounds of men crying,because of the bad situation,”al-Bik said.“The situation is unbearable,we have no energy left, we want it to end today."

test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_043.jpg	and they struggle to cope with harsh winter conditions that have killed several babies from hypothermia in recent weeks.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_044.jpg	Meanwhile, the Biden administration in its final days is shifting more than \$100 million in military aid from Israel and Egypt to Lebanon as it tries to bolster a cease-fire agreement it helped mediate between Israel and Hezbollah.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_045.jpg	In separate notices sent to Congress, the State Department said it was moving \$95 million in military assistance intended for Egypt and \$7.5 million for Israel toward supporting the Lebanese army and its government.The notices were dated Jan.3 and obtained by the Associated Press on Tuesday.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_046.jpg	Most of the money will go to the Lebanese Armed Forces, which have a critical role in standing up the cease-fire that was agreed to in November following an all-out war between Israeli and Hezbollah that battered much of southern and eastern Lebanon for two months.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_047.jpg	It is intended to help the LAF deploy in the south of the country and supplement the role of the UN peacekeeping mission patrolling the so-called Blue Line,which has separated Israel and Lebanon since the end of a monthlong Israel-Hez-bollah war in 2006.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_048.jpg	Both Israel and Hezbollah agreed to pull their forces out of southern Lebanon before the end of January, with com-
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_049.jpg	pliance to be overseen by the Lebanese army and UN peace- keepers.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_050.jpg	“US security assistance to the LAF increases its capacity as the country's only legitimate military force and defender of Lebanon's territorial integrity, enables the LAF to prevent potential destabilization from ISIS and other terrorist groups, and enables the LAF to provide security both for the Lebanese people and for US personnel," the State Department said.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_051.jpg	Pro-Israel members of Congress and others have in the past complained about any diversion of US assistance to Israel,although it was not immediately clear if there would be objections to such a small amount of shifted money.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_052.jpg	At the same time,some of those who have been forceful advocates of Israel and critics of US assistance to the Lebanese military have often complained that it has been infiltrated by Hezbollah.The notices rejected that claim.
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_053.jpg	“US support to the LAF reinforces the LAF as an important institutional counter- weight to Hezbollah, which receives weapons, training, and financial support from Iran,” the State Department said. “The LAF continues to be an independent, non-sectarian institution in Lebanon, and is respected across all sectors.”
test/images/newspaper_TheBostonGlobe-2025-1-8@magazinesclubnew_page_003_line_054.jpg	In a third notice,also sent to Congress on Jan.3, the department said it was going to provide \$15 million to Lebanon's Internal Security Forces to ensure that they become the primary law enforcement entity in the country and assist the LAF in controlling areas in the south.

test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_000.jpg	7.1-magnitude quake kills at least 95 in southern Tibet
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_001.jpg	BY LYRIC LI AND CHRISTIAN SHEPHERD
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_002.jpg	An earthquake killed at least 95 people and left 130 injured after it struck a remote area of southern Tibet near China's border with Nepal at dawn on Tuesday，Chinese state media reported.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_003.jpg	Liu Huazhong, deputy mayor of Shigatse, broke into tears during a news conference Tuesday afternoon while announcing the human toll and the damage.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_004.jpg	Emergency responders were still assessing the extent of the damage in Dingri county from the quake, which struck at 9:05 a.m. Beijing time, state broadcaster CCTV said. The U.S. Geological Survey put the quake at 7.1 magnitude. Earlier reports from Chinese media classified it as 6.8-magnitude.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_005.jpg	The tremor brought down
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_006.jpg	more than 1,700 homes in a county that is home to 60,000 people, according to the Chinese People's Liberation Army's Tibet Military Command. The broader Shigatse, at the epicenter of the quake, is the second-largest city in Tibet and the holy seat of the second-most-important religious leader in Tibetan Buddhism, the state-appointed Panchen Lama.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_007.jpg	The China Earthquake Networks Center has recorded multiple aftershocks and expects more in coming days. Tibet sits on the seismically active Himalayan region and is prone to earthquakes because of the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_008.jpg	Chinese leader Xi Jinping called for an all-out search-and-rescue effort to minimize casualties. "We must ... repair damaged infrastructure as soon as possi-
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_009.jpg	JIGME DORJE/XINHUA/AP
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_010.jpg	Emergency workers search for survivors Tuesday in the aftermath of an earthquake in a remote area of southern Tibet.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_011.jpg	ble, arrange basic living arrangements for the people, and ensure people are safe and warm through the winter,” CCTV re-
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_012.jpg	ported him as saying.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_013.jpg	Some 1,500 firefighters were searching the rubble for signs of life. Around noon, a child was
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_014.jpg	rescued from a collapsed residential building, CCTV reported.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_015.jpg	Several hundred rescuers who arrived before noon began providing medical aid and relief to displaced residents. Another team, involving hundreds of rescuers and six helicopters, was expected to follow.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_016.jpg	Sitting high on the Tibetan plateau at an elevation of 14,100 feet and 236 miles from the regional capital of Lhasa, Dingri is a major stop on the way to the North Base Camp, where adventurers set out climbing Mount Everest from the north face. The region can be bitterly cold in January, with temperatures of-ten plunging as low as 5 degrees Fahrenheit (minus-15 degrees Celsius).
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_017.jpg	Local tourism officials on Tuesday closed the North Base Camp until further notice.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_018.jpg	Power and internet remained
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_019.jpg	off in parts of Shigatse on Tuesday afternoon, CCTV reported.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_020.jpg	A Dingri resident told local media that the shaking at first felt similar to the massive 2008 earthquake in neighboring Sichuan province that killed 70,000. Then the second floor of his house caved in and crushed his car, said a man identified only by his surname, Wang.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_021.jpg	Past earthquakes that hit mountainous Tibetan regions of western China have drawn public concern about badly constructed homes in relatively poor and isolated villages.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_022.jpg	As Trump eyes Greenland. Danish king makes it bigger on coat of arms
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_023.jpg	BY ANNABELLE TIMSIT
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_024.jpg	Denmark's king has unveiled a new royal coat of arms that more prominently features Greenland, as President-elect Donald Trump again floats the idea of the United States buying the Danish territory, a proposal that disrupted relations with Copenhagen during his first term in office.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_025.jpg	King Frederik X issued a declaration in December to change the royal coat of arms for the first time in over 50 years, the Danish royal household said in a statement on Jan.1. The new design, which replaces the one Frederik's mother, Queen Margrethe II, established in June 1972, “strengthens the prominence of the Commonwealth in the royal coat of arms," it said.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_026.jpg	The bottom-left quadrant of the shield at the center of the coat of arms previously featured three crowns representing the union of Sweden, Norway and Denmark, as well as symbols of the two parts of the Danish Commonwealth: a
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_027.jpg	KONGEHUSET.DK
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_028.jpg	In the new Danish royal coat of arms, right, the bear symbolizing Greenland and the ram for the Faroe Islands have been enlarged.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_029.jpg	polar bear for Greenland and a ram for the Faroe Islands. In the new coat of arms, the crowns have been removed, and the polar bear and ram have their own quadrants.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_030.jpg	The king's move represents a “continuation” of the Danish royal family's traditional role as a bridge-builder within the Com-
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_031.jpg	monwealth, said Mikkel Runge Olesen, senior researcher at the Danish Institute for International Studies. Danish royals have historically played the role “of keeping the kingdom together," Olesen said.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_032.jpg	As Trump gets set for his second term, he is again promoting the idea of Greenland becoming
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_033.jpg	part of the United States-even after Danish and Greenlandic officials dismissed the notion in the years since he first brought it up. His son Donald Trump Jr. visited Greenland on Tuesday in what he described as a “very long personal day trip.”
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_034.jpg	The president-elect wrote Monday on Truth Social that his son“and various representatives" would travel to Greenland to “visit some of the most magnificent areas and sights."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_035.jpg	“Greenland is an incredible place, and the people will benefit tremendously if, and when, it becomes part of our Nation," Trump said. “We will protect it, and cherish it, from a very vicious outside World."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_036.jpg	Greenland, an Arctic island roughly three times the size of Texas with a population of about 57,000 people, is an autonomous territory with its own parliament and government and has been vying for independence from Denmark. It is home to a large U.S. military base, and Washington views the territory as a key strategic location while competition with China and Russia increases in the Arctic.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_037.jpg	Last month, Trump said, “For purposes of National Security and Freedom throughout the
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_038.jpg	World, the United States of America feels that the ownership and control of Greenland is an absolute necessity."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_039.jpg	Trump initially proposed buying Greenland during his first term, prompting rejections from Greenlandic and Danish officials. He canceled a planned two-day state visit to Denmark in 2019 after Danish Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen called the idea of the sale “absurd."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_040.jpg	Relations eventually improved,and U.S. and Danish officials characterized a call between Trump and Frederiksen in August 2019 as “constructive."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_041.jpg	“Everyone moved on," said Olesen，but the question of Greenland's status remains “a touchy subject."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_042.jpg	How Danish officials will respond to Trump's overtures about Greenland during his second term remains to be seen. But the situation could be slightly different this time around, Olesen said. In October, three former Trump administration officials wrote in an editorial for National Interest in favor of a U.S.-Greenland free-association agreement, acknowledging the Danish government's sovereignty over the territory.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_043.jpg	Though Trump has not spoken publicly about the idea and it is
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_044.jpg	not clear whether the former officials’ proposal represents his thinking, the editorial could hint to a softening of the president-elect's stance, Olesen said.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_045.jpg	The Trump campaign did not immediately respond Tuesday to a request for comment.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_046.jpg	For the Danish government, this situation is “a huge headache,” Olesen said. It cannot be seen to be pressuring Greenland or jeopardize relations with Washington, which it depends on for security guarantees through NATO. Copenhagen has focused on increasing its support for the island: In addition to an annual block grant of about \$511 million, Denmark's defense minister announced last month a defense package for Greenland that comes to some \$1.5 billion.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_047.jpg	Frederik also highlighted the importance of Greenland in his New Year's address.
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_048.jpg	“We are all united and each of us committed for the Kingdom of Denmark,” he said. “From the Danish minority in South Schleswig – which is even situated outside the Kingdom – and all the way to Greenland. We belong together."
test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_009_line_049.jpg	Rachel Pannett contributed to this report.

test/images/newspaper_USAToday-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_012_line_055.jpg	From USA TODAY Network and wire reports
test/images/newspaper_USAToday-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_012_line_056.jpg	| WEDNESDAY JANUARY 8, 2025 | USA TODAY
test/images/newspaper_USAToday-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_012_line_057.jpg	MONEY
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_000.jpg	Dark layers ...
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_001.jpg	The Sentimentality of Nature, 2022
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_002.jpg	The bold nature of these works -some have commented on their frank configuration and their "difficult brown-ish" palette- belies the challenging environment in which Grewal came to embrace painting. At Brighton's School of Art, he says, the emphasis was on satire and conceptual justification: people doing bad paintings to make fun of the whole idea. His work, by contrast, was figurative and heavy on metaphor, hinging on the expressive, the poetic, the dreamlike."I might be too earnest,” he says. “There are different ways of making things and some are never going to be me. I'd much rather look at something that's going to make me cry than laugh, especially in painting."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_003.jpg	After graduating,he worked as a cleaner,listening to homoerotic audiobooks while trying to keep his studio practice afloat. Then he did an MA at the Royal Drawing School in London-and everything changed. He is keen to namecheck one tutor, Sarah Pickstone, for encouraging him to push his honesty to the limit. "There was this energy that built up within me," he says. "It demanded I take the clothes off the figures.There was still a lot of metaphor and symbolism, but now the figures were naked and in nature. From there, I got looser and looser."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_004.jpg	He spent a month at Rhode Island School of Design, on a scholarship led by Gwen Strahle, who would talk to him about JMW Turner, Frank Auerbach and Leon Kossoff-artists who were fundamental to Grewal, yet unknown to his many of his peers. It felt like validation. He also discovered compressed charcoal and has not stopped using it since.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_005.jpg	During Covid, since museums were closed, Grewal tracked down National Gallery catalogues on eBay and used the works they contained as drawing inspiration. He dived into queer cinema, became obsessed with Degas. "I really poured my heart into my practice," he says. "I was drenched in it."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_006.jpg	His studio is everything you could want an artist's studio to be.Paint pots and spills are everywhere. Books line the window sills and shelves,all within
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_007.jpg	easy reach.When a British Museum curator recently discovered Grewal's charcoal drawings,she directed him to Georges Seurat. "Before Seurat gets to pointillism, he does this dissolving thing, says Grewal,opening a nearby book to show me.When I mention Eadweard Muybridge's studies of movement,he replies"Exactly!" and reaches for book about them."I have all these clipboards,”he says, lifting one up bearing a fragment of a landscape photograph,ready to be consulted while painting. "I think this is how Jenny Saville works.Like,I want to paint this place which is somewhere I haven't been,but which reminds me of Cornwall.And I want to paint it like Monet,in terms of a shimmery effect.I don't know how successful that will be but..."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_008.jpg	For all his love of charcoal, the Studio Voltaire exhibition will feature only paintings on canvas. The refreshed palette-the brilliant azures,yellows and pinks,not to mention the outsized dimensions of that central piece-will surprise fans as much as they have surprised the artist himself.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_009.jpg	"My paintings often reflect an emotional state," he says.“They're an expression of a history,or a memory-a kind of figuring out.In the past,the dark layers have been directly confusing. They've been about hiding things.Those qualities are probably going to be in these new paintings now,but they're also bathed in this light,which I’m surprised about."He ascribes this- and a “weird confidence”-to time spent sketching around St Ives in Cornwall,as well as to a recent and wide-ranging trip to India,his first to the country his father comes from.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_010.jpg	Grewal is often described as a queer figurative painter,with his south Asian heritage frequently added to the mix.None of these are labels he particularly covets."I'm queer and I'm a figurative painter," he says."I don't really know what else to say.It’s almost like I'm being asked to label myself before I've made those personal discoveries myself.I haven't referenced being south Asian in a formal way in an exhibition-because that's not yet my experience."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_011.jpg	For a long time,it felt important that people knew that he was from London.However,he has begun to wonder if the unspecified locations in his paintings are his way of grappling with feelings of displacement."That is something that I'm processing," he says."It's lovely having people write about your work-but it's a funny thing when you're still trying to figure out why you make it."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_012.jpg	He goes on:“In queer figuration, there's a desire for people to think of adolescence,of nymphs,of idealised youth.There's a yearning for unattainable beauty,or purity, that a lot of older gay men have. There is nostalgia in my work, but there's also a darkness and a vulnerability.I'm trying to be as honest and as real as possible." Jake Grewal:Under the Same Sky is at Studio Voltaire, London,from 15 January until 13 April
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_013.jpg	Vast space...
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_014.jpg	Arnhem's panopticon
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_015.jpg	Captive audiences
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_016.jpg	They were built to instil terror. Now the giant domed prisons of the Netherlands are being reborn as arts hubs-with escape rooms.Charis McGowan reports
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_017.jpg	It used to be that you couldn't leave.Now you won't want to
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_018.jpg	One of the architectural features that marks out the skyline of Haarlem, a small Dutch city, is a 37.6m-high dome, crowning a rotunda. You might assume it was built for religious purposes - until you notice the bars covering its 230 windows.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_019.jpg	Operating as a prison from 1899 until 2016, the Koepelgevangenis("dome prison")is one of three panoptic penitentiaries built in the Netherlands. All were shut down in the past decade as part of the country's drive to reduce its prison population and are now being repurposed as arts venues.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_020.jpg	Haarlem's Koepel opened as a cultural hub in 2022,thanks to a grassroots initiative led by the local population."So many of us Haarlemers had been biking around the dome for years,but no one could go in,says Jacqueline van de Sande,co-founder of the
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_021.jpg	Koepel Foundation, the organisation behind the building's transformation. "We needed to open it up."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_022.jpg	As you step inside, you're immediately struck by the vastness of the open space, bathed in sunlight streaming
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_023.jpg	through the 16 original skylights. A cafe fills the ground floor with chatter. Above,nearly all the former cells are being rented. There's a podcast studio,art school ateliers and gallery spaces,while the darkened cinema bar offers a more intimate charm.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_024.jpg	The Koepel's transformation is a far cry from the design's dark history.The panopticon dates back to the 1700s,first envisioned by British philosopher Jeremy Bentham.Circular prisons with a central guard tower meant prisoners could feel as if they were being watched,without really knowing whether they were or not.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_025.jpg	Few panopticonic structures were ever built,but the Netherlands has emerged as the unwitting"heartland"of panopticon design.The three well-preserved structures in Haarlem,Breda and Arnhem were engineered by Dutch architect Johan Frederik Metzelaar and his son Willem Cornelis,in the late1880s.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_026.jpg	After its closure as a prison,the Koepel was temporarily repurposed to house Syrian refugees while the government sought to sell the building.However,its bleak design,dark atmosphere and staggering heating costs of €400,000 a year made it unappealing."The
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_027.jpg	state was worried it wouldn't sell,"recalls van de Sande.Determined to find a solution,she called developers, universities, museums and organisations to garner support.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_028.jpg	The foundation eventually got the cooperation of the Haarlem municipality, allowing them to acquire the €6.5m property and embark on a €25m renovation project. Without modifying the protected structure, they were permitted to put extra windows in each cell,add a cinema and upgrade insulation and heating systems,slashing bills by $90\%$. It is now undergoing further development with a hotel,student accommodation and an escape room planned.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_029.jpg	The Arnhem panopticon,acquired by Q Hospitality Group,is currently undergoing reconstruction with plans to transform the dome into a multi-function event space and hotel.Breda,jointly acquired by Being and VDD Project Development,is set to open in 2028.The revamped three-hectare site includes private housing around the dome, with the panopticon transformed into an exhibition space.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_030.jpg	"It's a big,open space. Where can you find that in historic city centres?"says Bas van Dam,the founder of Being.“There's an obligation to give new life to places like this."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_031.jpg	They have already spoken to creative partners who might want to use the space, including Belgian festival Tomorrowland. "As a prison,it used to be a space where you didn't want to be and couldn’t leave.We're going to turn that around,"says van Dam. "You won't want to leave."
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_032.jpg	Back in Haarlem,the refurbishment has generated hundreds of jobs and the building welcomes up to 1,500 visitors daily.Yet if its multi-purpose atmosphere feels uplifting, ventures like prison escape rooms(a feature in both Haarlem and Arnhem) risk trivialising the building's past.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_033.jpg	One of the most striking experiences is a historical one,in a tiny cell next to the main entrance.On the wall,panels describe the life of Hannie Schaft,one of the few female armed resistance fighters in the Netherlands.She was caught in March 1945 and imprisoned in the Koepel,before being executed aged 24.
test/images/newspaper_The Guardian UK_0801@magazinesclubnew_page_061_line_034.jpg	Sophie Poldermans,author of a book about Schaft's life,stresses its importance:"So people are aware,not only of Schaft's story but the fact it was a prison.You can still feel that energy, even though it is now a much lighter space."Nonetheless,as a Haarlemer herself,she is thrilled to see the dome gain a new life."It's an icon.And it's beautiful."

test/images/newspaper_TheWashingtonPost-2025-01-08@magazinesclubnew_page_042_line_010.jpg	Terms of Sale : A deposit of \$30,000 in the form of certified check , cashier ' s check or money order will be required of the purchaser at time and place of sale . Balance of the purchase price to be paid in cash within ten days of final ratification of sale by the Circuit Court for Prince George ' s County . Interest to be paid on the unpaid purchase money at the rate pursuant to the Deed of Trust Note from the date of sale to the date funds are received in the office of the Trustees . There will be no abatement of interest in the event additional funds are tendered before settlement or if settlement is delayed for any reason . The noteholder shall not be obligated to pay interest if it is the purchaser . TIME IS OF THE ESSENCE FOR THE PURCHASER . There will be no reduction of interest due to overpayment of deposit . Adjustment of all real property taxes ( excluding recapture of previously reduced or exempt taxes ) and any other public charges or assessments , to the extent such amount survive foreclosure sale , including water / sewer charges , and ground rent to be adjusted to date of sale and paid at execution of the deed , except where the secured party is the purchaser , and thereafter assumed by the purchaser . All due and / or unpaid private utility , water and sewer facilities charges , condo / HOA assessments and Columbia Assoc . assessments , to the extent such amount survive foreclosure sale , are payable by the purchaser without adjustment . Cost of all documentary stamps , transfer taxes , recaptured taxes ( including but not limited to agricultural taxes ), and settlement expenses shall be borne by the purchaser . Purchaser shall be responsible for obtaining physical possession of the property . Purchaser assumes the risk of loss or damage to the property from the date of sale forward . Additional terms to be announced at the time of sale . If the Trustees are unable to convey good and marketable title , the purchaser ' s sole remedy in law and equity shall be limited to a refund of the deposit without interest . If the purchaser fails to go to settlement , subject to order of the court , the entire deposit ( including any amount received over the advertised deposit amount ) shall be forfeited to the Trustees as liquidated damages for all losses occasioned by the purchaser ' s default and purchaser shall have no further liability . In the event of a resale , the defaulting purchaser shall not be entitled to receive the surplus , if any , even if such surplus results from improvements to the property by said defaulting purchaser and the defaulting purchaser shall be liable to the Trustees and secured party for reasonable attorney ' s fees and expenses incurred in connection with all litigation involving the Property or the proceeds of the resale . Defaulting purchaser waives personal service of any document filed in connection with such a motion on him / herself and / or any principal or corporate designee , and expressly agrees to accept service of any such document by regular mail directed to the address provided by said purchaser at the time of the foreclosure auction . Trustees file number 24-002560- MD - F -1.

Balance of the purchase price , together with interest on the unpaid purchase money at the current rate contained in the Deed of Trust Note , or any modifications thereto , from the date of sale to the date funds are received by the Sub . Trustees , payable in cash within ten days of final ratification of the sale by the Circuit Court . There will be no abatement of interest due to the purchaser in the event additional funds are tendered before settlement . TIME IS OF THE ESSENCE FOR THE PURCHASER . Adjustment of all real property taxes , including agricultural taxes , if applicable , and any and all public and / or private charges or assessments , to the extent such amounts survive foreclosure sale , including water / sewer , ground rent and front foot benefit charges , to be adjusted to date of sale and thereafter assumed by purchaser . Purchaser is responsible for any recapture of homestead tax credit . All transfer taxes and recordation taxes shall be paid by Purchaser . The purchaser shall be responsible for the payment of the ground rent escrow , if required . Condominium fees and / or homeowners association dues , if any , shall be assumed by the purchaser from the date of sale . Purchaser is responsible for obtaining physical possession of the property , and assumes risk of loss or damage to the property from the date of sale . The sale is subject to post - sale audit of the status of the loan with the loan servicer including , but not limited to , determination of whether the borrower entered into any repayment agreement , reinstated or paid off the loan prior to the sale . In any such event , this sale shall be null and void , and the Purchaser ' s sole remedy , in law or equity , shall be the return of the deposit without interest . If purchaser fails to settle within ten days of ratification , subject to order of court , purchaser agrees that property will be resold and entire deposit retained by Sub . Trustees as liquidated damages for all losses occasioned by the purchaser ' s default and purchaser shall have no further liability . The purchaser waives personal service of any papers filed in connection with its failure to settle within ten days of ratification and expressly agrees to accept service by first class mail at the address provided by the Purchaser as identified on the Memorandum of Sale . The defaulted purchaser shall not be entitled to any surplus proceeds resulting from said resale even if such surplus results from improvements to the property by said defaulted purchaser . Sub . Trustees will convey either marketable or insurable title . If they cannot deliver one or the other , or if ratification of the sale is denied by the Circuit Court for any reason , the Purchaser ' s sole remedy , at law or equity , is return of the deposit without interest .( Matter No .371502-2)

The noteholder shall not be obligated to pay interest if it is the purchaser . TIME IS OF THE ESSENCE FOR THE PURCHASER . There will be no reduction of interest due to overpayment of deposit . Purchaser shall be responsible for obtaining physical possession of the property . Purchaser assumes the risk of loss or damage to the property from the date of sale forward . Additional terms to be announced at the time of sale . If the Trustees are unable to convey good and marketable title , the purchaser ' s sole remedy in law and equity shall be limited to a refund of the deposit without interest . If the purchaser fails to go to settlement , subject to order of the court , the entire deposit ( including any amount received over the advertised deposit amount ) shall be forfeited to the Trustees as liquidated damages for all losses occasioned by the purchaser ' s default and purchaser shall have no further liability . In the event of a resale , the defaulting purchaser shall not be entitled to receive the surplus , if any , even if such surplus results from improvements to the property by said defaulting purchaser and the defaulting purchaser shall be liable to the Trustees and secured party for reasonable attorney ' s fees and expenses incurred in connection with all litigation involving the Property or the proceeds of the resale . Defaulting purchaser waives personal service of any document filed in connection with such a motion on him / herself and / or any principal or corporate designee , and expressly agrees to accept service of any such document by regular mail directed to the address provided by said purchaser at the time of the foreclosure auction . The defaulted purchaser shall not be entitled to any surplus proceeds resulting from said resale even if such surplus results from improvements to the property by said defaulted purchaser . Sub . Trustees will convey either marketable or insurable title .
